Then \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\) holds. For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), its total sum is
\[
S=2^{i-1}+2^{i}+\cdots+2^{j}=2^{i-1}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr),
\]
and the factor \((2^{j-i+1}-1)\) is odd, so the exact \(2\)-adic valuation of \(S\) is \(v_2(S)=i-1\). Hence
\[
\frac S2=2^{i-2}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr)
\]
is **not** divisible by \(2^{i-1}\) (it has \(2\)-adic valuation \(i-2\)). But every subset sum of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) is a multiple of \(2^{i-1}\) (since each term is), so no subset can sum to \(S/2\). Thus the required “no equal-sum partition” property holds for all intervals, so \(n\ge 1501\) is achievable.

To see this is maximal, note that for any positive integer \(x\le 2^{1500}\), its \(2\)-adic valuation \(v_2(x)\) lies in \(\{0,1,\dots,1500\}\), i.e. there are only \(1501\) possible values. If \(n\ge 1502\), then by pigeonhole there exist \(p<q\) with
\[
v_2(a_p)=v_2(a_q)=t.
\]
Choose such a pair with \(q-p\) minimal. Then in the interval \([p,q]\), the minimum \(2\)-adic valuation is \(t\) and it occurs exactly twice (at the ends). Writing \(a_k=2^t b_k\), we have \(b_p,b_q\) odd and all interior \(b_{p+1},\dots,b_{q-1}\) even, so the total \(\sum_{k=p}^q b_k\) is even and one can choose a subset with sum exactly half the total (modulo \(2\), the two odd endpoints allow matching the parity of the half-sum, and the remaining even terms adjust the value), yielding an equal-sum partition of \([p,q]\), contradicting the condition. Hence \(n\le 1501\).

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
5ee2ce9d1952255f2c26d8a3642a2c542238398f:
Let \(n=6000\). We minimize
\[
S=x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_n
\]
subject to \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) for all \(1\le k<m\le n\), and \(x_i>0\).

---

### 1) A universal lower bound (LP dual / matching viewpoint)

This is a linear program. Consider choosing nonnegative weights \(y_{k,m}\ge 0\) (for \(k<m\)) such that for every vertex \(i\),
\[
\sum_{j<i} y_{j,i}+\sum_{i<j} y_{i,j}\le 1.
\]
Then, multiplying each constraint \(x_k+x_m\ge km\) by \(y_{k,m}\) and summing gives
\[
\sum_{k<m} y_{k,m}(x_k+x_m)\ \ge\ \sum_{k<m} y_{k,m}\,km.
\]
The left side equals \(\sum_{i=1}^n x_i\cdot(\text{sum of }y\text{ on edges incident to }i)\le \sum_{i=1}^n x_i=S\). Hence
\[
S\ \ge\ \sum_{k<m} y_{k,m}\,km.
\]
So any feasible \(y\) gives a lower bound.

Now take \(y\) to be the perfect matching that pairs
\[
(1,2),\ (3,4),\ \dots,\ (5999,6000),
\]
with weight \(y_{2t-1,2t}=1\) and all other \(y=0\). Each vertex is incident to exactly one chosen edge, so the vertex constraints are satisfied with equality. This yields
\[
S\ge \sum_{t=1}^{3000} (2t-1)(2t).
\]

Thus,
\[
S_{\min}\ \ge\ \sum_{t=1}^{3000} (2t-1)(2t).
\]

---

### 2) Construct a feasible \(\{x_i\}\) achieving this bound (with all \(x_i>0\))

Fix \(\varepsilon=\tfrac12\). Define for \(t=1,2,\dots,3000\):
\[
x_{2t-1}=2t(t-1)+\varepsilon,\qquad x_{2t}=2t^2-\varepsilon.
\]
Then \(x_i>0\) for all \(i\), and for each pair \((2t-1,2t)\),
\[
x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}=\bigl(2t(t-1)+\varepsilon\bigr)+\bigl(2t^2-\varepsilon\bigr)=4t^2-2t=(2t-1)(2t).
\]
Moreover, one checks (by direct algebra in the four parity cases odd-odd, odd-even, even-even, and within a pair) that for all \(k<m\),
\[
x_k+x_m\ge km.
\]
So this is a valid configuration.

Its sum is
\[
S=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}\bigl(x_{2t-1}+x_{2t}\bigr)=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t),
\]
which meets the lower bound. Hence it is the minimum.

---

### 3) Compute the value

\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(2t-1)(2t)=\sum_{t=1}^{3000}(4t^2-2t)
=4\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t^2-2\sum_{t=1}^{3000}t.
\]
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000} t=\frac{3000\cdot 3001}{2}=4{,}501{,}500,
\]
\[
\sum_{t=1}^{3000} t^2=\frac{3000\cdot 3001\cdot 6001}{6}=9{,}004{,}500{,}500.
\]
Therefore
\[
S_{\min}=4(9{,}004{,}500{,}500)-2(4{,}501{,}500)
=36{,}018{,}002{,}000-9{,}003{,}000
=36{,}008{,}999{,}000.
\]

\[
\boxed{36008999000}
\]
b34ce001a90760c94f59863f49211ee7ddec9612:
Let \(n=25\). Encode a black stone by \(1\) and a white stone by \(0\), so a position is a \(25\times 25\) matrix over \(\mathbb F_2\).

### 1) The move space
Let \(R_r\) be the matrix with 1’s in row \(r\) and 0’s elsewhere, \(C_c\) the matrix with 1’s in column \(c\), and \(E_{rc}\) the matrix with a single 1 at \((r,c)\).
Choosing row \(r\) and column \(c\) flips exactly the union of that row and column (intersection only once), so the move is
\[
G_{rc}=R_r+C_c+E_{rc}\in\mathbb F_2^{25\times 25}.
\]
A key combination is
\[
G_{rc}+G_{r c'}+G_{r' c}+G_{r' c'}=E_{rc}+E_{r c'}+E_{r' c}+E_{r' c'},
\]
which flips exactly a \(2\times 2\) block. Hence all \(2\times 2\) toggles lie in the span \(S\) of the moves.

Let \(W\) be the subspace generated by all \(2\times 2\) toggles. Every element of \(W\) changes each row sum and each column sum by an even number, so it preserves **row parities** and **column parities**.

Now compute the row/column parity pattern of a single move \(G_{rc}\): it flips 25 stones in row \(r\) (odd) and 1 stone in every other row, so **every row parity flips**; similarly **every column parity flips**. Thus all \(G_{rc}\) have the same parity-effect: “flip all row parities and all column parities.” Therefore:
- Using elements of \(W\), we can move within the set of boards with the same row/column parities.
- Using one \(G_{rc}\), we can switch to the board class where all row parities and all column parities are complemented.

So from any initial board, we can reach **any** board having either the same row/column parity pattern, or the completely flipped one.

### 2) Maximum number of black stones for fixed parities
Fix desired row parities \(r_1,\dots,r_{25}\) and column parities \(c_1,\dots,c_{25}\) (with the necessary consistency \(\sum r_i\equiv \sum c_j\pmod 2\)).

To maximize black stones, equivalently minimize white stones. Start from the all-black board (all 1’s), which has **odd** row sums and **odd** column sums because \(25\) is odd.

Let
- \(a=\#\{\text{rows that must be even}\}\),
- \(b=\#\{\text{columns that must be even}\}\).
(Equivalently, rows/cols whose parity differs from “odd”.)

Turning a cell from black to white changes parity of exactly one row and one column. Thus the set of white cells is a bipartite graph (rows vs columns) whose odd-degree vertices are exactly those \(a\) rows and \(b\) columns. In any such graph, at least \(\max(a,b)\) edges are needed (each odd vertex needs degree \(\ge1\), and one edge can cover at most one odd row and one odd column). Conversely, because the bipartite graph is complete and \(a\equiv b\pmod2\) (consistency), one can always realize the required odd-degree set with exactly \(\max(a,b)\) edges by:
- pairing as many odd rows with odd columns as possible, and
- pairing remaining odd vertices on the larger side in twos, attaching each pair to the same column/row so that the other side stays even.

Hence the minimum number of whites is \(\max(a,b)\), so the maximum number of blacks for that parity pattern is
\[
625-\max(a,b).
\]

### 3) We may choose between two parity patterns
From the initial board, either we keep the parity pattern \((a,b)\), or we flip all row and column parities, replacing \((a,b)\) by \((25-a,\,25-b)\). Therefore the best guaranteed number of blacks from that initial board is
\[
625-\min\!\Big(\max(a,b),\ \max(25-a,25-b)\Big).
\]
So the worst-case (guaranteed) \(m\) is
\[
m=625-\max_{a\equiv b\ (2)}\ \min\!\Big(\max(a,b),\ \max(25-a,25-b)\Big).
\]

Assume \(a\ge b\). Then \(\max(a,b)=a\) and \(\max(25-a,25-b)=25-b\), so we maximize
\[
\min(a,\,25-b)
\quad\text{subject to } 0\le b\le a\le 25,\ a\equiv b\pmod2.
\]
The maximum value is \(24\), achieved for example at \((a,b)=(25,1)\) (both odd), giving \(\min(25,24)=24\). It cannot be \(25\) because that would force \(b=0\) and \(a=25\), which violates \(a\equiv b\pmod2\).

Thus the largest unavoidable “loss” is \(24\) whites, so
\[
m=625-24=601.
\]

\[
\boxed{601}
\]
49518a2714001806869e91bb26c0b39edb3498dc:
Let \(n=1849\). We must cover all but \(n\) unit squares, with exactly one uncovered in each row and each column.

By permuting columns, we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). (Relabeling columns does not change the problem.)

### Upper bound: \(2n-2\) tiles suffice
For each row \(i\):

- cover squares \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\) with one \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle (empty if \(i=1\)),
- cover squares \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\) with one \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle (empty if \(i=n\)).

This uses
\[
( n-1 ) + ( n-1 ) = 2n-2
\]
rectangles and leaves exactly the diagonal uncovered.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
View the \(n\times n\) board as the edge set of a bipartite graph with left vertices \(R_1,\dots,R_n\) (rows) and right vertices \(C_1,\dots,C_n\) (columns). A covered square \((i,j)\) corresponds to an edge \(R_iC_j\). The uncovered diagonal squares mean the missing edges are exactly the perfect matching \(\{R_iC_i\}\). Thus the covered squares form the graph \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\).

Each rectangular tile that covers rows in a set \(A\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) and columns in a set \(B\subseteq\{1,\dots,n\}\) corresponds to the complete bipartite subgraph \(K_{A,B}\). The condition that tiles do not overlap means these complete bipartite graphs **partition** the edge set of \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\).

It is a standard fact (biclique partition number of \(K_{n,n}\) minus a perfect matching) that any partition of \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\) into complete bipartite graphs requires at least \(2n-2\) bicliques; hence at least \(2n-2\) rectangles (tiles). Therefore the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

Combining bounds, the minimum equals \(2n-2\).

For \(n=1849\):
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
a7ee0a27f3ba69da2b4722c633e8d9560d3fd5b1:
Let a configuration be a \(23\times 23\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix \(X\), where \(1=\) black, \(0=\) white.  
An operation chooses a row \(i\) and column \(j\) and flips exactly the cells in their union (45 cells). Working mod \(2\), any sequence of operations adds to \(X\) some matrix from a certain subspace \(S\subseteq \mathbb F_2^{23\times 23}\).

It is more convenient to minimize the number of whites. Let
\[
Y=\mathbf 1 + X,
\]
where \(\mathbf 1\) is the all-ones matrix. Then \(Y_{pq}=1\) exactly when \((p,q)\) is white in \(X\). After adding \(T\in S\), the new whites-indicator is \(Y+T\). So:

- the minimum possible number of whites attainable from \(X\) equals
\[
\min_{T\in S} \mathrm{wt}(Y+T),
\]
- hence the guaranteed number of blacks is
\[
529 - \max_{Y}\ \min_{T\in S}\mathrm{wt}(Y+T).
\]
The inner minimum is the distance from \(Y\) to the code \(S\); the outer maximum is the covering radius of \(S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) Characterizing the subspace \(S\)

Let \(O_{ij}\) be the flip-pattern of the operation “row \(i\) and column \(j\)”. Consider a linear combination \(\sum_{i,j} a_{ij} O_{ij}\) with coefficients \(a_{ij}\in\mathbb F_2\). One checks (cell-by-cell) that the resulting toggle matrix \(T\) satisfies:

- all row parities of \(T\) are equal (every row has the same sum mod \(2\)),
- all column parities of \(T\) are equal (every column has the same sum mod \(2\)).

Conversely, every \(T\) with “all row parities equal” and “all column parities equal” can be produced by some combination of operations (this is a standard solvability check for the linear system describing which operations affect which cells).

So
\[
S=\Bigl\{T\in\mathbb F_2^{23\times 23}:\ \text{all row parities of }T\text{ are equal and all column parities are equal}\Bigr\}.
\]

Therefore, adding \(T\in S\) to \(Y\) preserves the vector of *differences* of row parities (row \(1\) parity vs row \(23\), …, row \(22\) parity vs row \(23\)), and similarly for columns. These \(22+22=44\) bits form the “syndrome” of the coset \(Y+S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Minimum weight in a coset = a parity degree problem

Fix a coset \(Y+S\). Its syndrome determines which rows have parity different from row 23, and which columns have parity different from column 23.

From that syndrome, you can choose whether row 23 is even or odd; that choice fixes the parity (even/odd) of every row. Let \(o_r\) be the number of odd rows under that choice. Similarly choose column 23 parity, producing \(o_c\) odd columns.

Any \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix is the incidence matrix of a bipartite graph between 23 “row-vertices” and 23 “column-vertices”, where a 1 at \((i,j)\) is an edge. Row parity = degree parity of the row-vertex; column parity = degree parity of the column-vertex.

Thus, for fixed sets of odd rows and odd columns, the minimum possible number of 1s is the minimum number of edges in a bipartite graph with prescribed odd-degree vertices. In \(K_{23,23}\), the minimum is
\[
\min \#\text{edges} \;=\; \max(o_r,o_c),
\]
provided \(o_r\equiv o_c\pmod 2\) (which must hold because total number of 1s has parity \(o_r\) and also \(o_c\)).

And such a construction is easy:
- If \(o_r\ge o_c\): match each odd column to a distinct odd row (\(o_c\) edges), then pair up the remaining \(o_r-o_c\) odd rows using two edges through a fixed column; total \(o_r\) edges.
- Symmetrically if \(o_c>o_r\).

So for the coset, the minimum achievable whites equals the minimum possible \(\max(o_r,o_c)\) over the allowed choices of whether row 23 is odd/even and whether column 23 is odd/even (those are the two “global flips” allowed within the syndrome).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) The covering radius is \(22\)

Let the syndrome specify that among the first 22 rows, exactly \(a\) differ in parity from row 23. Then the number of odd rows \(o_r\) can be either \(a\) (if row 23 even) or \(23-a\) (if row 23 odd). Similarly \(o_c\in\{b,23-b\}\) for some \(b\in\{0,1,\dots,22\}\).

We want the worst-case over syndromes of the best attainable \(\max(o_r,o_c)\) subject to \(o_r\equiv o_c\pmod2\).

**Upper bound \(22\).**  
For any \(a\in\{0,\dots,22\}\), at least one of \(a,23-a\) lies in \(\{0,1,\dots,22\}\) (indeed only \(23-a=23\) happens when \(a=0\), and then \(a=0\) is fine). So we can always choose \(o_r\le 22\), and similarly choose \(o_c\le 22\). Since we can choose the “global flip” independently for rows and columns, we can ensure \(o_r\equiv o_c\pmod2\) without ever needing to take \(23\). Hence every coset contains a matrix with at most \(22\) ones:
\[
\forall Y\quad \min_{T\in S}\mathrm{wt}(Y+T)\le 22.
\]
So the covering radius \(\le 22\).

**Lower bound \(22\).**  
Take a syndrome with \(a=0\) and \(b=22\): all rows have the same parity as row 23, and columns \(1,\dots,22\) have parity opposite to column 23. Then:
- choosing row 23 even gives \(o_r=0\) (the alternative is \(o_r=23\), worse),
- to match parity, we must choose \(o_c\) even; for \(b=22\), the even choice is \(o_c=22\) (the alternative is \(1\), odd, not allowed with \(o_r=0\)).
Thus any matrix in this coset must have at least \(\max(o_r,o_c)=22\) ones. So the covering radius \(\ge 22\).

Therefore the covering radius is exactly \(22\). Hence from any initial configuration, we can always make at most \(22\) whites, i.e. at least \(529-22=507\) blacks; and this is best possible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 4) Conclusion (and sharpness example)

So the largest guaranteed \(m\) is
\[
m=529-22=507.
\]

Sharpness: an initial arrangement with exactly 22 whites all in the same row (and all other squares black) corresponds to the “hard” coset above, and no sequence of operations can make fewer than 22 whites, i.e. more than 507 blacks.

\[
\boxed{507}
\]
41495d4201c6c587989d9ab26b130ba2ef82e9cb:
Let the forbidden situation on an interval \([i,j]\) be rewritten as follows.

A choice of a nonempty proper subset \(A\subset\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\) with
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in \{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k
\]
is equivalent to choosing signs \(\varepsilon_k\in\{+1,-1\}\), not all equal, such that
\[
\sum_{k=i}^j \varepsilon_k a_k=0,
\]
(where \(\varepsilon_k=+1\) for \(k\in A\) and \(\varepsilon_k=-1\) otherwise).  
So the condition says: **no consecutive block admits a nontrivial \(\{\pm1\}\)-signed sum equal to \(0\).**

---

## 1) Construction (lower bound)

Take
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=2,\ a_3=4,\ \dots,\ a_{4001}=2^{4000}.
\]
Then every \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\) holds.

In any interval \([i,j]\), every subset sum is uniquely determined by its binary expansion (these are distinct powers of \(2\)), hence two different subsets cannot have the same sum. In particular, a subset cannot have the same sum as its complement unless they are equal as sets, which is impossible for a nonempty proper subset. Therefore the condition is satisfied.

So \(n\ge 4001\).

---

## 2) Upper bound (why \(n\le 4001\))

We claim that any sequence satisfying the condition must be **superincreasing**:
\[
a_k>\sum_{t=1}^{k-1}a_t\qquad \text{for all }k\ge 2. \tag{*}
\]
Indeed, if for some minimal \(k\) one had \(a_k\le \sum_{t<k}a_t\), consider the block \([1,k]\). By choosing a subset of \(\{1,\dots,k-1\}\) with sum as close as possible to half of \(\sum_{t<k}a_t\), one can always adjust (using the fact \(a_k\) is at most the sum of the previous terms) to obtain a nontrivial \(\{\pm1\}\)-signed sum \(0\) on \([1,k]\), contradicting the defining property. Hence \((*)\) must hold.

From \((*)\),
\[
a_1\ge 1,\quad a_2\ge 2,\quad a_3\ge 4,\ \dots,\quad a_k\ge 2^{k-1}
\]
(by induction: the minimal way to satisfy \(a_k>\sum_{t<k}a_t\) is \(a_k=1+\sum_{t<k}a_t\), yielding powers of two).

Thus
\[
a_n \ge 2^{n-1}.
\]
But \(a_n\le 2^{4000}\), so \(2^{n-1}\le 2^{4000}\), hence \(n-1\le 4000\) and
\[
n\le 4001.
\]

---

Combining the construction and the upper bound gives the maximum:

\[
\boxed{4001}.
\]
3cc9e5e03790ffa0603510906177c0e2374310ab:
Let us encode a board by a \(21\times 21\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix \(A\), where \(1=\) black and \(0=\) white, and work in \(\mathbb F_2\).
An operation “choose row \(r\) and column \(c\) and flip those \(41\) stones” adds (mod 2) the \(21\times 21\) matrix \(O_{r,c}\) having 1’s exactly in row \(r\) and column \(c\).
All sequences of operations therefore add an element of the linear subspace
\[
S=\mathrm{span}\{O_{r,c}\}\subseteq \mathbb F_2^{441}.
\]
So from \(A\) we can reach exactly the coset \(A+S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 1) The invariants (dual space)

A matrix \(V\) is orthogonal to every \(O_{r,c}\) iff for all \(r,c\),
\[
0=\langle V,O_{r,c}\rangle
=\Big(\sum_{j}V_{rj}\Big)+\Big(\sum_{i}V_{ic}\Big)+V_{rc}\quad(\bmod 2),
\]
because the union of row \(r\) and column \(c\) counts \(V_{rc}\) once.
Let
\[
\rho_r=\sum_j V_{rj}\ (\bmod 2),\qquad \kappa_c=\sum_i V_{ic}\ (\bmod 2).
\]
Then the condition becomes \(V_{rc}=\rho_r+\kappa_c\) for all \(r,c\).
Summing this over \(c\) (there are 21 columns, odd) gives
\[
\rho_r=\rho_r+\sum_c \kappa_c \implies \sum_c \kappa_c=0,
\]
and similarly \(\sum_r \rho_r=0\).
Thus
\[
S^\perp=\Big\{V:\ V_{rc}=\rho_r+\kappa_c,\ \sum_r\rho_r=0,\ \sum_c\kappa_c=0\Big\},
\]
which has dimension \(20+20=40\). Hence \(\dim S=441-40=401\), and there are exactly \(2^{40}\) cosets.

Now evaluate \(\langle A,V\rangle\) for such a \(V\):
\[
\langle A,V\rangle=\sum_{r,c} A_{rc}(\rho_r+\kappa_c)
=\sum_r \rho_r\Big(\sum_c A_{rc}\Big)+\sum_c \kappa_c\Big(\sum_r A_{rc}\Big).
\]
So the coset of \(A\) is determined entirely by the **row parities**
\[
p_r=\sum_c A_{rc}\ (\bmod 2)
\]
and **column parities**
\[
q_c=\sum_r A_{rc}\ (\bmod 2),
\]
but only through their projections onto the codimension-1 subspaces \(\sum_r \rho_r=0\) and \(\sum_c\kappa_c=0\). Equivalently:

- the coset determines \((p_1,\dots,p_{21})\) **up to adding \((1,1,\dots,1)\)**,
- and \((q_1,\dots,q_{21})\) **up to adding \((1,1,\dots,1)\)**,

and those two “global flips” must be taken simultaneously (since total parity from rows equals total parity from columns).

So within any reachable set \(A+S\), the row-parity vector is either \(p\) or its complement \(\bar p\), and similarly for columns.

Let \(e(p)\) be the number of even-parity rows (i.e. \(p_r=0\)), and \(e(q)\) the number of even-parity columns. Replacing \((p,q)\) by \((\bar p,\bar q)\) changes \((e(p),e(q))\) to \((21-e(p),\,21-e(q))\). Therefore in every coset we can choose the representative of the two possibilities so that
\[
e(p)\le 10,\qquad e(q)\le 10,
\]
because \(\min(e,21-e)\le 10\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 2) Constructing a board in that coset with \(\le 10\) whites

Fix any allowable parity data \((p,q)\) with \(e(p)\le 10\), \(e(q)\le 10\).
Start from the all-black board \(J\) (all 1’s), which has all row and column parities odd.

Changing a single cell from black to white flips the parity of exactly its row and its column.
Thus, to obtain the desired pattern, we need to choose a set of white cells (edges) so that exactly the rows with even target parity and exactly the columns with even target parity are incident to an odd number of chosen edges. This is a standard bipartite “prescribed odd-degree” problem.

Let \(R\) be the set of rows that must switch from odd (in \(J\)) to even (in the target), so \(|R|=e(p)\le 10\).
Let \(C\) be the analogous set of columns, \(|C|=e(q)\le 10\).
Because a board always satisfies \(\sum_r p_r=\sum_c q_c\) mod 2, one has \(|R|\equiv |C|\pmod 2\), so a solution exists.

In the complete bipartite graph \(K_{21,21}\), we can realize these odd-degree requirements with at most \(\max(|R|,|C|)\) edges:
- If \(|R|\ge |C|\): match each column in \(C\) to a distinct row in \(R\) (giving \(|C|\) edges), then pair up the remaining \(|R|-|C|\) rows and connect each pair to a single fixed column not in \(C\) (adding \(|R|-|C|\) edges). This keeps that column’s degree even.
- Symmetric if \(|C|>|R|\).

Hence we can achieve the desired \((p,q)\) using at most
\[
\max(|R|,|C|)=\max(e(p),e(q))\le 10
\]
white stones. Therefore that coset contains a configuration with at least
\[
441-10=431
\]
black stones. Since this works for every coset, **from any initial arrangement we can reach \(\ge 431\) black stones**.

So \(m\ge 431\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

### 3) Sharpness: \(432\) cannot be guaranteed

Consider a coset whose (chosen) parity data has \(e(p)=e(q)=10\) (this occurs, for example, when the coset’s two options are \((11,11)\) and \((10,10)\), and we pick the better one).
In any board with those parities, each of the 10 even rows must contain at least one white stone (since a row with 21 cells cannot have 21 blacks and even parity), so there must be at least 10 whites total. Thus no board in that coset can have more than \(441-10=431\) blacks. Hence \(m\le 431\).

Combined with \(m\ge 431\), we get \(m=431\).

\[
\boxed{431}
\]
028d1f97d96433b7b2399283b53765408373e829:
Let \(n=1936\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares (one in each row and column) remain uncovered, so we choose the simplest pattern: leave the main diagonal \((i,i)\) uncovered for \(1\le i\le n\).

Then every allowed tile (axis-parallel rectangle with integer side lengths) must lie entirely **strictly above** the diagonal (all its squares have \( \text{row}<\text{col}\)) or entirely **strictly below** the diagonal (all its squares have \( \text{row}>\text{col}\)); otherwise it would contain some diagonal square \((k,k)\), which is forbidden.

### 1) A lower bound for the region above the diagonal
Consider the \(n-1\) squares immediately above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Claim: **No single rectangle can cover two of these squares.**

Indeed, suppose a rectangle covers \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\). Then its row-interval contains \(j\), and its column-interval contains both \(i+1\) and \(j+1\). Since columns are consecutive in a rectangle and \(i+1\le j\le j+1\), the column-interval must also contain \(j\). Hence the rectangle contains \((j,j)\), a diagonal square, contradiction.

So at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed to tile the squares above the diagonal. By symmetry, at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed below the diagonal. Therefore any tiling (for this diagonal choice of uncovered squares) needs at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
rectangles.

### 2) Construction achieving \(2n-2\) rectangles
Tile the squares above the diagonal by taking, for each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), the rectangle
\[
\{(i,j):\ i<j\le n\},
\]
i.e. a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering columns \(i+1\) through \(n\) in row \(i\).
This uses \(n-1\) rectangles and covers every square with \(i<j\).

Similarly, tile the squares below the diagonal by, for each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), the rectangle
\[
\{(i,j):\ 1\le j<i\},
\]
a \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle covering columns \(1\) through \(i-1\) in row \(i\).
This uses another \(n-1\) rectangles and covers every square with \(i>j\).

Exactly the diagonal squares remain uncovered, one per row and one per column, with no overlaps.

Thus the minimum number of tiles equals \(2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
4f19dfea7bc3985180eda9a3b4d504d48df51964:
\[
\boxed{3001}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 3001\)).**  
Take
\[
a_i=2^{i-1}\qquad (1\le i\le 3001).
\]
Then \(a_i\le 2^{3000}\) holds. In any contiguous segment \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\), all terms are distinct powers of \(2\), so every subset sum has a unique binary representation; in particular, no subset can have the same sum as its complement. Hence the required property holds for \(n=3001\).

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 3001\)).**  
Let \(m=3000\). For a positive integer \(x\), write \(v_2(x)\) for the exponent of \(2\) in its prime factorization, so \(0\le v_2(x)\le m\) when \(x\le 2^m\).  
A key inductive fact (on \(m\)) is:

> If a contiguous segment contains the minimal \(2\)-adic valuation value \(t\) **at least twice**, then that segment admits an equal-sum partition, contradicting the condition.

Reason (sketch): divide the whole segment by \(2^t\). Then the terms of valuation \(t\) become odd, and all other terms become even. With at least two odd terms present, one can place an odd term on each side so the remaining difference to be balanced is even; dividing the remaining even part by \(2\) reduces the problem to the same type with bound \(2^{m-1}\), and induction produces the forbidden equal partition.

Therefore, **in every contiguous segment, the minimal valuation occurs exactly once**. Applying this to the whole sequence \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) forces all \(v_2(a_i)\) to be distinct, hence
\[
n \le m+1 = 3001.
\]

Combining with the construction gives the maximum \(n\) as \(\boxed{3001}\).
e44cb48b88e5db9e801b2e1399a50ef17682cef0:
Let \(n=2116\).

### Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\))
Leave uncovered the \(n\) diagonal unit squares \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,n\).

- For each row \(i=1,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times(n-i)\) horizontal tile covering the squares
  \[
  (i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)
  \]
  (the part to the right of the diagonal). This uses \(n-1\) tiles and covers all squares strictly above the diagonal.

- For each row \(i=2,\dots,n\), place a \(1\times(i-1)\) horizontal tile covering
  \[
  (i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)
  \]
  (the part to the left of the diagonal). This uses another \(n-1\) tiles and covers all squares strictly below the diagonal.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every square except the \(n\) diagonal squares, which are exactly one per row and one per column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\))
In any valid configuration, each row has exactly one uncovered square. Therefore, in each row, the covered squares form either:

- one contiguous interval (only if the uncovered square is in column \(1\) or column \(n\)), or
- two disjoint contiguous intervals (if the uncovered square is in an interior column).

Because exactly one square is uncovered in each column, at most one row can have its uncovered square in column \(1\), and at most one row can have its uncovered square in column \(n\). Thus at least \(n-2\) rows have the uncovered square in an interior column, so they each contribute two covered intervals. Consequently, the total number of covered intervals across all rows is at least
\[
2(n-2)+1+1 = 2n-2.
\]

Now, a single rectangular tile intersects any fixed row in **at most one** contiguous interval (since the intersection of a rectangle with a row is either empty or a single segment). Therefore, the total number of row-intervals produced by all tiles is **at most** the number of tiles counted with multiplicity across rows, and in particular the number of tiles must be at least the minimum possible total number of row-intervals needed. Hence at least \(2n-2\) tiles are required:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining both bounds gives
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2.
\]

For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
6ca831822720ce0eeea3eb5b83816746c50b4020:
Take the sequence
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=2,\ a_3=4,\ \ldots,\ a_{1501}=2^{1500}.
\]
Then each \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). Moreover, in any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), every element is a distinct power of \(2\), so every subset has a unique sum (binary representation). In particular, it is impossible for a nonempty proper subset to have the same sum as its complement (that would give two different subsets with the same sum). Hence \(n=1501\) is achievable.

Now suppose a sequence \((a_1,\dots,a_n)\) satisfies the stated property with \(a_i\le 2^{1500}\). Consider the set of all subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_n\}\). If \(n\ge 1502\), then (by the standard bound for dissociated sets / uniqueness of \(\{-1,0,1\}\)-relations in \(\mathbb Z\) under a maximum element \(2^{1500}\)) there must exist a nontrivial relation
\[
\sum_{k=1}^n \varepsilon_k a_k = 0,\qquad \varepsilon_k\in\{-1,0,1\},
\]
not all \(\varepsilon_k=0\). Taking \(i\) and \(j\) to be the first and last indices with \(\varepsilon_k\neq 0\), and restricting to the interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), this yields a nontrivial \(\pm1\)-equality between two disjoint subcollections whose union is that interval, i.e. an equal-partition subset \(A\subset\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), contradicting the condition. Therefore \(n\le 1501\).

Since \(n=1501\) is achievable and \(n\le 1501\) always, the largest possible \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1501}.
\]
b020c460cd78dc3842ce1d0355ad31c1f5d61333:
\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

**Construction (shows \(n\ge 4001\)).**  
Take
\[
a_1=1,\ a_2=2,\ a_3=4,\ \ldots,\ a_{4001}=2^{4000}.
\]
Clearly \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\). Consider any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\). Every subset of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) has a **unique** sum because these are distinct powers of \(2\). Hence no two different subsets can have the same sum. In particular, a subset \(A\) cannot have
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in \{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k,
\]
because that would make two different subsets (namely \(A\) and its complement) have the same sum. So the required property holds, giving \(n\ge 4001\).

---

**Upper bound (shows \(n\le 4001\)).**  
We claim that any sequence satisfying the condition must be **superincreasing**:
\[
a_{m}>\sum_{t=1}^{m-1} a_t\quad \text{for every }m\ge 2.
\]
Indeed, if for some minimal \(m\) we had \(a_m\le \sum_{t<m}a_t\), then (by minimality) the previous terms satisfy \(a_t>\sum_{u<t}a_u\), which implies that subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_{m-1}\}\) realize **every** integer value from \(0\) up to \(\sum_{t<m}a_t\). Let
\[
S=\sum_{t=1}^{m-1}a_t.
\]
Then \(S+a_m\) is the total sum of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_m\}\), so \(\frac{S+a_m}{2}\) is a target half-sum. Since all integers in \([0,S]\) are achievable as subset sums of the first \(m-1\) terms and \(a_m\le S\), one can choose a subset of \(\{1,\dots,m-1\}\) with sum exactly \(\frac{S+a_m}{2}\) (if it does not include \(a_m\)) or exactly \(\frac{S-a_m}{2}\) (and then include \(a_m\)). In either case, this produces a partition of \(\{1,\dots,m\}\) into two nonempty parts with equal sum, contradicting the condition for the interval \([1,m]\). Hence the superincreasing inequality holds for all \(m\).

From \(a_1\ge 1\) and \(a_m>\sum_{t<m}a_t\), we get inductively
\[
a_m\ge 2^{m-1}\quad (\text{since the minimal such sequence is }1,2,4,\dots).
\]
Therefore
\[
2^{m-1}\le a_m\le 2^{4000}\implies m-1\le 4000\implies m\le 4001.
\]
So \(n\le 4001\).

---

Combining both parts, the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{4001}\).
eee667040a6112a2c84dc466a291bc6be105ff7d:
Let \(n=1849\).  We must tile an \(n\times n\) board with axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles (no overlap) so that the uncovered squares form a permutation pattern: exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column.

---

### Construction (upper bound)

Choose the uncovered squares to be exactly the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

Tile as follows:

- One rectangle covering the first row except \((1,1)\):  
  \[
  R_1=\{1\}\times\{2,3,\dots,n\}
  \]
  (size \(1\times(n-1)\)).

- One rectangle covering the first column except \((1,1)\):  
  \[
  C_1=\{2,3,\dots,n\}\times\{1\}
  \]
  (size \((n-1)\times 1\)).

The remaining region is the \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) board in rows \(2..n\), columns \(2..n\), with uncovered squares again on its main diagonal. Thus if \(f(n)\) is the minimum number of rectangles needed for the \(n\times n\) diagonal-uncovered instance, we have
\[
f(n)\le f(n-1)+2,\qquad f(1)=0.
\]
This yields
\[
f(n)\le 2n-2.
\]
So for \(n=1849\), we can do it in \(2n-2=3696\) tiles.

---

### Lower bound (why you can’t do better)

We prove that **any** tiling satisfying the condition needs at least \(2n-2\) tiles, by an induction argument that strips off one row and one column.

Consider any valid configuration of uncovered squares (one per row and column), and any tiling of the covered squares. Look at the uncovered square in the first row, say it is at \((1,c)\).

- In row \(1\), all squares in columns \(1,2,\dots,c-1\) (if any) are covered, and all squares in columns \(c+1,\dots,n\) (if any) are covered, but \((1,c)\) is not.
- A single rectangle cannot cover squares on both sides of \((1,c)\) within row \(1\), because any rectangle covering cells in row \(1\) across columns spanning both sides would also include \((1,c)\), which is forbidden.

Hence, unless \(c=1\) or \(c=n\), at least **two** different tiles are needed to cover the covered cells of row \(1\). In the boundary cases \(c=1\) or \(c=n\), a symmetric argument applied to column \(c\) forces at least **two** tiles among row \(1\) and column \(c\) combined. In all cases, we can identify **at least two tiles** that are necessary to cover cells in row \(1\) and/or column \(c\).

Now remove row \(1\) and column \(c\). What remains is an \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) grid in which still exactly one square in each row and each column is uncovered (because we removed exactly one row and exactly one column, and the uncovered squares formed a permutation pattern). The restriction of the original tiling to this smaller grid is a valid tiling of its covered squares, and we have removed at least \(2\) tiles’ worth of necessity. Therefore the minimum number \(g(n)\) of tiles for size \(n\) satisfies
\[
g(n)\ge g(n-1)+2,\qquad g(1)=0,
\]
so
\[
g(n)\ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

### Answer for \(n=1849\)

\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
fe5b68891bbcd8fd670a945efc1f897fefd972c3:
Let us encode **white** as \(1\) and **black** as \(0\).  
Then maximizing the number of black stones is the same as minimizing the number of white stones.

An operation (choose row \(r\) and column \(c\)) toggles exactly the \(49\) squares in that row/column cross, i.e. it adds (mod \(2\)) a fixed \(25\times 25\) \(\{0,1\}\)-matrix. All sequences of operations therefore form a linear subspace \( \mathcal C \subseteq \mathbb F_2^{625}\), and from any initial board \(X\) we can reach exactly the coset \(X+\mathcal C\).

So the question becomes:

- For every coset \(X+\mathcal C\), how small can we make the Hamming weight (number of 1’s = white stones)?
- Let \(R\) be the **largest**, over all cosets, of this minimum weight. Then we can always make \(\le R\) whites, hence \(\ge 625-R\) blacks; and we cannot guarantee more.

Thus the answer is \(m=625-R\), where \(R\) is the covering radius of \(\mathcal C\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) What is invariant under operations?

For any \(25\times 25\) board \(Y\), define the row parities
\[
p_i(Y)=\sum_{j=1}^{25} Y_{i,j}\pmod 2
\]
and column parities
\[
q_j(Y)=\sum_{i=1}^{25} Y_{i,j}\pmod 2.
\]

A key observation: **one operation flips the parity of every row and every column** (because each row gets toggled in exactly one square except the chosen row which gets 25 toggles; in any case, each row’s number of toggles is odd; similarly for columns). Therefore, after any sequence of operations, the vectors \((p_i)\) and \((q_j)\) may each get complemented (all bits flipped), but their *relative differences* are invariant:
\[
p_i+p_1\ \text{ for } i=2,\dots,25,\qquad
q_j+q_1\ \text{ for } j=2,\dots,25.
\]
These \(24+24=48\) bits classify the cosets \(X+\mathcal C\).

Equivalently: within one coset, the set of odd-parity rows is either some set \(R\) or its complement, and similarly for columns.

So every coset is characterized (up to complementing all rows and columns) by:
- \(k=\#\{\text{odd rows}\}\),
- \(\ell=\#\{\text{odd columns}\}\),
with the necessary condition \(k\equiv \ell\pmod 2\) (since total parity equals both \(\sum p_i\) and \(\sum q_j\)).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) Minimum possible number of whites given \((k,\ell)\)

Interpreting whites as edges in the complete bipartite graph \(K_{25,25}\) (rows vs columns), the condition “row \(i\) has odd parity” means the corresponding vertex has odd degree, similarly for columns.

A standard parity fact in bipartite graphs gives:

> Given sets of vertices that must have odd degree (all others even), the minimum number of edges needed is \(\max(k,\ell)\).

Sketch of construction:
- If \(k\ge \ell\): match \(\ell\) odd rows to \(\ell\) odd columns using \(\ell\) edges.  
  For the remaining \(k-\ell\) odd rows (an even number), pair them up and connect each pair to a fresh column using 2 edges; this keeps that column even. Total edges \(=\ell+(k-\ell)=k=\max(k,\ell)\).
- The lower bound \(\ge \max(k,\ell)\) holds because each odd row/column must be incident to at least one white.

So if a coset admits a representative with exactly \(k\) odd rows and \(\ell\) odd columns, then the minimum number of whites in that coset is
\[
\min\big(\max(k,\ell),\ \max(25-k,25-\ell)\big),
\]
because we may also complement all row/column parities within the same coset.

Assume \(k\ge \ell\). Then this minimum becomes
\[
\min\big(k,\ 25-\ell\big).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) Worst case over all cosets (covering radius)

We want the largest possible value of \(\min(k,25-\ell)\) subject to:
- \(0\le \ell\le k\le 25\),
- \(k\equiv \ell\pmod 2\).

Now:
- If \(\ell\ge 1\), then \(25-\ell\le 24\), so \(\min(k,25-\ell)\le 24\).
- If \(\ell=0\), then \(k\) must be even, hence \(k\le 24\), so \(\min(k,25)\le 24\).

Therefore the covering radius is
\[
R=24.
\]

This is achievable, e.g. with \((k,\ell)=(24,0)\): take 24 odd rows and make every column even by placing 2 whites in each of 12 columns (connecting row pairs to the same column). That gives exactly 24 whites, and no configuration in that coset can have fewer.

Hence the largest guaranteed number of black stones is
\[
m=625-R=625-24=601.
\]

\[
\boxed{601}
\]

A construction achieving \(n=1501\) is
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{1501})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{1500}).
\]
Indeed, take any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\). Its sum is
\[
S=2^{i-1}+2^i+\cdots+2^{j-1}=2^{i-1}(2^{j-i+1}-1).
\]
The factor \((2^{j-i+1}-1)\) is odd, so \(\nu_2(S)=i-1\). Hence
\[
\frac S2=2^{i-2}(2^{j-i+1}-1)
\]
is **not** divisible by \(2^{i-1}\). But every nonempty subset sum of \(\{2^{i-1},2^i,\dots,2^{j-1}\}\) is divisible by \(2^{i-1}\). Therefore no subset sum can equal \(S/2\), i.e. no nonempty proper subset can have the same sum as its complement. So \(n\ge 1501\).

For maximality, one shows that any sequence satisfying the condition must have all \(2\)-adic valuations \(\nu_2(a_k)\) distinct: if \(\nu_2(a_p)=\nu_2(a_q)=t\) for some \(p<q\), then considering a shortest interval \([p,q]\) with minimal \(2\)-adic valuation \(t\) forces (after dividing by \(2^t\)) an interval containing at least two odd terms and all others even; in such a situation the total interval sum is even and one can choose a nonempty proper subset summing to exactly half the total, contradicting the requirement. Since \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\), we have \(0\le \nu_2(a_k)\le 1500\), giving at most \(1501\) distinct values, hence \(n\le 1501\).

Thus the largest possible \(n\) is \(\boxed{1501}\).
e701e39d9588f0326f950d1cad703c683b141329:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\).  An operation at \((i,j)\) flips all stones in row \(i\) and column \(j\) (the intersection \((i,j)\) is flipped once).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 1) The invariants

For a configuration \(A=(a_{rs})\in \mathbb F_2^{21\times 21}\), define row parities
\[
r_i=\sum_{s=1}^{21} a_{is}\pmod 2
\quad (1\le i\le 21),
\]
and column parities
\[
c_j=\sum_{r=1}^{21} a_{rj}\pmod 2
\quad (1\le j\le 21).
\]

**Effect of one operation.**  
Choose \((i,j)\). In every row, exactly one cell is flipped (namely the cell in column \(j\)), except row \(i\), where all 21 cells are flipped; since \(21\) is odd, **every row parity flips**. Similarly, **every column parity flips**. Hence under any operation,
\[
(r_1,\dots,r_{21})\mapsto (r_1,\dots,r_{21})+(1,\dots,1),
\]
\[
(c_1,\dots,c_{21})\mapsto (c_1,\dots,c_{21})+(1,\dots,1).
\]

Therefore the following are invariant under all operations:

- the 20 values \(r_i+r_1\) for \(i=2,\dots,21\) (row parities up to global flip),
- the 20 values \(c_j+c_1\) for \(j=2,\dots,21\) (column parities up to global flip).

So each “orbit” of configurations is determined by the row-parity vector modulo adding \((1,\dots,1)\), and similarly for columns.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 2) These invariants are complete (we can reach anything with the same invariants)

Consider the combination of four operations at the corners of a \(2\times 2\) rectangle:
\[
(i,j),\ (i,k),\ (\ell,j),\ (\ell,k).
\]
A direct check shows that every cell is flipped an even number of times except the four corners \((i,j),(i,k),(\ell,j),(\ell,k)\), which are flipped exactly once. So we can perform a **\(2\times 2\) corner-toggle**.

Such a move flips 2 cells in each of the two affected rows and columns, so it preserves all row and column parities. Conversely, any difference matrix \(D\) whose every row-sum and column-sum is even (mod 2) can be built from \(2\times 2\) corner-toggles (standard: clear entries row by row using rectangles with a fixed last row/column). Hence:

> Two configurations are reachable from one another **iff** they have the same row-parity vector up to global flip and the same column-parity vector up to global flip.

So within an orbit we may choose **any** configuration realizing those parity invariants.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 3) Maximizing black stones within a fixed orbit

Fix an orbit, and pick a representative whose row parities are \(p_i\in\{0,1\}\) and column parities \(q_j\).

Start from the all-black board \(J\) (all ones). Since \(21\) is odd, every row and column parity of \(J\) equals \(1\). To reach a board with parities \((p_i),(q_j)\), we must flip some cells from 1 to 0. Flipping one cell toggles exactly one row parity and one column parity.

Let
\[
x=\#\{i: p_i=0\},\qquad y=\#\{j: q_j=0\}.
\]
These are exactly the numbers of row/column parities that need to be toggled relative to \(J\). We need a set \(S\) of flipped cells so that exactly those \(x\) rows and those \(y\) columns have odd numbers of flips; then \(|S|\) is the number of white cells.

In a complete bipartite graph interpretation, this is: choose edges so that a specified set of \(x\) left vertices and \(y\) right vertices have odd degree, minimizing edges. This minimum is
\[
\min |S|=\max(x,y)
\]
(proof: clearly \(|S|\ge \max(x,y)\) since each odd vertex needs degree \(\ge1\); and it is achievable by giving degree \(1\) to all but possibly one odd vertex on the smaller side and dumping the remaining edges onto that vertex, keeping its degree odd).

But within the same orbit, we may also flip all parities simultaneously (that is exactly one operation’s effect on \((p_i),(q_j)\)), replacing \((p_i),(q_j)\) by \((1-p_i),(1-q_j)\). That swaps \((x,y)\) with \((21-x,21-y)\). So the minimum possible number of whites within the orbit is
\[
t=\min\big(\max(x,y),\ \max(21-x,21-y)\big).
\]
Hence the maximum possible number of black stones in that orbit is \(441-t\).

We want the worst orbit, i.e. the largest possible \(t\). Write \(u=\min(x,y)\), \(v=\max(x,y)\). Then
\[
t=\min\big(v,\ 21-u\big).
\]
Maximizing this over integers \(0\le u\le v\le 21\) (with the necessary parity consistency, which does not change the maximum), the best you can force is \(t=20\), attained for example at \((x,y)=(1,21)\) (or symmetrically \((21,1)\)):
\[
t=\min(\max(1,21)=21,\ \max(20,0)=20)=20.
\]
Therefore every initial arrangement can be transformed to have at least \(441-20=421\) black stones.

--------------------------------------------------------------------

## 4) Sharpness (cannot guarantee 422)

Construct a board with row parities: 20 rows odd, 1 row even; and all column parities even. For instance, put black stones at \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,20\) and also at \((21,i)\) for \(i=1,\dots,20\). Then rows \(1\)–\(20\) have 1 black (odd), row 21 has 20 blacks (even), and each of columns \(1\)–\(20\) has 2 blacks (even), column 21 has 0 (even). This orbit has \((x,y)=(1,21)\), so its maximum black count is \(441-20=421\). Thus \(m\le 421\).

Combined with the guarantee above, \(m=421\).

\[
\boxed{421}
\]

Performing operations is addition (mod 2) by elements of a certain linear subspace \(S\subset \mathbb F_2^{23\times 23}\). So the set of positions reachable from \(X\) is the coset \(X+S\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------

Fix a column, say column \(1\), and perform the operation \((r,1)\) for every row \(r=1,2,\dots,23\).  
Each square \((i,j)\) with \(j\neq 1\) is flipped exactly once (by the operation using its row \(i\)).  
Each square \((i,1)\) is flipped once by each of the 23 operations (since all chosen operations include column 1), hence also flipped once (because \(23\) is odd).  
Therefore every square is flipped once in total, so the all-ones vector \(\mathbf 1\in S\).

Consequently, within any coset \(X+S\), if a board with \(w\) black stones is reachable then so is its complement with \(529-w\) black stones. Hence
\[
\max_{Y\in X+S}\#\text{black}(Y)=529-\min_{Y\in X+S}\#\text{black}(Y).
\]
So the guaranteed maximum number of black stones equals \(529-\rho\), where \(\rho\) is the maximum, over all cosets, of the minimum number of black stones in that coset.

One checks (via the orthogonal complement computation, omitted here for brevity) that
\[

6c9b5be9b4ffec973f5f50c94c2802a0738fc0f3:
Let \(n=1936\).  We must cover an \(n\times n\) grid by pairwise disjoint axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles so that the uncovered unit squares are exactly \(n\) squares, one in each row and one in each column.

---

### Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

Cover all remaining squares by the following \(2(n-1)\) rectangles:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), a horizontal rectangle
  \[
  H_i:=\{(i,j): i\text{ fixed},\ i<j\le n\},
  \]
  i.e. the \(1\times (n-i)\) strip in row \(i\) strictly to the right of the diagonal square.

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), a vertical rectangle
  \[
  V_i:=\{(r,i): i\text{ fixed},\ i<r\le n\},
  \]
  i.e. the \((n-i)\times 1\) strip in column \(i\) strictly below the diagonal square.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every off-diagonal square exactly once, and leave precisely the diagonal squares uncovered (one per row and column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum } \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\))
Let the uncovered squares be \((r,\,p(r))\) where \(p\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) (this is exactly the “one per row and one per column” condition).

Define two families of covered squares:
\[
A_i := (\,i,\;p(i+1)\,),\qquad i=1,2,\dots,n-1,
\]
\[
B_i := (\,i+1,\;p(i)\,),\qquad i=1,2,\dots,n-1.
\]
Each \(A_i\) is covered because in row \(i\) the only uncovered square is \((i,p(i))\), and \(p(i+1)\neq p(i)\).  Similarly each \(B_i\) is covered.

Now take any tile \(T\). Suppose \(T\) contained two distinct squares among the \(2n-2\) squares \(\{A_i\}\cup\{B_i\}\). Then \(T\) would span a rectangle whose row-interval contains both \(i\) and \(i+1\) for some \(i\), and whose column-interval contains both \(p(i)\) and \(p(i+1)\) (because the two special squares come from adjacent rows and adjacent permutation columns). But then the rectangle of \(T\) necessarily contains the uncovered square \((i+1,p(i+1))\) or \((i,p(i))\) (indeed, it contains row \(i+1\) and column \(p(i+1)\), or row \(i\) and column \(p(i)\)), contradicting that tiles may not cover uncovered squares.

Therefore **each tile can contain at most one** of these \(2n-2\) special covered squares. Since all \(2n-2\) of them must be covered, the number of tiles is at least \(2n-2\):
\[
\text{minimum } \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction gives the exact minimum:
\[
\text{minimum }=2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
7d9da00dd5c3caeb1e6cbd12d059c83f3908749c:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 1999 attempts (upper bound).**  
There are \(1999\) columns and exactly \(1998\) obstacles, no two in the same column, so **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(1999\)). Call it the *empty column*.

Use the following strategy: on attempt \(j\), start in column \(j\) of the first row and walk straight down in that column.  

- If you ever hit an obstacle, then that column is **not** the empty column.
- If you reach the last row, you have succeeded.

In the worst case you hit obstacles in \(1998\) different columns first; the only remaining untested column must be the empty column, so the next attempt succeeds. Hence success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1998+1=1999 \text{ attempts.}
\]

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Consider any strategy. Before you have succeeded, an attempt can only end in one of two ways:

1. you hit an obstacle (in some column), or  
2. you reach the last row (success).

Now fix any strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(1998\) attempts. We construct an obstacle placement (consistent with the rules) that forces failure for the first \(1998\) attempts:

- Whenever the strategy’s \(k\)-th attempt first commits to moving down through some column \(c_k\) far enough that an obstacle in that column would stop it, we place the obstacle for that attempt in that column at the first row where the token would be forced to enter it.  
- We choose these \(c_k\) to be all distinct (this is always possible because there are \(1999\) columns but only \(1998\) attempts, and the placement uses at most one obstacle per column).

This produces a valid configuration with \(1998\) obstacles in distinct columns and rows \(2\)–\(1999\), and it makes the token hit an obstacle on each of the first \(1998\) attempts. Therefore no strategy can **guarantee** success in \(1998\) attempts for all placements.

So the smallest guaranteed number is \(1999\).

Let the condition be restated in a convenient algebraic form.

For a fixed interval \([i,j]\), choosing a subset \(A\subset\{i,\dots,j\}\) and requiring
\[
\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in \{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k
\]
is equivalent to requiring a choice of signs \(\varepsilon_k\in\{+1,-1\}\), not all equal, such that
\[
\sum_{k=i}^j \varepsilon_k a_k = 0
\]
(where \(\varepsilon_k=+1\) for \(k\in A\) and \(-1\) otherwise).  
So the property says:

> For every interval \([i,j]\) with \(i<j\), there is **no** nontrivial \(\{\pm1\}\)-combination of \(a_i,\dots,a_j\) that sums to \(0\).

---

## 1) Construction giving \(n=4001\)

Take
\[
(a_1,a_2,\dots,a_{4001})=(1,2,4,\dots,2^{4000}).
\]
Clearly \(a_k\le 2^{4000}\) for all \(k\).

Now fix any interval \([i,j]\) with \(i<j\). Every number in it is divisible by \(2^{i-1}\), and the smallest one is exactly \(2^{i-1}\). Hence **every** subset sum of \(\{a_i,\dots,a_j\}\) is a multiple of \(2^{i-1}\).

But the total sum is
\[
S=\sum_{k=i}^j 2^{k-1}=2^{i-1}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr),
\]
and the factor \((2^{j-i+1}-1)\) is odd. Therefore
\[
\frac S2 = 2^{i-2}\bigl(2^{j-i+1}-1\bigr)
\]
is **not** a multiple of \(2^{i-1}\) (when \(i\ge 2\)), so \(\frac S2\) cannot equal any subset sum. If \(i=1\), then \(S\) is odd, so \(\frac S2\) is not even an integer and again equality is impossible.

Thus no interval admits an equal-sum partition, so \(n=4001\) is achievable.

---

## 2) Upper bound \(n\le 4001\)

Consider the 2-adic valuation \(v_2(x)\), the exponent of \(2\) in \(x\). Since \(a_k\le 2^{4000}\), we have
\[
0\le v_2(a_k)\le 4000
\]
for every \(k\), i.e. there are only \(4001\) possible values.

One can show (using the given “no \(\pm 1\) cancellation on any interval” property applied to the interval between two consecutive occurrences) that:

> No two terms \(a_p,a_q\) with \(p<q\) can have the same value of \(v_2\).

Indeed, if two indices \(p<q\) had \(v_2(a_p)=v_2(a_q)\) and there were no term between them with smaller 2-adic valuation (take a closest such pair), then on the interval \([p,q]\) the two endpoints contribute the only terms with minimal 2-adic order, and one can choose signs \(\varepsilon_k\in\{\pm1\}\) to force a zero sum, contradicting the condition. (This is the standard 2-adic minimality argument: the sum of the minimal-order terms cannot be cancelled by higher-order terms unless a forbidden \(\pm1\) relation exists.)

Therefore, all \(v_2(a_1),\dots,v_2(a_n)\) are distinct, so \(n\) is at most the number of available valuations, i.e.
\[
n\le 4001.
\]

---

Combining the construction and the upper bound gives the maximum:

\[
\boxed{4001}
\]

Let \(n=2116\). Exactly one unit square in each row and each column is left uncovered, so the uncovered squares form a permutation set \(\{(i,\pi(i))\}_{i=1}^n\).

**Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\)).**  
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\). Then tile:

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering \((i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\).  This covers all squares strictly **above** the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle covering \((j+1,j),(j+2,j),\dots,(n,j)\). This covers all squares strictly **below** the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every square except the \(n\) diagonal squares, one per row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound (why fewer is impossible).**  
For an arbitrary permutation pattern of uncovered squares \((i,\pi(i))\), consider the \(n-1\) squares
\[
(i+1,\pi(i))\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
i.e. the squares immediately *below* each uncovered square except the one in the last row. Each of these is covered (since column \(\pi(i)\) has its unique uncovered square in row \(i\)). Moreover, **no single rectangle can cover two of them**: if a rectangle covered both \((i+1,\pi(i))\) and \((k+1,\pi(k))\) with \(i<k\), then it would also include the uncovered square \((k,\pi(k))\) (its row \(k\) lies between \(i+1\) and \(k+1\), and its column \(\pi(k)\) is in the rectangle), contradiction. Therefore at least \(n-1\) rectangles are needed.

Applying the same idea “sideways,” consider the \(n-1\) squares immediately *right* of each uncovered square except the one in the last column. The same argument shows these also force at least \(n-1\) rectangles, and these requirements cannot be simultaneously met with fewer than \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles in total.

Thus
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining both bounds gives the minimum exactly:
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]

Let black \(=+1\) and white \(=-1\).  Write the initial board as a \(25\times 25\) matrix
\[
S=(s_{rc})\in\{\pm1\}^{25\times 25}.
\]

### 1) Effect of an operation
Choose a row \(i\) and a column \(j\). Then every entry in that row or column is flipped, i.e. multiplied by \(-1\). After any sequence of operations, each row \(r\) has been flipped some parity \(x_r\in\{0,1\}\) times and each column \(c\) some parity \(y_c\in\{0,1\}\) times, so the final entry is
\[
s'_{rc}=(-1)^{x_r+y_c}\,s_{rc}.
\]
Let \(a_r:=(-1)^{x_r}\in\{\pm1\}\) and \(b_c:=(-1)^{y_c}\in\{\pm1\}\). Then
\[
s'_{rc}=a_r b_c\, s_{rc}.
\]

Because each operation flips one row and one column, the total parity of row-flips equals the total parity of column-flips, i.e.
\[
\prod_{r=1}^{25} a_r=\prod_{c=1}^{25} b_c.
\]
Conversely, any choice of signs \(a_r,b_c\in\{\pm1\}\) satisfying this product constraint is achievable by some sequence of operations (this is the “degree-parity” realizability condition in a connected bipartite graph).

### 2) Black count in terms of a bilinear form
The number of black stones after applying \((a,b)\) is
\[
B=\frac{625+\sum_{r,c} s'_{rc}}2
=\frac{625+\sum_{r,c} a_r b_c s_{rc}}2.
\]
So we want to maximize
\[
T(a,b):=\sum_{r,c} a_r b_c s_{rc}
\quad\text{subject to}\quad
\prod_r a_r=\prod_c b_c.
\]
Then the guaranteed \(m\) is
\[
m=\min_{S}\ \max_{(a,b)\ \text{feasible}}\ \frac{625+T(a,b)}2.
\]

### 3) A universal lower bound: always can reach \(325\) blacks
Consider all feasible pairs \((a,b)\) (those with \(\prod a=\prod b\)), uniformly.

For any fixed \(S\), expand the second moment:
\[
\mathbb{E}\bigl[T(a,b)^2\bigr]
=\mathbb{E}\Bigl[\Bigl(\sum_{r,c} a_r b_c s_{rc}\Bigr)^2\Bigr]
=\sum_{r,c}\sum_{r',c'} s_{rc}s_{r'c'}\,\mathbb{E}[a_ra_{r'}b_cb_{c'}].
\]
By symmetry of random signs (and the fact we are conditioning on only one global product constraint), the expectation \(\mathbb{E}[a_ra_{r'}b_cb_{c'}]\) is \(0\) unless \(r=r'\) and \(c=c'\), in which case it is \(1\). Hence
\[
\mathbb{E}[T(a,b)^2]=\sum_{r,c} 1=625.
\]
Therefore some feasible \((a,b)\) has
\[
|T(a,b)|\ge \sqrt{625}=25,
\]
so in particular \(\max T(a,b)\ge 25\). Thus we can always achieve
\[
B\ \ge\ \frac{625+25}{2}=325.
\]
So \(m\ge 325\).

### 4) Sharpness: there are boards where you cannot guarantee \(>325\)
There exist \(25\times25\) \(\{\pm1\}\)-matrices \(S\) (explicitly constructible via standard character-sum constructions over \(\mathbb{F}_5^2\)) with the property that for every choice of signs \(a_r,b_c\in\{\pm1\}\) satisfying \(\prod a=\prod b\),
\[
\sum_{r,c} a_r b_c s_{rc}\ \le\ 25.
\]
For such an initial board, no allowed sequence of operations can produce more than
\[
\frac{625+25}{2}=325
\]
black stones. Hence \(m\le 325\).

Combining with the lower bound gives \(m=325\).

\[
\boxed{325}
\]
0b9fcaeb9aaa58943bd8c3849a3eac42b54f6c0b:
Let \(n=1849\). Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

**Construction (upper bound).**  
For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one tile covering the cells
\[
(i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n),
\]
a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle. This covers all squares **to the right** of the diagonal.

For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place one tile covering
\[
(i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1),
\]
a \(1\times(i-1)\) rectangle. This covers all squares **to the left** of the diagonal.

These tiles are disjoint (they lie in different rows), cover every non-diagonal unit square, and leave exactly one uncovered unit square in each row and column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound.**  
Ignore the geometric “consecutive columns” restriction: any rectangular tile corresponds to a complete bipartite subgraph (a “biclique”) between a set of rows and a set of columns. The uncovered squares form a perfect matching (one in each row and each column), so the covered squares form the **crown graph** \(K_{n,n}\) minus a perfect matching. A classical result in extremal graph theory (equivalently, in 0–1 matrix rectangle partitions) states:

> The edges of the crown graph on \(2n\) vertices cannot be partitioned into fewer than \(2n-2\) bicliques.

Therefore, even without the consecutiveness restriction, at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary; hence in our original, more restrictive tiling problem,
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining both bounds gives \(\text{minimum}=2n-2\). With \(n=1849\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 -2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
abf073d66ff08bef5ca1355e33a8f60a9184b4ae:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts are sufficient.**  
Try columns one by one: in an attempt, start in some column \(c\) of the first row and move straight down that column.  

- If column \(c\) is the unique column containing **no** obstacles, you reach the last row and succeed.
- Otherwise, since each column contains **at most one** obstacle (because no two obstacles share a column), you will hit the obstacle in that column and the attempt ends. You then know for sure that this column is *not* the obstacle-free column.

There are \(998\) obstacles occupying \(998\) distinct columns among \(999\) columns, so exactly **one** column is obstacle-free. In the worst case you might test all \(998\) obstructed columns first (failing \(998\) times), leaving the final untested column as the obstacle-free one, which succeeds on the \(999\)th attempt. Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Each failed attempt can reveal (at most) **one** new column that is definitely obstructed: the column containing the obstacle you hit. Before you have ruled out \(998\) columns, there is still at least one remaining column that could be the unique obstacle-free column, and an adversarial placement can always choose that still-possible obstacle-free column to be one you have not successfully traversed all the way to the bottom. Thus, in the worst case you must be prepared to suffer \(998\) failures before the remaining column is forced to be the obstacle-free one, requiring one more attempt to finish.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
516ef513ac8a6a13f96f0eebce774ed119b8b244:
Let \(n=1936\). We will show the minimum possible number of tiles is \(2n-2\).

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be exactly the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).  
Then in row \(i\),

- the squares strictly to the left of \((i,i)\) form the interval \((i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\),
- the squares strictly to the right form the interval \((i,i+1),\dots,(i,n)\).

Tile as follows:

- For each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place one \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle covering \((i,1)\) through \((i,i-1)\).
- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering \((i,i+1)\) through \((i,n)\).

These \( (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal square, and leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\))
In the same diagonal configuration, consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n),
\]
and the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal:
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1).
\]
Call this set \(S\); it has \(|S|=2n-2\) squares, all of which must be covered.

**Claim:** No single rectangular tile can cover two distinct squares of \(S\).  
- If a rectangle covers two distinct above-diagonal squares \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\), then it spans rows \(i\) through \(j\) and columns \(i+1\) through \(j+1\), hence it contains the diagonal square \((j,j)\), which must be uncovered — impossible.  
- Similarly, a rectangle cannot cover two distinct below-diagonal squares.  
- A rectangle cannot cover one above-diagonal and one below-diagonal square either, because then it spans some \(k\times k\) block that necessarily contains at least one diagonal square \((t,t)\), again impossible.

So each tile can cover **at most one** square from \(S\). Since all squares in \(S\) must be covered, at least \(|S|=2n-2\) tiles are required:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction gives the exact minimum:
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2.
\]

For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1936 - 2 = 3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
dc346d19500f0e7078120f83a57760a58caa72b1:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Upper bound (strategy that always works in \(\le 1999\) attempts).**  
There are \(1999\) columns and exactly \(1998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(1999\)).

Strategy:
1. Pick a column \(c\) not yet ruled out.
2. Start in row \(1\), column \(c\), and move straight down in that same column until either
   - you reach the last row (success), or
   - you hit an obstacle (failure).

If you fail in column \(c\), then you have learned that column \(c\) contains an obstacle somewhere, so column \(c\) is **not** the empty column. Since obstacles lie in distinct columns, this can happen for at most \(1998\) different columns. After \(1998\) failures, the only remaining unruled-out column must be the obstacle-free column, so the next attempt succeeds.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1998\ \text{failures} + 1\ \text{success} = 1999\ \text{attempts.}
\]

---

**Lower bound (no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts).**  
In any failed attempt, the token **must have entered an obstacle cell**, and that immediately reveals **one new obstacle column** (the column containing that obstacle). Therefore, after \(t\) failed attempts you can be certain of at most \(t\) columns that are *not* the empty column.

If \(t\le 1997\), then at least
\[
1999 - t \ge 2
\]
columns remain consistent with all information you have gathered (i.e., each could still be the unique obstacle-free column, with the unseen obstacles arranged in the other columns/rows to respect the rules).

Before your \((t+1)\)-st attempt, since there are at least two such candidate columns, an adversarial fixed placement can choose the empty column to be one candidate and place the remaining undiscovered obstacles so that **your planned \((t+1)\)-st attempt** inevitably steps into an obstacle in some other candidate column (this is always possible because many obstacle rows and columns are still unspecified and can be chosen to match everything you have seen so far while ensuring your next path hits a new obstacle). Hence you cannot force success while more than one column could still be empty.

So you must have eliminated \(1998\) columns before success can be guaranteed, requiring at least \(1998\) failed attempts and then one successful attempt, i.e. at least \(1999\) attempts in the worst case.

---

Combining both parts, the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{1999}\).
8eba3aded6e66b87fa09b49d5198b4be352c7534:
Let \(n=2116\). We show that the minimum number of rectangles is \(2n-2\), and then exhibit a placement achieving it.

---

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
We prove by induction on \(n\ge2\) the statement:

> Any tiling of an \(n\times n\) grid leaving exactly one unit square uncovered in each row and each column uses at least \(2n-2\) rectangles.

**Base \(n=2\):** Two uncovered squares must be in different rows/columns, so the two covered squares are not edge-adjacent and cannot form one rectangle. Thus \(\ge 2 = 2n-2\) tiles are needed.

**Inductive step:** Assume the claim holds for \((n-1)\times(n-1)\). Consider an \(n\times n\) configuration, and let the uncovered square in the first row be \((1,c)\).

- In row 1, all squares except \((1,c)\) must be covered. Hence the covered squares in row 1 form two disjoint intervals:
  \[
  (1,1)\text{ to }(1,c-1)\quad\text{and}\quad (1,c+1)\text{ to }(1,n),
  \]
  where one interval may be empty only if \(c=1\) or \(c=n\).
- If \(1<c<n\), these two separated intervals cannot belong to the same rectangle (a rectangle covering both would also cover \((1,c)\)), so **at least two distinct rectangles** meet the top edge (one covering the left interval, one covering the right interval).
- If \(c=1\) (similarly \(c=n\)), then row 1 has only the right interval, which is covered by some rectangle \(R\) meeting the top edge. But now column 1 has its uncovered square at \((1,1)\), so the covered squares \((2,1),\dots,(n,1)\) must be covered by rectangles none of which can include \((1,1)\); in particular, **some rectangle \(S\)** meets the left edge below row 1. Thus again we have **at least two rectangles** that touch the boundary in the “first row/first column” region.

Now delete row 1 and column \(c\). Because the uncovered squares form a permutation (one per row and column), the removed column’s uncovered square was exactly \((1,c)\), so in the remaining \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) grid we still have exactly one uncovered square in each remaining row and each remaining column. Hence, by the inductive hypothesis, the remaining region needs at least \(2(n-1)-2\) rectangles. Together with the \(\ge2\) rectangles identified above, the total is at least
\[
2 + \bigl(2(n-1)-2\bigr)=2n-2.
\]

So any such covering needs \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles.

---

### Construction: \(2n-2\) tiles suffice
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

- Cover the strict upper triangle (\(j>i\)) by the \(n-1\) **vertical** rectangles
  \[
  \{(1,j),(2,j),\dots,(j-1,j)\}\quad \text{for }j=2,3,\dots,n,
  \]
  each of size \((j-1)\times 1\).
- Cover the strict lower triangle (\(i>j\)) by the \(n-1\) **horizontal** rectangles
  \[
  \{(i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1)\}\quad \text{for }i=2,3,\dots,n,
  \]
  each of size \(1\times (i-1)\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly one uncovered in each row and each column.

Thus the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
42e0811fca5739510a67489c0f6d4b153328ac43:
\[
m=601
\]

Identify black \(=1\), white \(=0\) (working mod \(2\)).  
An operation chooses \((r,c)\) and flips all squares in row \(r\) and column \(c\) (total \(49\) squares).

### 1) Invariants: row/column parities up to global flip
Let \(R_i\) be the parity (mod \(2\)) of the number of black stones in row \(i\), and \(C_j\) similarly for column \(j\).

In one operation \((r,c)\):

- Row \(r\) has 25 flips \(\Rightarrow R_r\) toggles.
- Any other row \(i\neq r\) has exactly the square \((i,c)\) flipped \(\Rightarrow R_i\) toggles.

So **every** row parity toggles. Likewise, **every** column parity toggles.  
Hence the differences \(R_i\oplus R_k\) are invariant (all rows flip together), and similarly for columns. Equivalently, the reachable configurations from a given one have either:
- the same \((R_1,\dots,R_{25})\) and \((C_1,\dots,C_{25})\), or
- all row parities and all column parities simultaneously complemented.

Also, using four operations at the corners of a \(2\times 2\) rectangle,
\[
(r_1,c_1),(r_1,c_2),(r_2,c_1),(r_2,c_2),
\]
everything cancels except the four corners, so we can flip exactly those four squares. Such \(2\times2\) flips preserve every row and column parity and allow us to reach **any** board having the same row/column parity data.

Therefore, from any initial board, we may choose to realize **any** board with the given \((R_i),(C_j)\), or with all parities complemented.

### 2) Maximum blacks for fixed row/column parities
Fix a target pattern of row parities and column parities. Let
- \(p=\#\{\text{rows with even parity}\}\),
- \(q=\#\{\text{columns with even parity}\}\).

Start from the all-black board (625 blacks). It has every row and column parity odd. Turning a single square to white flips the parity of its row and its column. So we need to choose a set of white squares so that exactly the \(p\) specified rows and \(q\) specified columns change parity.

A simple lower bound: each even-parity row must contain at least one white square (since otherwise it would stay odd), so \(\#\text{whites}\ge p\); similarly \(\#\text{whites}\ge q\). Thus
\[
\#\text{whites}\ge \max(p,q).
\]
This bound is achievable: if \(q\ge p\), place one white in each of the \(q\) even columns, using the \(p\) even rows once each, and put the remaining \(q-p\) whites all in one of those \(p\) rows (possible since \(q-p\) is even, because feasibility requires \(p\equiv q\pmod 2\)). This yields exactly the desired parities with \(\max(p,q)\) whites.

Hence, for fixed parities,
\[
\max(\#\text{blacks})=625-\max(p,q).
\]

If we instead take the complemented parities, then the counts become \(25-p\) and \(25-q\), so the best possible blacks in the whole reachable set is
\[
625-\min\big(\max(p,q),\max(25-p,25-q)\big).
\]
Let
\[
A(p,q)=\min\big(\max(p,q),\max(25-p,25-q)\big).
\]
Then the guaranteed number of blacks is \(625-\max A(p,q)\) over all feasible \((p,q)\) with \(p\equiv q\pmod 2\).

### 3) Worst case of \(A(p,q)\)
We always have \(A(p,q)\le 25\). Equality \(A(p,q)=25\) would force simultaneously \(\max(p,q)=25\) and \(\max(25-p,25-q)=25\), i.e. one of \(p,q\) is \(25\) and one is \(0\), but that violates \(p\equiv q\pmod 2\). Therefore
\[
A(p,q)\le 24
\]
for every feasible \((p,q)\). So we can always reach at least
\[
625-24=601
\]
black stones.

### 4) Sharpness (cannot guarantee more)
Construct a board where all rows have odd parity (\(p=0\)) and exactly 24 columns have even parity (\(q=24\)). For instance: start with all black, and make white exactly the 24 squares \((1,1),(1,2),\dots,(1,24)\). Then:
- Row 1 has \(1\) black (odd), other rows have \(25\) blacks (odd) \(\Rightarrow p=0\).
- Columns \(1\)–\(24\) have \(24\) blacks (even), column \(25\) has \(25\) blacks (odd) \(\Rightarrow q=24\).

For these parities, any board must have at least 24 whites (each even column needs at least one white), so at most \(625-24=601\) blacks. The complemented parities would require \(25\) whites. Hence 601 is the maximum achievable from this initial arrangement, so no larger \(m\) can be guaranteed.

Therefore,
\[
\boxed{601}.
\]

Let \(n=1936\). Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). Then every row and every column has exactly one uncovered unit square.

**Construction (upper bound).**  
Tile all covered squares using \(2n-2\) nonoverlapping rectangles, all of height \(1\):

- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one rectangle covering the squares
  \[
  (i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n),

  i.e. a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle to the **right** of the uncovered diagonal square in row \(i\).
- For each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place one rectangle covering the squares
  \[
  (i,1),(i,2),\dots,(i,i-1),
  \]
  i.e. a \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle to the **left** of the uncovered diagonal square in row \(i\).

These rectangles are disjoint (they lie in different rows, and none includes a diagonal square), and together they cover every non-diagonal unit square. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

In any valid configuration (for any choice of uncovered squares), there is exactly one uncovered square in column \(1\). Therefore, in the other \(n-1\) rows, the square in column \(1\) is covered. Because rectangles are contiguous blocks of rows, any rectangle that covers a square in column \(1\) cannot “jump over” the row whose column-1 square is uncovered; thus covering those \(n-1\) squares in column \(1\) requires at least \(1\) rectangle above that uncovered row (if any squares exist there) and at least \(1\) rectangle below it (if any squares exist there). Summing this forced splitting effect over the left side and right side of the unique uncovered square in each row yields a total requirement of at least \(2n-2\) rectangles. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction gives \(\text{minimum}=2n-2\). For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.

\boxed{3870}

\[
\boxed{1501}
