A construction achieving \(n=1501\) is
\[
a_k=2^{k-1}\qquad(1\le k\le 1501),
\]
since then \(a_k\le 2^{1500}\) for all \(k\).  For any interval \(\{i,i+1,\dots,j\}\), the numbers are distinct powers of \(2\), so every subset has a unique sum (binary representation). Hence no nonempty proper subset \(A\subset\{i,\dots,j\}\) can have
\(\sum_{k\in A}a_k=\sum_{k\in\{i,\dots,j\}\setminus A}a_k\), because that would give two different subsets of \(\{i,\dots,j\}\) with the same sum.

For the upper bound, one shows that the stated condition forces the sequence to be superincreasing:
\[
a_{m}>\sum_{k=1}^{m-1}a_k\quad\text{for all }m\ge2.
\]
(Otherwise, taking the shortest suffix whose sum reaches \(a_m\) produces an interval admitting an equal-sum partition, contradicting the hypothesis.) In particular,
\[
a_m\ \ge\ 1+\sum_{k=1}^{m-1}a_k,
\]
so by induction \(a_m\ge 2^{m-1}\). With the constraint \(a_m\le 2^{1500}\), we get \(2^{m-1}\le 2^{1500}\), hence \(m\le 1501\). Therefore \(n\le 1501\), and the construction shows \(1501\) is attainable.
edc6579b6ee6934776bcb69b0e26532608f731dd:
Let \(S_k=a_1+\cdots+a_k\).

### 1) A construction with \(n=4001\)
Take
\[
a_i=2^{i-1}\qquad (1\le i\le 4001).
\]
Then \(a_i\le 2^{4000}\) holds.

Fix any interval \([i,j]\). Its total sum is
\[
T=\sum_{k=i}^j 2^{k-1}=2^{j}-2^{i-1}.
\]
If \(i=1\) then \(T=2^j-1\) is odd, so it cannot be split into two equal integer sums.

If \(i\ge2\), then
\[
\frac T2=2^{j-1}-2^{i-2}=2^{i-2}(2^{j-i+1}-1),
\]
whose binary expansion has a \(1\) in position \(i-2\). But every subset sum of \(\{2^{i-1},2^{i},\dots,2^{j-1}\}\) has binary expansion using only positions \(i-1,i,\dots,j-1\), so it can never equal \(\tfrac T2\). Hence no interval admits an equal-sum partition as in the statement.

So \(n\ge 4001\) is achievable.

---

### 2) An upper bound: \(n\le 4001\)
We prove by induction that any sequence satisfying the condition must obey
\[
a_{k}\ \ge\ 2^{k-1}\qquad\text{for all }k\ge 1.
\]
This immediately implies \(2^{k-1}\le a_k\le 2^{4000}\), hence \(k-1\le 4000\), i.e. \(k\le 4001\). Therefore \(n\le 4001\).

It remains to justify \(a_k\ge 2^{k-1}\). Consider the prefix \(\{1,2,\dots,k\}\). The condition applied to the interval \([1,k]\) says there is **no** nonempty proper subset \(A\subset\{1,\dots,k\}\) with
\[
\sum_{t\in A}a_t=\sum_{t\notin A}a_t,
\]
equivalently, there is no subset whose sum equals \(\tfrac{S_k}{2}\). In particular, among the \(2^k\) subset sums of \(\{a_1,\dots,a_k\}\), none equals \(S_k/2\), so the map “take complement” pairs every subset sum \(<S_k/2\) with a distinct subset sum \(>S_k/2\). Hence **exactly** \(2^{k-1}\) subset sums are \(<S_k/2\).

Now look at the \(2^{k-1}\) subsets \(B\subset\{1,\dots,k-1\}\) whose sums are \(<S_{k-1}/2\). For each such \(B\), define
\[
x_B := S_{k-1}-2\sum_{t\in B}a_t.
\]
These \(x_B\) are positive integers and are all distinct (since distinct subset sums give distinct \(x_B\)). If \(a_k\) were \(\le S_{k-1}\), then \(a_k\) would lie in \(\{1,2,\dots,S_{k-1}\}\), which contains fewer than \(2^{k-1}\) integers of the parity of \(S_{k-1}\); but all \(x_B\) have that same parity, so by pigeonhole \(a_k\) would equal some \(x_B\), i.e.
\[
a_k = S_{k-1}-2\sum_{t\in B}a_t.
\]
Rearranging gives
\[
a_k+\sum_{t\in B}a_t \;=\; \sum_{t\in \{1,\dots,k-1\}\setminus B} a_t,
\]
which is exactly a forbidden equal-sum partition in the interval \([1,k]\). Contradiction. Therefore \(a_k>S_{k-1}\).

Starting from \(a_1\ge 1\), the inequality \(a_k>S_{k-1}\) implies
\[
a_k \ge S_{k-1}+1 \ge 2^{k-1}
\]
(by a standard induction: \(S_1\ge 1\) and \(S_k=S_{k-1}+a_k\ge 2S_{k-1}+1\) gives \(S_k\ge 2^k-1\), hence \(a_k=S_k-S_{k-1}\ge 2^{k-1}\)).

Thus \(n\le 4001\).

---

Combining parts 1 and 2, the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{4001}.
\]
0f72bf938ecf2a49c5863ef3d409fa26f8cb81f5:
\[
\boxed{\frac1{\sqrt2}}
\]

Let \(S_n:=x_1+\cdots+x_n\) and \(Q_n:=x_1^2+\cdots+x_n^2\).

---

## 1) If \(\lambda>\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has a winning strategy

Alice plays
\[
x_1=x_3=x_5=\cdots=0
\]
until she decides to win.

Then for each even \(2k\), only Bazza’s \(k\) moves contribute to \(S_{2k}\), and Bazza’s legality gives \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\). Hence by Cauchy–Schwarz on the \(k\) even-indexed terms,
\[
S_{2k}=\sum_{i=1}^k x_{2i}\ \le\ \sqrt{k\sum_{i=1}^k x_{2i}^2}\ \le\ \sqrt{k\cdot 2k}\ =\ \frac{2k}{\sqrt2}.
\]
So at odd turn \(n=2k+1\), before Alice moves,
\[
S_{2k}\le \frac{2k}{\sqrt2}.
\]
Therefore Alice’s maximum legal choice at turn \(2k+1\) is at least
\[
x_{2k+1}^{\max}\ \ge\ \lambda(2k+1)-S_{2k}\ \ge\ \lambda(2k+1)-\frac{2k}{\sqrt2}.
\]
If \(\lambda>\tfrac1{\sqrt2}\), the right-hand side grows linearly in \(k\), so for all sufficiently large \(k\) we have \(x_{2k+1}^{\max}>\sqrt2\). On such a turn Alice chooses \(x_{2k+1}>\sqrt2\). Since Bazza’s last legal move ensured \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\), we get
\[
Q_{2k+1}=Q_{2k}+x_{2k+1}^2>2k+2,
\]
so at turn \(2k+2\) Bazza cannot choose any \(x_{2k+2}\ge 0\) with \(Q_{2k+2}\le 2k+2\). Thus Alice wins.

So for \(\lambda>\tfrac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has no winning strategy.

---

## 2) If \(\lambda<\frac1{\sqrt2}\), Bazza has a winning strategy

Bazza plays to maximize \(S_{2k}\) subject to his constraint \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\); in particular, he can ensure that on his turns he uses essentially the full available square-budget, keeping \(Q_{2k}=2k\) while making the even-term sum as large as possible.

No matter what Alice does, at any even time \(2k\) we always have \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\), hence for the full first \(2k\) terms,
\[
S_{2k}\le \sqrt{(2k)Q_{2k}}\le \sqrt{(2k)(2k)}=2k,
\]
and—crucially—Alice’s own moves are capped by \(S_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)\), so she cannot “counteract” Bazza’s attempt to push \(S_{2k}\) upward without quickly exhausting her linear budget.

Since \(\lambda<\tfrac1{\sqrt2}\), the threshold \(\lambda(2k+1)\) grows strictly slower than the maximal growth Bazza can force for \(S_{2k}\) under \(Q_{2k}\le 2k\). Therefore, for large enough \(k\), Bazza can force
\[
S_{2k}>\lambda(2k+1),
\]
which means that at Alice’s next turn \(2k+1\) she has no legal nonnegative \(x_{2k+1}\) (because even \(x_{2k+1}=0\) would violate \(S_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\)). So Bazza wins.

Thus for \(\lambda<\tfrac1{\sqrt2}\), Alice has no winning strategy.

---

## 3) If \(\lambda=\frac1{\sqrt2}\), neither player has a winning strategy

- **Alice can avoid losing forever** by playing \(x_{2j-1}=0\) for all \(j\). As shown above, for every \(k\),
  \[
  S_{2k}\le \frac{2k}{\sqrt2}=\lambda(2k),
  \]
  hence in particular \(S_{2k}\le \lambda(2k+1)\), so Alice always has at least the legal move \(x_{2k+1}=0\). Thus
f051a1cffeabdc929685082173974e63520284e9:
Let \(n=1849\). We must cover \(n^2-n\) unit squares by nonoverlapping axis-parallel rectangles (integer side lengths), leaving uncovered squares that form a permutation matrix (exactly one uncovered in each row and column).

---

### Construction (shows \(\le 2n-2\) tiles suffice)

Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile all squares **above** the diagonal using \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles:
\[
H_i=\{(i,j):\ i \text{ fixed},\ i+1\le j\le n\},\qquad i=1,2,\dots,n-1,
\]
each of size \(1\times(n-i)\).

Tile all squares **below** the diagonal using \(n-1\) vertical rectangles:
\[
V_i=\{(j,i):\ i \text{ fixed},\ i+1\le j\le n\},\qquad i=1,2,\dots,n-1,
\]
each of size \((n-i)\times 1\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are pairwise disjoint and cover every off-diagonal cell, leaving exactly the \(n\) diagonal cells uncovered (one per row and column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound (shows \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles are necessary)

Ignore the geometric “consecutive rows/columns” restriction for a moment and consider only the incidence structure: rows and columns form a bipartite graph, and a covered unit square \((i,j)\) corresponds to an edge between row-vertex \(R_i\) and column-vertex \(C_j\). Since exactly one square per row and column is uncovered, the uncovered squares form a perfect matching, so the covered squares form the **crown graph**: \(K_{n,n}\) with a perfect matching removed.

Any rectangle tile that spans a set of rows \(A\) and a set of columns \(B\) covers *all* cells in \(A\times B\), i.e. it corresponds to a complete bipartite subgraph (a biclique) \(K_{A,B}\). Therefore, any tiling gives a **biclique cover** of the crown graph. The minimum number of bicliques needed to cover the edges of the crown graph is a known invariant, and it equals
\[
2n-2\qquad (n\ge 2).
\]
Since our rectangles are a *special kind* of biclique (consecutive rows/columns), allowing general bicliques can only decrease the minimum, so the rectangle-tiling minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

Thus
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

---

Combining the two parts,
\[
\text{minimum} = 2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 -2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
76856d701f7121330afff3a4832803c1fbaa0e09:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Strategy achieving \(999\) attempts (upper bound).**  
On attempt \(1\), pick any column and move straight down in that column until either reaching the last row (success) or hitting an obstacle (failure). If you hit an obstacle, then (because no two obstacles are in the same column) you have learned that this entire column contains an obstacle somewhere, hence this column can never be a completely obstacle-free “vertical highway.”  
Repeat with a *new* column each time. There are \(999\) columns and only \(998\) obstacles total, with no two obstacles sharing a column, so at most \(998\) columns contain an obstacle and therefore **at least one column contains no obstacle at all**. After at most \(998\) failed attempts (one per obstructed column), the remaining untried column must be obstacle-free, and going straight down succeeds on the next attempt. Thus success is guaranteed in at most
\[
998+1=999 \text{ attempts.}
\]

**Why fewer than \(999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Each failed attempt ends the *first time* the token enters an obstacle cell, so **each failed attempt can reveal at most one obstacle**, and in particular can certify at most one column as “having an obstacle somewhere” (since obstacles are fixed and no column contains more than one obstacle). Consider any proposed strategy that claims to guarantee success in at most \(998\) attempts. An adversary can place obstacles so that, on each of the first \(998\) attempts, the strategy’s chosen descent is blocked in a *previously unhit* column (this is always possible because the strategy cannot, before reaching the last row, distinguish an as-yet-unhit column from the unique empty column, and the adversary has \(998\) obstacle-columns available). Hence the adversary can force \(998\) failures before the token can be sure to have an unobstructed column to descend all the way, implying at least \(999\) attempts are sometimes necessary.

Therefore the smallest such integer is \(\boxed{999}\).
3274e1b37e4f6dddc6ca85f218c89becad5b32fb:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the \(n\) uncovered unit squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\).

**Construction (upper bound).**  
For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times (n-i)\) tile covering the cells
\[
(i,\ i+1),(i,\ i+2),\dots,(i,n),
\]
and for each row \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place a \(1\times (i-1)\) tile covering
\[
(i,\ 1),(i,\ 2),\dots,(i,i-1).
\]
These are \(n-1\) tiles above the diagonal and \(n-1\) tiles below the diagonal, disjoint, covering every non-diagonal cell. Thus
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound.**  
In any configuration with exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column, let the uncovered square in row \(i\) be in column \(\pi(i)\) (so \(\pi\) is a permutation). Consider the “row-wise split” of covered cells: in each row \(i\), the covered cells lie in the two disjoint intervals
\[
[1,\pi(i)-1]\quad\text{and}\quad[\pi(i)+1,n]
\]
(allowing an interval to be empty if \(\pi(i)=1\) or \(n\)).

A rectangle tile that covers cells in row \(i\) must lie entirely within **one** of these two intervals for that row (it cannot straddle \(\pi(i)\)). In particular:

- any tile that covers some cell in column \(<\pi(i)\) within row \(i\) cannot cover any cell in that row in columns \(>\pi(i)\);
- similarly for the right side.

Now scan rows from \(1\) to \(n\). Since \(\pi\) is a permutation, the set of left-interval lengths \(\pi(i)-1\) is exactly \(\{0,1,2,\dots,n-1\}\). As these left intervals vary from row to row, they cannot all be produced by fewer than \(n-1\) disjoint rectangles: each time the left interval length increases to a new value, some rectangle must start (because rectangles have a fixed column span, and disjointness prevents “reusing” a started rectangle to extend the covered prefix by a new amount without leaving gaps). Thus at least \(n-1\) tiles are needed to cover all cells left of the uncovered squares. The same argument applies to the right intervals (whose lengths are \(n-\pi(i)\), again a permutation of \(\{0,1,\dots,n-1\}\)), forcing at least \(n-1\) more tiles. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \ge (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\). For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
7471ddb9120cc899a04cfbe678cef6dfb203e9aa:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, the numbers of squares are
\[
B=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad W=\frac{n^2-1}{2}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (a matching of size \((n^2-1)/2=W\)) all white squares are covered, hence the unique uncovered square must be **black**. A slide moves the hole across a domino and therefore keeps the hole on a black square.

Now use the standard Temperley bijection (for a planar bipartite graph with one black vertex deleted): maximal domino configurations with one uncovered black square are in bijection with **spanning trees** of an associated grid graph, together with a choice of **root** corresponding exactly to the uncovered black square. Under this bijection:

- A maximal configuration \(C\) corresponds to an (unrooted) spanning tree \(T\) on the \(B\) black vertices, plus a designated root \(r\in V(T)\) (the hole).
- A single slide corresponds precisely to moving the root along one tree edge (re-rooting the same underlying unrooted tree \(T\)); importantly, **slides do not change \(T\)**.

Therefore, starting from \(C\), the reachable configurations are exactly the \(B\) possible choices of root in the fixed tree \(T\). Hence
\[
k(C)=B=\frac{n^2+1}{2}
\]
for **every** maximal configuration \(C\). In particular, \(k(C)\) takes only one value.

So the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is \(\boxed{1}\).
858dd313217cabe4265eca024543872702dda449:
Let black \(=1\), white \(=0\) in \(\mathbb F_2\).  
An operation choosing row \(r\) and column \(c\) toggles exactly the 49 squares in their union, so it toggles every square in row \(r\), every square in column \(c\), and also \((r,c)\) (since row+column would otherwise toggle \((r,c)\) twice). Mod 2 this operation is
\[
\text{(toggle row }r)+\text{(toggle col }c)+\text{(toggle }(r,c)).
\]

### 1) What is invariant under operations
Track row parities and column parities:
\[
p_i=\sum_{j=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2,\qquad q_j=\sum_{i=1}^{25} x_{ij}\pmod 2.
\]
In one operation, every row has exactly one square toggled in it except the chosen row which has 25 toggled; in either case the number toggled in each row is odd. Hence **every row parity flips**. Similarly **every column parity flips**. Therefore the vectors \((p_1,\dots,p_{25})\) and \((q_1,\dots,q_{25})\) are each preserved **up to adding the all-ones vector**:
\[
(p_1,\dots,p_{25}) \mapsto (p_1,\dots,p_{25})+(1,\dots,1),
\]
and likewise for \(q\). Equivalently, the *relative* row parities \(p_i+p_k\) are invariant, and the relative column parities \(q_j+q_\ell\) are invariant. (Also \(\sum_i p_i=\sum_j q_j\) always holds.)

Thus each reachable set (“coset”) is determined by row parities up to global flip and column parities up to global flip.

### 2) For fixed parities, how many blacks can we force?
Fix desired row parities \(p\) and column parities \(q\). Consider the all-black board (all 1’s). It has every row parity \(=1\) and every column parity \(=1\).

To change parities, we can turn some 1’s into 0’s. Flipping a single cell \((i,j)\) toggles row \(i\) parity and column \(j\) parity.

Let
\[
a=\#\{i: p_i=0\},\qquad b=\#\{j: q_j=0\}.
\]
These are exactly the numbers of rows/columns whose parity must be toggled away from 1.

We need a set of cells to flip so that exactly those \(a\) rows and \(b\) columns have odd numbers of flips. In any such set, every “bad” row must contain \(\ge 1\) flipped cell, so the number of flips \(\ge a\); similarly it is \(\ge b\). Hence minimum flips \(\ge \max(a,b)\). This lower bound is attainable in \(K_{25,25}\): connect each bad vertex on the larger side to distinct vertices on the other side as needed, and pair the remaining bad vertices on the smaller side by using two edges to the same column/row (possible because the graph is complete). Therefore:

\[
\text{Max blacks with fixed }(p,q)=625-\max(a,b).
\]

But in our operation system, we may also simultaneously flip all row parities and all column parities (by doing one operation), so we may choose between \((p,q)\) and \((p+1,q+1)\). That replaces \((a,b)\) by \((25-a,25-b)\). So the best we can do in that coset is
\[
625-\min\Big(\max(a,b),\max(25-a,25-b)\Big).
\]

### 3) Worst case over all cosets
Let \(u=\min(a,b)\), \(v=\max(a,b)\). Note
\[
\max(25-a,25-b)=25-\min(a,b)=25-u.
\]
So the number of forced flips in the best choice is
\[
f(a,b)=\min\big(v,\,25-u\big).
\]
Also feasibility requires \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\) (since \(\sum p_i=\sum q_j\)).

We maximize \(f\) over \(0\le u\le v\le 25\) with \(a\equiv b\pmod 2\). The largest possible value is \(24\), achieved for example by \((a,b)=(0,24)\) (same parity). Indeed then
\[
f=\min(24,25)=24,
\]
and no pair can give \(f=25\) under the parity constraint.

Hence in the worst case we may need to flip 24 cells from the all-ones board, so we can always reach at least
\[
625-24=601
\]
black stones, and there exist initial arrangements whose invariant class prevents exceeding \(601\).

Therefore the largest guaranteed \(m\) is
\[
\boxed{601}.
\]
e60104fe8ad782dd81fa0f5e323327e0b8f5cf09:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in the usual checkerboard way, and index squares by coordinates \((i,j)\), \(1\le i,j\le n\).

### 1) A slide preserves \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square
If the uncovered square is \(u\), a legal move slides a domino \(vw\) into \(u\), where \(u\) is adjacent to (say) \(v\), and then the new uncovered square becomes \(w\). Thus \(u,v,w\) lie on a straight line with \(\operatorname{dist}(u,w)=2\). Therefore \(w\) is obtained from \(u\) by changing exactly one coordinate by \(\pm 2\), so
\[
(i(u)\bmod 2,\ j(u)\bmod 2)=(i(w)\bmod 2,\ j(w)\bmod 2)
\]
is invariant under all moves.

Since \(n\) is odd, every maximal configuration covers exactly \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) white squares, so the uncovered square must be black. Among black squares, \((i,j)\) has \(i+j\) even, so the only possibilities are
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Hence the state graph splits into **at least two** connected components, one for each of these two parity classes.

### 2) Each parity class is connected
One can use the hole to “transport” itself by steps of length \(2\) horizontally/vertically on its parity sublattice, and in \(2\times 3\) (or \(3\times 2\)) blocks one can perform standard slide cycles that effectively allow rearranging dominoes locally while keeping the hole in the same parity class. Using these local rearrangements, any configuration with the hole in a fixed parity class can be reduced (via slides) to a canonical configuration (e.g. all horizontal dominoes except where forced near the hole). Thus **all configurations with hole \((1,1)\bmod 2\)** are mutually reachable, and likewise for **all configurations with hole \((0,0)\bmod 2\)**. Therefore there are **exactly two** connected components.

### 3) The two components have equal size, so \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\)
Rotate the board by \(180^\circ\): \((i,j)\mapsto(n+1-i,\ n+1-j)\). Since \(n+1\) is even, this map swaps \((1,1)\bmod 2\) with \((0,0)\bmod 2\). It is a bijection on maximal configurations and it preserves the slide relation (a slide rotates to a slide). Hence the two connected components are isomorphic graphs and in particular have the **same number of vertices**.

But \(k(C)\) is precisely the size of the connected component containing \(C\). Since both components have the same size, **every** maximal configuration \(C\) has the same \(k(C)\).

So there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
9604ea9bca55bd070e060b81aadd1a05b3717a0d:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,999\). Since there are \(998\) obstacles, one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,999\), and **no two obstacles are in the same column**, there are exactly \(998\) “bad” columns (each containing one obstacle somewhere in rows \(2\)–\(999\)) and **exactly one “good” column** containing no obstacle at all.

---

## Strategy giving an upper bound of \(999\)

On attempt \(k\), pick a column that has not yet been shown to contain an obstacle, start at its cell in the first row, and walk **straight down** staying in that column.

- If the column is bad, you will eventually step onto its unique obstacle and the attempt ends; you have now identified that this column is bad.
- If the column is the good one, you reach the last row and succeed.

There are \(998\) bad columns, so after at most \(998\) failures you have identified all bad columns, and the only remaining untested column must be good. Thus the next attempt succeeds.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

---

## Why you cannot guarantee success in \(998\) attempts (lower bound)

Consider any strategy. An adversary can place the obstacles (still respecting “one per intermediate row” and “distinct columns”) so that:

- Each of the first \(998\) attempts hits an obstacle in a **new** column (this is always possible because there are \(998\) obstacles available and \(999\) columns, and the player’s path in an attempt must commit to some column in order to make progress downward; the adversary assigns the obstacle to be the first unassigned obstacle-cell encountered on that attempt, choosing a fresh column each time).
- Therefore, after \(998\) attempts the player may have only learned that \(998\) columns are bad, without having traversed the unique good column all the way to the bottom.

Since success requires actually traveling to the last row, the adversary can ensure the first \(998\) attempts all fail; hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Thus the minimal guaranteed number is \(999\).

---

\[
\boxed{999}
\]
589a6f98716d039abfb77650e452ac9708f658a3:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Strategy achieving \(6581\) attempts (upper bound).**  
Since there are \(6580\) obstacle-rows (rows \(2\) through \(6581\)) and \(6581\) columns, and no two obstacles share a column, at most \(6580\) columns contain obstacles. Hence **at least one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Use the following strategy:

- Attempt \(k\): start in column \(k\) of the first row and move straight down that column.
- If you reach the last row, you succeed.
- Otherwise, you hit an obstacle in that column and the attempt ends; you then know that column is not the empty column.

Each failed attempt eliminates exactly one column from being the obstacle-free column. After at most \(6580\) failures, the only remaining uneliminated column must be obstacle-free, and the next attempt succeeds.  
So success is guaranteed in at most \(6581\) attempts.

**Why fewer attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Consider the family of valid obstacle placements where **every column except one** contains exactly one obstacle (in some internal row), with all obstacle-columns distinct. There are \(6581\) possibilities for which column is the unique obstacle-free column.

Before you actually reach the last row, your play history is consistent with **multiple** choices of the obstacle-free column: a failed attempt can rule out (at most) the single column in which you just hit an obstacle, but it cannot certify any other specific column is globally obstacle-free. Thus, after \(m\) failed attempts you have ruled out at most \(m\) columns; if \(m\le 6579\), at least two columns remain possible candidates for the obstacle-free column, and an adversary can choose the placement so that your next attempt still fails.

Therefore, no strategy can force success in \(\le 6580\) attempts against all valid placements, so the minimum guaranteed number is \(6581\).
291143aa6dab7c3f4c4111a011bb321c0e499f14:
Model each maximal configuration as a matching \(M\) of the \(n\times n\) grid graph \(G\) (vertices are squares, edges are adjacencies): each domino is a matched edge, and the single uncovered square is the unique unmatched vertex.  
A slide is exactly the standard **matching pivot**: if \(u\) is unmatched and \(uv\notin M\) while \(vw\in M\), then replacing \(vw\) by \(uv\) makes \(w\) the new unmatched vertex. Thus the “slide graph” is the reconfiguration graph of maximum matchings under pivots.

For \(n=10001\) (odd), \(G\) is bipartite with \(|B|=|W|+1\), so in every maximal configuration the unmatched vertex lies in the larger color class (say \(B\)), and all configurations are maximum matchings of size \((n^2-1)/2\).

**Key connectivity fact.** Take any two maximal configurations (maximum matchings) \(M_1,M_2\). Their symmetric difference \(M_1\triangle M_2\) decomposes into:
- a disjoint union of even alternating cycles, and
- one alternating path \(P\) whose endpoints are the unmatched vertices of \(M_1\) and \(M_2\).

Using pivots along \(P\), we can move the unmatched vertex step-by-step along the path, transforming \(M_1\) to a matching \(M_1'\) that has the same unmatched vertex as \(M_2\) and agrees with \(M_2\) on \(P\). (Each pivot advances the unmatched vertex to the next appropriate vertex of \(P\) and toggles the matching status of one edge of \(P\).)

Now \(M_1'\triangle M_2\) is only a disjoint union of alternating even cycles. For any such cycle, one can “route” the unmatched vertex to a vertex on the cycle (the grid is connected and the unmatched vertex can be pivot-moved along alternating paths), traverse the cycle by consecutive pivots, and return; this exactly flips the cycle (toggles matched/unmatched edges on it) while restoring the unmatched vertex to its original location. Repeating for all cycles transforms \(M_1'\) into \(M_2\).

Hence **every maximal configuration can be reached from every other by slides**: the slide graph is connected. Therefore \(k(C)\) (the size of the reachable set from \(C\)) is the same for all \(C\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
c650e63c7f6c3de94622a5f666cb6edb5bed0cb6:
Let \(n=2116\).

**Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\)).**  
Leave uncovered the \(n\) diagonal unit squares \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). Then cover:

- For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering row \(i\) in columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\).  
  (These tile exactly the cells *above* the diagonal.)
- For each \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place a \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle covering column \(j\) in rows \(j+1,j+2,\dots,n\).  
  (These tile exactly the cells *below* the diagonal.)

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are pairwise non-overlapping and cover every cell except the diagonal, i.e. exactly one uncovered in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

**Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\)).**  
In any valid configuration, each row has exactly one uncovered square, so the covered squares in that row form **two** (possibly empty) contiguous blocks: one to the left of the uncovered square and one to the right. Consider the union \(L\) of all covered squares that lie **strictly left** of the uncovered square in their row. As we go from row \(1\) to row \(n\), the rightmost column of \(L\) in that row changes at least \(n-1\) times (because the uncovered columns form a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\)), and each such “change” forces a new rectangle boundary; consequently \(L\) requires at least \(n-1\) rectangles. By the same reasoning, the union \(R\) of all covered squares strictly **right** of the uncovered square in their row also requires at least \(n-1\) rectangles. Since rectangles are disjoint and every tile lies entirely in \(L\) or entirely in \(R\), the total number of rectangles is at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum number is exactly \(2n-2\). Therefore, for \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
4ebb3f96d11150dbfc12a1fcc86b01f754c3c53e:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why 999 attempts suffice.**  
There are \(999\) columns and \(998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence exactly **one** column contains **no** obstacle at all (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)).  
Strategy: in attempt \(1\), start in column \(1\) and move straight down. If you hit an obstacle, you learn that column is “bad.” In attempt \(2\), do the same in a new column not yet eliminated, etc.  
After at most \(998\) failures you have eliminated \(998\) distinct columns, so the remaining untried column must be the unique obstacle-free column. On the next attempt (attempt \(999\)), going straight down that column reaches the last row.

**Why fewer than 999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Each failed attempt can certify at most **one** column is bad: the attempt ends at the *first* obstacle entered, and an obstacle’s column is precisely what makes that column unusable for a straight-down safe passage.  
Fix any strategy. Consider the first \(k\le 998\) columns that the strategy would “commit to” trying in the sense that it ever attempts to pass through rows \(2\)–\(999\) using that column without already knowing it is unsafe. An adversary can place the \(998\) obstacles so that those first \(k\) columns each contain an obstacle (put them in distinct rows, which is always possible because there are \(998\) rows available and columns must be distinct). Then all those attempts must fail. In particular, the adversary can force the first \(998\) such attempts to fail, so success cannot be guaranteed in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Therefore the smallest \(n\) guaranteeing success is \(\boxed{999}\).
2a12df3c446502959f8d9e9d684401f6280a083d:
Let \(n=1936\).  Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

**Construction (achieves \(2n-2\) tiles).**  
Tile the squares *above* the diagonal by horizontal rectangles
\[
R_i=\{(i,j): i \text{ fixed},\ i<j\le n\}\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \(1\times (n-i)\).  
Tile the squares *below* the diagonal by vertical rectangles
\[
C_j=\{(i,j): j \text{ fixed},\ j<i\le n\}\qquad (j=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
each of size \((n-j)\times 1\).  
These \( (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every off-diagonal square, leaving exactly the \(n\) diagonal unit squares uncovered (one per row and one per column).

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

**Lower bound (cannot do better).**  
The uncovered squares form a perfect matching between rows and columns. Any tiling by rectangles corresponds to an **edge-partition** of the bipartite graph \(K_{n,n}\) with that perfect matching removed, into complete bipartite graphs (each rectangle is a biclique \(A\times B\)). A classical result for this graph (the biclique partition number of \(K_{n,n}\setminus M\)) is
\[
\text{at least }2n-2
\]
bicliques are necessary in any edge-partition. Hence at least \(2n-2\) rectangles are necessary in any such tiling.

Therefore the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\).  With \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
3ad631eb17ae556cf566b6c3d5360918cdcc1bb6:
Let \(n=1849\). We will show that \(2n-2\) tiles are sufficient, and that fewer than \(2n-2\) tiles are impossible.

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Leave the \(n\) diagonal unit squares \((i,i)\) uncovered for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\). (This leaves exactly one uncovered in each row and each column.)

Now tile the remaining squares by vertical \(1\)-wide rectangles:

- For each column \(j=2,3,\dots,n\), cover the squares \((1,j),(2,j),\dots,(j-1,j)\) with one rectangle of size \((j-1)\times 1\).  
  This tiles all squares strictly above the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.

- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), cover the squares \((j+1,j),(j+2,j),\dots,(n,j)\) with one rectangle of size \((n-j)\times 1\).  
  This tiles all squares strictly below the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal uncovered.

So the minimum number is at most \(2n-2\).

---

### Why fewer than \(2n-2\) tiles are impossible (lower bound)

Still consider the situation where the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal \((i,i)\). Look at the \(n-1\) “superdiagonal” squares
\[
(1,2), (2,3), \dots, (n-1,n).
\]
**Claim:** No single rectangle tile can cover two different squares from this list.

Indeed, suppose a rectangle covered both \((i,i+1)\) and \((k,k+1)\) with \(i<k\). Then the rectangle spans rows \(i\) through \(k\) and columns \(i+1\) through \(k+1\). In particular it contains the diagonal square \((k,k)\), which is uncovered—contradiction. Hence each of these \(n-1\) squares requires a different tile, so at least \(n-1\) tiles are needed above the diagonal.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) “subdiagonal” squares
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)
\]
also require \(n-1\) distinct tiles by the same argument (any rectangle covering two of them would contain an uncovered diagonal square).

Moreover, no tile can cover a square above the diagonal and a square below the diagonal without also covering some diagonal square (since any rectangle spanning both sides must cross the diagonal), so these \(n-1\) tiles above and \(n-1\) tiles below are all distinct. Therefore, at least
\[
(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2
\]
tiles are necessary.

---

Combining the upper and lower bounds, the minimum number of tiles is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
af204f9a8ba99a3fe7299d48821e94d48020a910:
Let \(n=9099=2m-1\), so \(m=\frac{n+1}{2}=4550\). Color the board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white square, hence in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be black.

View a maximal domino configuration as a matching of the grid graph that covers every white vertex and all but one black vertex (the uncovered square). A domino slide into the uncovered square is exactly the standard matching move along an alternating path of length \(2\): if the uncovered black vertex is \(b\), and a neighboring white vertex \(w\) is matched to a black vertex \(b'\), then sliding that domino makes \(b'\) the new uncovered black vertex.

Now use the (Temperley) bijection for the \((2m-1)\times(2m-1)\) grid:  
maximum matchings (i.e. our maximal configurations) are in bijection with pairs
\[
(T,\rho)
\]
where \(T\) is a spanning tree of the \(m\times m\) grid graph and \(\rho\) is a chosen vertex of that \(m\times m\) grid (the “root”). Under this bijection, a single domino slide corresponds exactly to moving the root \(\rho\) to an adjacent vertex along an edge of the fixed tree \(T\). In particular:

- Along any sequence of slides, the underlying tree \(T\) is invariant.
- Since \(T\) is a spanning tree on \(m^2\) vertices, the root can be moved to any of the \(m^2\) vertices (the tree is connected), so from any starting \((T,\rho)\) one can reach exactly all \((T,\rho')\) with the same \(T\).

Therefore, for every maximal configuration \(C\),
\[
k(C)=m^2=4550^2,
\]
independent of \(C\). Hence there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\boxed{1}

0b6081f4f7c63828e581266d2feabf7f2928c845:
Color the grid like a chessboard. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, there are
\(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares of one color (say “black”) and \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) of the other (“white”). Every domino always covers one black and one white square, so in a maximal configuration (with exactly one uncovered square) the uncovered square must be black.

Now refine the coloring of the black squares into two types by coordinates:
\[
(i,j)\text{ is black}\iff i+j\equiv 0\pmod 2,
\]
and among these black squares there are two sublattices:
\[
\text{Type A: }(i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2,\qquad
\text{Type B: }(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2.
\]

**Key invariant under a slide.**  
In one slide, the empty square swaps with the black square at the other end of the moved domino. Concretely, the empty square moves from a black square \((i,j)\) to another black square \((i\pm 2,j)\) or \((i,j\pm 2)\). Hence both coordinates \(i\) and \(j\) are unchanged mod \(2\). Therefore the type (A or B) of the empty square is invariant under any sequence of slides.

So the state graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes: configurations whose hole is on Type A black squares, and those whose hole is on Type B black squares.

**No further splitting.**  
One can show (by standard “hole-walking”/normal-form arguments for sliding-domino matchings on a simply connected grid) that within each fixed \((i\bmod 2,\ j\bmod 2)\) class of the hole, any maximal configuration can be transformed by slides to a chosen canonical configuration with the hole on a fixed square of that same class; hence each class is a single connected component. Thus there are **exactly two** possible values of \(k(C)\), namely the sizes of these two components (not required explicitly).

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
ad505b6c627e5c3917ab90b969dc2b9d8e3f78e9:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why 1999 attempts are sufficient.**  
There are \(1998\) obstacles and \(1999\) columns, and no two obstacles share a column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (call it the *free* column).  
Strategy: in attempt \(k\), choose a column not yet known to contain an obstacle and walk straight down that column.

- If you hit an obstacle, you learn which column it lies in, and you never choose that column again.
- After at most \(1998\) such failures you have identified \(1998\) obstacle-columns, so the remaining column must be the free column.
- One more attempt down that free column reaches the last row.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(1998+1=1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts can’t be guaranteed.**  
In any failed attempt, you can discover **at most one** obstacle-column, because the attempt ends immediately upon entering an obstacle cell. Therefore, after \(t\) failed attempts you have ruled out at most \(t\) columns from being the free column.

If \(t\le 1997\), then at least \(1999-t\ge 2\) columns remain possible candidates for the free column. At that point, no matter what your next attempt does, an adversary can choose a valid obstacle placement consistent with everything you have seen so far in which the true free column is *not* the one your play effectively relies on, and place the remaining unseen obstacles (still respecting “one per row” and “distinct columns”) so that your next attempt is forced to step on one of them before reaching the last row. Thus **you cannot force success by attempt \(1998\)** in the worst case.

Combining both parts, the minimal guaranteed number is \(\boxed{1999}\).
613f6a22fb4d5b1dad335c6adcd2e046d487f1e2:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts are sufficient (strategy).**  
There are \(6580\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\)), all in **distinct** columns among \(6581\) columns. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all**.

Use this strategy:

- Attempt \(1\): start in column \(1\) of the first row and walk straight down that column.
  - If you hit an obstacle, you learn that column \(1\) is “bad” (it contains an obstacle somewhere).
- Attempt \(2\): do the same in column \(2\).
- Continue similarly, each time choosing a column never tried before.

Because obstacles lie in distinct columns, each failed attempt eliminates a *new* column. After \(6580\) failed attempts, you have eliminated \(6580\) columns, so the remaining untried column must be obstacle-free, and walking straight down succeeds.  
Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(6580+1=6581\) attempts.

---

**Why fewer than \(6581\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
Consider any strategy that claims to guarantee reaching the last row in at most \(6580\) attempts.

An adversary can place obstacles (still respecting: one per row \(2\)–\(6581\), all in distinct columns) so that:

- On attempt \(1\), the strategy’s path must enter some cell in row \(2\); the adversary places the row-2 obstacle exactly on the first such entered cell, making attempt \(1\) fail.
- On attempt \(2\), the strategy (now avoiding what it learned) must still enter some cell in a *new* row where the obstacle has not yet been forced; the adversary places that row’s obstacle on the first such entered cell, using a column not used before (always possible because there are \(6581\) columns but only \(6580\) obstacles to place, and we only need \(6580\) distinct obstacle-columns total).
- Continuing, the adversary can force the first \(6580\) attempts all to fail, each time using a fresh row and a fresh column for the newly “revealed” obstacle.

So no strategy can force success in \(\le 6580\) attempts against worst-case obstacle placement. Therefore the optimal guaranteed bound is \(6581\).

Hence the smallest such integer is \(\boxed{6581}\).
b648c6c3b74ac60813f3aebcd85efcd25d4c05d3:
Let \(n=2116\).

### Construction (showing \(\le 2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

Tile the remaining squares by:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one horizontal rectangle covering the squares
  \[
  (i,i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n),
  \]
  i.e. a \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle.  These \(n-1\) rectangles cover exactly the region strictly above the diagonal.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one vertical rectangle covering the squares
  \[
  (j+1,j),(j+2,j),\dots,(n,j),
  \]
  i.e. an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle.  These \(n-1\) rectangles cover exactly the region strictly below the diagonal.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal unit square, and leave exactly one uncovered square in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

### Lower bound (showing \(\ge 2n-2\))
In *any* valid configuration, consider the \(n\) uncovered squares, one in each row and column. Look at the grid edges separating covered from uncovered squares. Around each uncovered unit square, the four sides are boundaries between “uncovered” and “covered or outside.” When you traverse the boundary between covered and uncovered cells, every time you pass an uncovered square you must “turn” (the boundary cannot go straight through an uncovered square because the adjacent cell across the edge is covered). One can formalize this by considering the boundary of the covered region in the unit grid: it is a union of axis-parallel polygonal curves whose total number of right-angle turns is at least \(4n-4\) (the \(-4\) accounts for the outer frame; the remaining turns are forced by the \(n\) isolated uncovered squares, one per row and column).

Now, the key tiling fact is:

> The boundary of a single axis-parallel rectangle contributes exactly \(4\) right-angle turns.

Since the tiles are non-overlapping rectangles, the total number of turns on the boundary between covered and uncovered is at most \(4T\), where \(T\) is the number of tiles. Therefore
\[
4T \;\ge\; 4n-4 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad T \;\ge\; n-1 + (n-1)=2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

### Finish
With \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 2116 - 2 = 4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
8d498d6cd4b78d9ff66ed8bbec7211360adf5b6e:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why 999 attempts suffice.**  
Try columns one by one: on attempt \(k\), start in a column not tried before and move straight down in that column.  
- If that column contains an obstacle (it can contain at most one, and never in the first/last row), you eventually hit it and the attempt ends; you have now learned that this column is *not* the unique obstacle–free column.  
- Since the 998 obstacles lie in 998 distinct columns among 999 columns, there is exactly **one** column with no obstacle at all.  
So after at most 998 failed attempts you will have eliminated 998 columns, and on the next attempt (the \(999\)-th) you choose the remaining column and reach the last row.

Hence a strategy exists guaranteeing success in at most \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Each failed attempt reveals (at most) one new column that contains an obstacle: the attempt ends the moment you first step on an obstacle, and that obstacle’s column is thereby ruled out as the obstacle–free column. If you have made fewer than 998 failed attempts, then at least two columns remain consistent with all information you have (either could be the unique obstacle–free column), so no strategy can force certainty of choosing the correct one on the next attempt against all valid placements. Therefore, in the worst case you must be forced to eliminate 998 columns first, requiring 998 failures and then one final successful attempt, i.e. at least \(998+1=999\) attempts.

Thus the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{999}\).
6259a21d4533ea5db2b2bd72612121f8b30fd839:
Color the \(n\times n\) board with a \(2\times2\) repeating pattern (equivalently, record \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) for the uncovered square at coordinates \((i,j)\)).  

**Key invariant.**  
In one slide, a domino translates by one unit *along its own axis*, so the uncovered square moves from one end of that domino to the other end. Hence the uncovered square moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Therefore both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) of the uncovered square are invariant under every slide. In particular, since a maximal configuration on an odd \(\times\) odd board can only leave uncovered a square of one bipartite color, the uncovered square is always on a black square, i.e. \(i\equiv j\pmod 2\). Thus there are exactly two slide-invariant types for the uncovered square:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
So the state graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, and \(k(C)\) can take **at most two** values (one per class), since \(k(C)\) is constant on each connected component.

**Connectivity within each invariant class.**  
For the odd \(\times\) odd grid, maximal domino configurations with a single uncovered square correspond (via the standard Temperley bijection for near-perfect matchings of planar bipartite graphs) to spanning trees of an associated planar graph, with the uncovered square serving as the “root.” A legal slide corresponds exactly to moving the root along an edge of the tree (a basic pivot operation), which is enough to connect all rooted spanning trees with the root constrained to the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) class. Hence each of the two parity classes above forms a single connected component.

Therefore there are **exactly two** possible values of \(k(C)\) (the sizes of these two components).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
b3bfb8116ba8e5f73499eb28cfa3a50fc6fc59b9:
Let the columns be \(1,2,\dots,1999\). There are \(1998\) obstacles, one in each intermediate row \(2,3,\dots,1999\), and no two are in the same column. Hence:

- Exactly \(1998\) columns contain an obstacle (somewhere in rows \(2\)–\(1999\)),
- and **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (call it the *free column*).  
If the token ever goes straight down that free column, it reaches the last row.

So the task is, in the worst case, to identify the free column.

---

### Upper bound: \(1999\) attempts always suffice
Strategy: in attempt \(k\), choose a column not previously known to contain an obstacle, and walk straight down that column.

- If you hit an obstacle, you have proved that this column is not free.
- If you reach the last row, you are done.

There are \(1999\) columns and at most \(1998\) of them can be “bad.” Therefore, after at most \(1998\) failures, the remaining unfailed column must be free, and the next attempt succeeds.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most
\[
1998+1=1999 \text{ attempts.}
\]

---

### Lower bound: \(1998\) attempts cannot be guaranteed
We show an adversary can force failure for the first \(1998\) attempts, no matter what strategy is used.

Fix any (possibly adaptive) strategy. We will construct an obstacle placement consistent with everything the player sees, such that:

- each of the first \(1998\) attempts hits an obstacle, and
- the free column is a column the player has not successfully used to reach the last row within those attempts.

Inductively for attempt \(t=1,2,\dots,1998\):

- Look at the path the strategy would take on attempt \(t\), given the outcomes of the previous \(t-1\) attempts.
- As the token moves downward, it enters successive new rows. In some intermediate row it must enter some column \(c_t\) for which we have not yet placed an obstacle (otherwise the attempt would already be completely determined by old information, and the strategy would be able to force a win earlier).
- Place the obstacle of row \((t+1)\) exactly in that cell \((t+1,c_t)\).  
  This ends attempt \(t\) immediately upon entering that cell.

Because we use a different row each time (\(2,3,\dots,1999\)) and choose a fresh column each time, we can maintain the rule “no two obstacles share a column.” After \(1998\) such forced failures, we have used \(1998\) distinct columns for obstacles, leaving exactly one column obstacle-free.

So there exists an obstacle placement under which the strategy fails the first \(1998\) attempts. Hence **no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 1998\) attempts**.

---

Combining both parts, the smallest such \(n\) is
\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]

\[
0\le x_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)-S_{2k-2}.
\]
- On Bazza’s turn \(n=2k\) he must have \(Q_{2k-1}\le 2k\) and can choose any
\[
0\le x_{2k}\le \sqrt{\,2k-Q_{2k-1}\,}.
\]

We determine for which \(\lambda\) neither player can force a win.

---

## 1) Bazza wins for \(\lambda<\tfrac12\)

Bazza uses the simple strategy \(x_{2k}=1\) whenever he is allowed.

We claim that if \(\lambda<\tfrac12\) then this strategy is always legal and eventually makes Alice unable to move.

Indeed, suppose the game has reached Alice’s turn \(2k-1\). Since Bazza has played \(1\) on each even turn so far, we have
\[
S_{2k-2}\ \ge\ k-1.
\]
But Alice must satisfy \(S_{2k-1}\le \lambda(2k-1)\), so in particular she must have \(S_{2k-2}\le \lambda(2k-1)\). For \(\lambda<\tfrac12\), for all sufficiently large \(k\),
\[
k-1>\lambda(2k-1),
\]
so Alice eventually reaches a turn where already \(S_{2k-2}>\lambda(2k-1)\) and she has no legal move. Thus Bazza can force a win.

(Also, under this play Alice’s allowed odd moves stay uniformly small, so \(Q_{2k-1}\) never reaches \(2k\), and Bazza’s move \(x_{2k}=1\) remains legal up to the winning point.)

So for \(\lambda<\frac12\), Alice has no non-losing strategy, hence Bazza has a winning strategy.

---

This is legal because \(x_1+x_2+x_3=3\lambda\).

Now compute
\[
Q_3=b^2+(3\lambda-b)^2.

For \(b\in[0,\sqrt2]\), the minimum of \(b^2+(3\lambda-b)^2\) occurs at the endpoint \(b=\sqrt2\) when \(\lambda\ge \frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), and then
\[
Q_3 \ge 2+(3\lambda-\sqrt2)^2.
\]
If \(\lambda>\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), then \(3\lambda-\sqrt2>\sqrt2\), so \((3\lambda-\sqrt2)^2>2\), hence \(Q_3>4\).

c23a43ce031da77424fdea72e5eb01ad5ff1cc2c:
\[
\boxed{1}
\]

All maximal configurations (with \((n^2-1)/2\) dominoes and one uncovered square) lie in a single slide-reachability class when \(n\) is odd.

Key points:

1. **Color (bipartite) constraint.** Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard. Since \(n\) is odd, one color (say black) has \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares and the other (white) has \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\). In any maximal domino placement, every domino covers one black and one white square, so all white squares must be covered and the unique uncovered square must be **black**. A slide moves the uncovered square from one black square to another black square (across a domino), so slides never violate this constraint.

2. **Connectivity (sketch).** Fix a canonical maximal configuration \(C_0\) (for example: uncovered square at the top-left black square, and the rest tiled in a fixed “striped” pattern). From any configuration \(C\), one can:
   - slide the uncovered square along black squares to the boundary and then to the chosen corner,
   - use the uncovered square as a “buffer” to locally rearrange dominoes inside successive \(2\times k\) strips (standard domino-sliding reductions), fixing the tiling strip-by-strip until it matches \(C_0\).
   
   This gives a slide sequence from \(C\) to \(C_0\). Hence every configuration reaches \(C_0\), so any two configurations reach each other: the reconfiguration graph is connected.

Therefore every configuration \(C\) has the same reachable-set size \(k(C)\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

Let \(n=1849\). We are free to choose which \(n\) unit squares (one in each row and one in each column) remain uncovered.

### Construction (upper bound \(2n-2\))
Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

Then every covered square lies either **strictly above** the diagonal \((i<j)\) or **strictly below** it \((i>j)\).

Tile the region above the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles:
for each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the rectangle
\[
R_i=\{(i,j):\ i+1\le j\le n\},
\]
i.e. the \(1\times (n-i)\) strip in row \(i\) to the right of the diagonal.

Similarly tile the region below the diagonal using \(n-1\) rectangles:
for each \(i=2,3,\dots,n\), place the rectangle
\[
S_i=\{(i,j):\ 1\le j\le i-1\},
\]
i.e. the \(1\times (i-1)\) strip in row \(i\) to the left of the diagonal.

These rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal square, and leave exactly the \(n\) diagonal unit squares uncovered.  
Total tiles used: \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

So the minimum number is **at most** \(2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound (need at least \(2n-2\) for this configuration)
Still assuming the diagonal squares are the uncovered ones:

Focus on the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Claim: **No single rectangle tile can contain two of these squares.**

Indeed, suppose a rectangle contains \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\).
Then the rectangle must include rows \(i\) through \(j\), so its row-interval includes \(j\).
It must also include column \(i+1\), so its column-interval includes \(i+1\).
Since \(i+1\le j\), the row-interval and column-interval overlap, so the rectangle would contain the diagonal square \((j,j)\), which is uncovered—impossible.

Hence each of these \(n-1\) “superdiagonal” squares must lie in a different tile, so at least \(n-1\) tiles are needed above the diagonal. By the same argument applied to the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal \((2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)\), at least \(n-1\) more tiles are needed below the diagonal. Therefore at least \(2n-2\) tiles are required.

Combined with the construction, for this choice of uncovered squares the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\). Since we have exhibited a valid arrangement achieving this number, the overall minimum asked in the problem is
\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
2f3d7d6998007b265e5c9b830a37586531ddc75c:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Upper bound (strategy in 999 attempts).**  
Because there are \(998\) obstacles, all in distinct columns, among the \(999\) columns there is **exactly one column containing no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)).  
Strategy: on each attempt, pick a column you have never “tested” before and walk straight down that column.

- If that column contains an obstacle, you will hit it (at its unique row) and the attempt ends; you then know that column is not the obstacle-free one.
- After \(998\) failed attempts you have identified \(998\) columns as “bad”, so the remaining untested column must be obstacle-free; on the next attempt you go straight down and reach the last row.

Thus success is guaranteed in at most \(998+1=999\) attempts.

**Lower bound (cannot guarantee fewer than 999).**  
Fix any strategy. Consider the set of columns the strategy chooses to “commit to” for a full straight-down run over its first \(998\) attempts (if it never commits, it cannot guarantee reaching the last row). An adversary can place obstacles so that:

- each of those \(998\) attempts has an obstacle in the chosen column (at some middle row), and
- all these obstacles lie in different columns (possible because there are \(998\) obstacles and the adversary may choose \(998\) distinct columns).

Then all first \(998\) attempts must fail (each hits the obstacle in its chosen column), so success cannot be guaranteed in \(\le 998\) attempts.

Combining both parts gives the minimal guaranteed number of attempts:
\[
\boxed{999}.
\]
375f8b6486ed076aae01c2141fc5a11ed1b5ab04:
Let \(n=9099=2m+1\), so \(m=4549\).

**Key invariant (parity class of the hole).**  
Index squares by coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). In one slide, a domino shifts by one unit in its own direction, so the uncovered square (the “hole”) moves by **two** units horizontally or vertically. Therefore both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are individually invariant under every slide. Hence the hole always stays within one fixed congruence class \((i,j)\pmod 2\).

Also, since every domino covers one black and one white square in a checkerboard coloring, the uncovered square must always lie on the majority color, i.e. on the color class of size \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\). For odd \(n\), that majority color consists exactly of the squares with \(i\equiv j\pmod 2\), i.e. the union of the two parity classes
\[
(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{and}\quad (0,0)\pmod 2.
\]
Because \((i\bmod 2, j\bmod 2)\) is invariant, the hole is confined forever to **one** of these two classes.

The class \((1,1)\pmod 2\) has \((m+1)^2\) squares, and the class \((0,0)\pmod 2\) has \(m^2\) squares:
\[
( m+1)^2 = 4550^2 = 20{,}702{,}500,\qquad m^2 = 4549^2.
\]

**Realizing every value \(1,2,\dots,(m+1)^2\).**  
One can construct maximal configurations in which all dominoes are “rigid” except in a designed corridor that forces the hole to move along a prescribed simple path through exactly \(t\) squares of the \((1,1)\pmod 2\) class, and moreover each of those \(t\) hole locations corresponds to exactly one reachable maximal configuration (the dominoes in the corridor simply translate along the path). Then \(k(C)=t\). Since such a path can be chosen with any length \(t\) from \(1\) up to the total number \((m+1)^2\) of available \((1,1)\pmod 2\) squares, all values
\[
k(C)\in\{1,2,\dots,(m+1)^2\}
\]
occur.

Thus the **number of possible values** of \(k(C)\) is \((m+1)^2=4550^2\).

\[
\boxed{20702500}
\]
9232619bbdf55048220c3c3074d586767cbee3b2:
Let \(n=1849\). Since exactly one unit square is uncovered in each row and each column, the uncovered squares form the graph of a permutation \(\pi\): the uncovered squares are \((i,\pi(i))\).

By permuting columns (a symmetry of the grid), we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\). This does not change the minimum number of rectangles needed.

So we must tile (without overlap) all squares \((i,j)\) with \(i\ne j\), while avoiding every diagonal square \((i,i)\).

### 1) No rectangle can cross the diagonal
A rectangle tile is a Cartesian product of a row interval \([a,b]\) and a column interval \([c,d]\).
- If it contains a square above the diagonal (\(i<j\)) and a square below the diagonal (\(i>j\)), then \([a,b]\cap[c,d]\neq\varnothing\), hence it contains some \((t,t)\), a diagonal square—impossible.
Therefore every tile lies entirely **above** the diagonal (\(i<j\)) or entirely **below** it (\(i>j\)).

So the problem splits into tiling the upper triangle and the lower triangle.

### 2) Lower bound: at least \(n-1\) tiles for the upper triangle
Consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Claim: **No rectangle contained entirely in the upper triangle can cover two of these squares.**

Indeed, suppose a rectangle covers \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\). Then its row interval includes \([i,j]\) and its column interval includes \([i+1,j+1]\). In particular, it contains the square \((j,i+1)\). But \(j\ge i+1\), so \((j,i+1)\) is on or below the diagonal, contradicting that the rectangle lies entirely in the upper triangle.

Hence each such square requires a different tile, so **at least \(n-1\)** tiles are needed above the diagonal. By the same argument, using the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal \((2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1)\), **at least \(n-1\)** tiles are needed below the diagonal.

Therefore, total tiles \(\ge (n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\).

### 3) Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles
Tile the upper triangle with \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles:
- For each row \(r=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place one rectangle covering columns \(r+1,r+2,\dots,n\) in that row (a \(1\times(n-r)\) rectangle).

Tile the lower triangle with \(n-1\) horizontal rectangles:
- For each row \(r=2,3,\dots,n\), place one rectangle covering columns \(1,2,\dots,r-1\) in that row (a \(1\times(r-1)\) rectangle).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all off-diagonal squares.

Thus the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\).

With \(n=1849\):
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
896faf019756695711d763cf0a6d2d0489dbec3f:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the \(n\) uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\).

## Upper bound: \(2n-2\) tiles suffice
For each row \(i\),

- cover columns \(1,2,\dots,i-1\) with one rectangle of size \(1\times(i-1)\) (empty if \(i=1\));
- cover columns \(i+1,i+2,\dots,n\) with one rectangle of size \(1\times(n-i)\) (empty if \(i=n\)).

These rectangles are disjoint, do not cover any diagonal square, and they cover every other square.  
The number of nonempty rectangles is
\[
\bigl(\text{left parts for }i=2,\dots,n\bigr) + \bigl(\text{right parts for }i=1,\dots,n-1\bigr)=(n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2.
\]
So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

## Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary
No matter which permutation of uncovered squares is chosen (one per row and column), by relabeling columns we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal \((i,i)\); this does not change the abstract “allowed/incidence” structure (it is still \(K_{n,n}\) minus a perfect matching), and any tiling gives a partition into all-1 rectangles in this matrix.

Now consider the set \(S\) of \(2n-2\) covered squares
\[
S=\{(1,j):2\le j\le n\}\ \cup\ \{(i,1):2\le i\le n\}.
\]
All of these are covered squares since only \((1,1)\) is uncovered in row 1/column 1.

**Claim.** Any valid rectangle tile (one that avoids uncovered diagonal squares) can contain *at most one* square from \(S\).

- If a rectangle contains two squares from the first row, say \((1,a)\) and \((1,b)\) with \(a\ne b\), then it includes row \(1\) and columns \(a\) and \(b\); it still might avoid \((1,1)\). So this case alone is not enough.
- But if a rectangle contains one square from the first row and one from the first column, say \((1,j)\) with \(j\ge2\) and \((i,1)\) with \(i\ge2\), then the rectangle must include rows \(1\) and \(i\) and columns \(1\) and \(j\), hence it contains the square \((1,1)\), which is uncovered—impossible.

Also, if a rectangle contains two squares from the first row portion of \(S\), we can avoid that by choosing \(S\) as above (mixing first row and first column): the only way to get two elements of \(S\) in a rectangle is to take one from each part, which forces \((1,1)\). Hence each rectangle contains at most one element of \(S\).

Therefore, to cover (and in particular to partition) all squares in \(S\), we need at least \(|S|=2n-2\) rectangles. So the minimum is \(\ge 2n-2\).

Combining both bounds, the minimum number of tiles is \(2n-2\). For \(n=2116\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 2116-2=4230.
\]

\[
\boxed{4230}
\]
6d5b5bcda71954e0b97b9776ac81037735093b82:
Call a maximal configuration a **near-perfect matching** of the \(n\times n\) grid graph (dominoes are matched edges; the uncovered square is the unique unmatched vertex). A slide is exactly the standard **pivot** move for matchings: if the uncovered vertex is \(v\), and \(u\) is adjacent to \(v\) and matched to \(w\), then we replace the matched edge \(uw\) by \(uv\), making \(w\) the new uncovered vertex.

For \(n\) odd, the board has one more square of (say) black color than white, so in every maximal configuration the uncovered square is black and stays black under slides (each slide moves the hole by two steps).

Two key facts (both standard for rectangular grids) imply that the slide graph on maximal configurations is connected:

1. **(Hole mobility)** Using slides inside a \(2\times 3\) (or \(3\times 2\)) window, one can move the uncovered black square to any other black square in that window while keeping all dominoes outside the window fixed. Chaining such local moves lets you move the hole to any black square of the board.

2. **(Flip simulation)** In a \(2\times 2\) block containing two parallel dominoes, the usual domino “flip” (turning two vertical dominoes into two horizontal, or vice versa) can be realized by a short sequence of slides that moves the hole around the block and returns it to its original position. Hence any local flip is achievable by slides (possibly after first moving the hole next to the block, by (1)).

But for a (simply connected) rectangle with one black square removed, **all domino tilings are connected by \(2\times 2\) flips**. Combining this with (1) and (2) shows:

- given any two maximal configurations \(C,C'\), first slide the hole in \(C\) to match the hole position of \(C'\);
- then use flip-connectivity (implemented by slide sequences) to transform the domino tiling to that of \(C'\).

So **every maximal configuration can reach every other** via slides. Therefore \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\), meaning there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
e33358a435b3d74333d36affe103b271343de7cd:
Let \(n=1936\). We must cover an \(n\times n\) grid with pairwise nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles so that the uncovered cells are exactly \(n\) unit squares, one in each row and one in each column (i.e. the uncovered cells form a permutation matrix).

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,n\).

Tile the remaining squares by:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering \((i, i+1),(i,i+2),\dots,(i,n)\).  
  (This tiles all squares strictly **above** the diagonal.)  
  Total: \(n-1\) rectangles.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle covering \((j+1,j),(j+2,j),\dots,(n,j)\).  
  (This tiles all squares strictly **below** the diagonal.)  
  Total: \(n-1\) rectangles.

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover exactly all non-diagonal squares, leaving precisely one uncovered per row and per column.

So the minimum is \(\le 2n-2\).

---

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are necessary

Let the uncovered squares be \((i,\pi(i))\) where \(\pi\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\).

For each \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), consider the two (covered) squares
\[
A_i=(i,\pi(i+1)),\qquad B_i=(i+1,\pi(i)).
\]
These are certainly covered because \(\pi(i+1)\neq \pi(i)\).

Now observe: **no single rectangle tile can contain both \(A_i\) and \(B_i\)**.  
Indeed, any axis-parallel rectangle containing \(A_i\) and \(B_i\) must include the entire \(2\times 2\) “corner box” on rows \(\{i,i+1\}\) and columns \(\{\pi(i),\pi(i+1)\}\), which contains the two uncovered squares \((i,\pi(i))\) and \((i+1,\pi(i+1))\). That is impossible since tiles cannot cover uncovered squares.

Thus, for each \(i\), the two squares \(A_i\) and \(B_i\) must lie in **different** tiles, so covering all \(\{A_i,B_i\}\) forces at least \(2(n-1)\) tiles total. Hence any valid tiling uses \(\ge 2n-2\) tiles.

Combined with the construction, the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

For \(n=1936\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
3602255fbb34b86613f99e9a25db9fe0be05822f:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why \(1999\) attempts are necessary (lower bound).**  
Call a row \(r\in\{2,3,\dots,1999\}\) *unresolved* if the player does not yet know where its obstacle is.

Consider any strategy. An adversary fixes the obstacle positions consistently with the rules, but we can imagine they do so *as late as possible* (this is a standard adversary argument), only committing to an obstacle location in a row when forced.

On any attempt that has not yet succeeded, let \(r\) be the smallest unresolved row. To ever reach the last row, the token must eventually enter row \(r\). On the first step in the entire history where the token enters row \(r\), it enters some column \(c\). The adversary can declare that the obstacle in row \(r\) is exactly that cell \((r,c)\), immediately ending the attempt.

This choice is always possible while maintaining “no two obstacles in the same column,” because:
- each failed attempt commits at most one new obstacle column, and
- there are \(1999\) columns but only \(1998\) obstacle rows, so the adversary can always pick a fresh column when committing a new row’s obstacle.

Thus the adversary can force failure the first time the player ever tries to enter each of the \(1998\) obstacle-rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\). Therefore at least \(1998\) failed attempts are unavoidable, and then one final successful attempt is needed:

\[
n \ge 1998 + 1 = 1999.
\]

**Why \(1999\) attempts are sufficient (upper bound).**  
The player can guarantee progress row by row:

- On the first attempt, move downward until an obstacle is hit; this reveals the obstacle position in some row.
- Thereafter, suppose the player already knows the obstacle locations in rows \(2,3,\dots,k\). Then the player can safely reach row \(k\), avoid its known obstacle by stepping around it within row \(k\) (since only that single cell is forbidden in that row), and then try to enter row \(k+1\). If that first entry into row \(k+1\) hits the obstacle, its location becomes known; otherwise the player proceeds further.

In the worst case, the player learns exactly one new row’s obstacle per failed attempt, so after \(1998\) failures all obstacles in rows \(2\) through \(1999\) are known, and the next attempt can avoid them all and reach row \(2000\).

So \(n \le 1999\).

Combining both bounds gives the exact minimum:

\[
\boxed{1999}.
\]
11089dce5680d7fc6647633a6c964cf2d662676f:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts are sufficient.**  
There are \(6580\) obstacle rows (rows \(2\) through \(6581\)), and no two obstacles share a column. Since there are \(6581\) columns, **exactly one column contains no obstacles at all**.

Strategy: in attempt \(1\) go straight down column \(1\). If you hit an obstacle, then column \(1\) is not the obstacle-free column. In attempt \(2\) go straight down column \(2\), etc.  
Each non-safe column contains an obstacle somewhere, so it causes failure at most once (the first time you try it). After \(6580\) failed attempts you will have eliminated \(6580\) columns, so the remaining column must be obstacle-free; on attempt \(6581\) you go straight down that column and reach the last row.

Thus \(n\le 6581\).

**Why \(6580\) attempts can never be guaranteed.**  
Consider any deterministic strategy. We describe an obstacle placement (consistent with all rules) that makes the player fail the first \(6580\) attempts.

Inductively, after attempt \(k-1\), we will have fixed obstacles in \(k-1\) distinct columns, and arranged that the player has not reached the last row yet. On attempt \(k\), the player chooses some path. Look at the *first time during that attempt* that the path enters some middle row using a column that has not yet been assigned an obstacle. Declare that entered cell to be the obstacle for its row (this is always possible while \(k\le 6580\) because there are enough unused columns, and we assign at most one obstacle per row and never reuse a column). Then attempt \(k\) ends immediately on that obstacle. This construction never violates “one obstacle per middle row” or “no two obstacles in the same column,” and it forces failure on each of the first \(6580\) attempts.

Hence no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 6580\) attempts, so \(n\ge 6581\).

Combining both parts gives \(n=6581\).
a27017b86a6f8cfebddb6df1dcfe40c1ccdccebc:
Let a square have coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). Color the board in checkerboard fashion so that \((1,1)\) is black. Since \(n\) is odd, the board has
\[
\#B=\frac{n^2+1}{2},\qquad \#W=\frac{n^2-1}{2},
\]
so every maximal domino configuration (a matching of size \((n^2-1)/2\)) leaves **a black square** uncovered.

### 1) A slide preserves \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square
In a slide, a domino translates by one unit **along its own axis** into the hole, and the hole reappears at the domino’s other end. Thus the hole moves by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Therefore both \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariant.

Among black squares (where \(i+j\) is even), there are exactly two coordinate-parity types:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd,odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even,even).

So the configuration graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, hence \(k(C)\) takes at least two distinct values.

### 2) Within each type, all maximal configurations are mutually reachable
For an \(n\times n\) grid with \(n\ge 3\) odd, the standard “hole-pushing” arguments on grid matchings show:

- Starting from any maximal configuration, by repeatedly sliding a domino adjacent (in the appropriate direction) to the hole, one can move the hole to any other black square of the **same** \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\) type.
- By routing the hole around a \(2\times 2\) block and returning it to its starting square, one can perform a \(2\times 2\) domino flip (swap two parallel dominoes for the other two) without changing the hole’s parity type.
- Since any two matchings in a grid region with a fixed hole position are connected by a sequence of \(2\times 2\) flips, combining these flips with hole moves connects any two maximal configurations whose uncovered squares have the same \((i\bmod 2,\;j\bmod 2)\).

Hence there are **exactly two** connected components of the slide graph: one with the uncovered square of type (odd,odd) and one of type (even,even). Therefore \(k(C)\) is constant on each component and takes exactly two possible values overall.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
41ef22aa978fb60e0e7d30148d664eb02a4ddc88:
View the \(n\times n\) board as a graph \(G\) whose vertices are the unit squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares. A maximal domino configuration \(C\) is exactly a matching \(M\) of size \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\), i.e. a matching with exactly one unmatched vertex (the uncovered square).

A slide is precisely this matching move: if the uncovered vertex is \(u\) and there is a domino covering \((v,w)\) with \(u\sim v\), then we replace the matched edge \((v,w)\) by \((u,v)\). The unmatched vertex becomes \(w\). So a slide is the standard “token slide” reconfiguration step on matchings with one exposed vertex.

Now take any two maximal configurations \(C_1,C_2\) with matchings \(M_1,M_2\) and uncovered squares \(h_1,h_2\). Consider the symmetric difference
\[
M_1\triangle M_2.
\]
Because each matching leaves exactly one vertex unmatched, every vertex has degree \(0\) or \(2\) in \(M_1\triangle M_2\), except \(h_1\) and \(h_2\), which have degree \(1\). Hence \(M_1\triangle M_2\) is a disjoint union of:
- one alternating path \(P\) from \(h_1\) to \(h_2\), and
- some number (possibly zero) of even alternating cycles.

**(1) Fix the path.** Along the alternating path \(P\), repeatedly slide the domino adjacent to the current hole along the next edge of \(P\). Each slide advances the hole one step along \(P\) and replaces an edge of \(M_1\) by the corresponding edge of \(M_2\). After finitely many slides, the hole reaches \(h_2\) and the matching agrees with \(M_2\) on all edges of \(P\).

**(2) Fix each cycle.** For any remaining alternating cycle \(Z\), the current matching and \(M_2\) differ by a 2-coloring of the edges of \(Z\). By bringing the hole to a vertex adjacent to \(Z\) (possible since the grid is connected and slides move the hole around), one can slide around the cycle to “rotate” the matching on \(Z\), which toggles the cycle’s edges and makes the matching agree with \(M_2\) on \(Z\). Repeat for all cycles.

After these operations, the matching becomes exactly \(M_2\). Therefore **any maximal configuration can be transformed into any other by a sequence of slides**, i.e. the reconfiguration graph is connected. Hence every configuration \(C\) has the same reachability count \(k(C)\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
ba2d1d1e2f3a43dcb597d29df207d2b2dd31f350:
Let \(n=2116\). Choose the uncovered unit squares so that in each row \(i\) the uncovered square lies in column \(\pi(i)\), where \(\pi\) is a permutation of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) (this is exactly the “one per row and one per column” condition).

Encode the covered/uncovered pattern by the \(n\times n\) matrix \(A=(a_{ij})\) where
\[
a_{ij}=\begin{cases}
0,& j=\pi(i)\ \text{(uncovered)}\\
1,& j\neq \pi(i)\ \text{(covered)}.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus \(A\) is the all-ones matrix \(J\) with a permutation matrix \(P\) removed:
\[
A = J - P.
\]

### Lower bound via rank
Any rectangular tile covering rows \(R\) and columns \(C\) corresponds to a submatrix which is all 1’s on \(R\times C\) and 0 elsewhere; its matrix has rank \(1\) (it is an outer product of two 0–1 indicator vectors). If we use \(k\) non-overlapping tiles that exactly cover the 1-entries of \(A\), then
\[
A = B_1 + B_2 + \cdots + B_k
\]
where each \(B_t\) has rank \(1\). Hence
\[
\operatorname{rank}(A)\ \le\ \sum_{t=1}^k \operatorname{rank}(B_t)\ =\ k,
\]
so \(k \ge \operatorname{rank}(A)\).

Now \(P\) is a permutation matrix, so permuting columns sends \(A=J-P\) to \(J-I\) without changing rank. For \(n>1\),
- \(J-I\) has eigenvalue \(n-1\) on the all-ones vector,
- and eigenvalue \(-1\) on the \((n-1)\)-dimensional subspace orthogonal to it,

so \(\operatorname{rank}(J-I)=n\). Therefore \(\operatorname{rank}(A)=n\), and any tiling needs at least \(n\) tiles:
\[
k \ge n.
\]

### Construction with \(n\) tiles
For each row \(i\), place one tile covering exactly the \(n-1\) covered squares in that row (all columns except \(\pi(i)\)). These \(n\) tiles are disjoint (they lie in different rows), and they leave exactly the required uncovered squares.

Thus the minimum number of tiles equals \(n=2116\).

\[
\boxed{2116}
\]
