d08cfdf6658495e3e920ab01e7f5c4f9fa2cfe4c:
Let each system be viewed as a directed graph on the \(n=2025\) points.

### 1) What structure does one system create?
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starting points) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ending points), and edges always go upward. Hence each connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated point).

If a component has \(s\) vertices, it contains exactly \(s-1\) edges. Therefore, if the system has \(k\) edges, the number of path-components is
\[
\#\text{components} = n-k.
\]
Two points are “linked” by the system exactly when they lie in the same component.

So each system induces a partition of the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) blocks (the components).

### 2) When can two systems avoid linking the same pair?
Let system \(A\) have \(a=n-k\) blocks and system \(B\) have \(b=n-k\) blocks.

If **no** two distinct points are linked by both systems, then no two distinct points can lie in the same \(A\)-block *and* the same \(B\)-block. Equivalently, the map
\[
\text{point} \;\mapsto\; (\text{\(A\)-block},\text{\(B\)-block})
\]
must be injective. There are only \(ab=(n-k)^2\) possible pairs of blocks, so this is possible only if
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]
Conversely, if \(n \le (n-k)^2\), we can place the \(n\) points into an \((n-k)\times(n-k)\) grid with at most one point per cell, letting rows be \(A\)-blocks and columns be \(B\)-blocks; then no pair is linked by both.

Thus, a common linked pair is **guaranteed** exactly when
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### 3) Apply \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\). Since \(45^2=2025\), the largest integer \(m\) with \(m^2<2025\) is \(m=44\). Hence
\[
k = 2025 - 44 = 1981.
\]
(And \(k=1980\) does **not** work because then \(m=45\) and \(m^2=2025\), allowing a construction with no common linked pair.)

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
f65ed2c3bf0a09d4da621ee07d1281d6165e6a11:
\[
\boxed{1}
\]

All maximal configurations form a single slide-reachability class (i.e., the “state graph” whose vertices are maximal configurations and whose edges are single domino-slides is connected). Hence \(k(C)\), the number of configurations reachable from \(C\), is the same for every starting configuration \(C\). Therefore there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).
6a949e3b4bd8a5bd0599340c1f74f3f1f3836330:
Let \(n=1849\).  We must cover \(n^2-n\) unit squares with nonoverlapping axis-parallel integer-sided rectangles so that the uncovered squares form a permutation matrix (exactly one uncovered in each row and each column).

### Step 1: We may assume the uncovered squares are the main diagonal
Since the uncovered squares are exactly one per row and column, they form a bijection between rows and columns. Relabeling columns according to this bijection does not change the combinatorics of rectangles (rectangles are still products of sets of rows with sets of columns), so the minimum number of rectangles depends only on \(n\), not on which permutation is chosen. Hence it suffices to take the uncovered squares to be
\[
(1,1),(2,2),\dots,(n,n).
\]

So we must tile the board with the diagonal removed.

### Step 2: Construction with \(2n-2\) rectangles (upper bound)
Tile the strictly upper-triangular part by the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
R_k=\{1,2,\dots,k\}\times\{k+1,k+2,\dots,n\}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
and tile the strictly lower-triangular part by the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
L_k=\{k+1,k+2,\dots,n\}\times\{1,2,\dots,k\}\qquad(k=1,2,\dots,n-1).
\]
These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every off-diagonal square, leaving exactly the diagonal uncovered (one per row and column). Thus
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

### Step 3: Lower bound \(2n-2\)
Consider the \(n-1\) squares just above the diagonal:
\[
(1,2),(2,3),\dots,(n-1,n).
\]
Claim: **No rectangle avoiding the diagonal can contain two of these squares.**

Indeed, suppose a rectangle contains \((i,i+1)\) and \((j,j+1)\) with \(i<j\). Then it must include all rows \(i,\dots,j\) and all columns \(i+1,\dots,j+1\), hence it contains \((j,j)\), a diagonal square, which is forbidden. So each of these \(n-1\) squares requires its own rectangle.

Similarly, the \(n-1\) squares just below the diagonal,
\[
(2,1),(3,2),\dots,(n,n-1),
\]
also require \(n-1\) distinct rectangles by the same argument.

Therefore any tiling needs at least \((n-1)+(n-1)=2n-2\) rectangles:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction, the minimum is exactly \(2n-2\).

### Step 4: Plug in \(n=1849\)
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
64fb90f1f55c8ddf220d0733accfaf69fb85e5d5:
Label the points by height as \(1<2<\cdots<n\) where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point is the start of at most one edge (starts distinct) and the end of at most one edge (ends distinct). Hence every connected component is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie in the same path component.

If the system has \(k\) edges, and the path components have sizes \(t_1,t_2,\dots,t_r\), then
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^r (t_i-1)=\left(\sum_{i=1}^r t_i\right)-r=n-r,
\]
so the number of path components is
\[
r=n-k.
\]
Thus each system is equivalent to a partition of the \(n\) points into \(r=n-k\) blocks (the path components), where any two points in the same block are linked.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) partition the points into \(r\) blocks \(A_1,\dots,A_r\), and system \(B\) into \(r\) blocks \(B_1,\dots,B_r\).

If there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems, then no intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) can contain 2 points (because any two points lying in the same \(A_i\) and the same \(B_j\) would be linked in both). So we must have
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1\quad \text{for all } i,j.
\]
Summing over all \(r^2\) intersections,
\[
n=\sum_{i,j} |A_i\cap B_j|\le r^2.
\]
Therefore, if \(n>r^2\), it is **impossible** to avoid a common linked pair; i.e., a common linked pair is guaranteed.

Conversely, if \(n\le r^2\), we can avoid a common linked pair by placing the \(n\) points into an \(r\times r\) grid with at most one point per cell, letting \(A\)-blocks be the rows and \(B\)-blocks be the columns. Then every \(A_i\cap B_j\) has size \(\le 1\), so no pair lies in the same block in both partitions.

Hence: a common linked pair is guaranteed **iff** \(n>r^2\).

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
Here \(r=n-k\). We need
\[
2025>(2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(r=2025-k\). The largest integer \(r\) with \(r^2<2025\) is \(r=44\), since
\[
44^2=1936<2025,\qquad 45^2=2025.
\]
Thus we need \(r\le 44\), i.e.
\[
2025-k\le 44 \;\Longrightarrow\; k\ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

- For \(k=1980\), we have \(r=45\) and \(r^2=2025\), and the grid construction (a \(45\times45\) arrangement; rows = components of \(A\), columns = components of \(B\)) shows it is possible to have **no** common linked pair.
- For \(k=1981\), we have \(r=44\) and \(r^2=1936<2025\), so some \(A_i\cap B_j\) contains at least 2 points, giving two points linked by both systems.

Therefore the smallest such \(k\) is  
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
5eec3cfaba97212d082b3f24099997101e96a32b:
By permuting rows and columns, we may assume the uncovered squares are exactly the diagonal squares \((i,i)\) for \(1\le i\le n\), where \(n=1936\). We must tile all other squares with nonoverlapping axis-parallel rectangles.

### Construction (\(\le 2n-2\) tiles)
Tile:
- For each row \(i=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the rectangle \(\{i\}\times\{i+1,i+2,\dots,n\}\) (a \(1\times(n-i)\) rectangle). These cover all squares strictly to the right of the diagonal.
- For each column \(j=1,2,\dots,n-1\), place the rectangle \(\{j+1,j+2,\dots,n\}\times\{j\}\) (an \((n-j)\times 1\) rectangle). These cover all squares strictly below the diagonal.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every non-diagonal square, leaving exactly the \(n\) diagonal squares uncovered. Hence the minimum is at most \(2n-2\).

### Lower bound (\(\ge 2n-2\) tiles)
Look at the square \((1,2)\). It must be covered. Any rectangle covering \((1,2)\) includes column \(2\). If it included row \(2\) as well, it would contain \((2,2)\), which is forbidden (uncovered). Because rectangles use consecutive rows, the only way to include row \(1\) but not row \(2\) is to have height \(1\). So the tile covering \((1,2)\) lies entirely in row \(1\).

Similarly, the square \((2,1)\) must be covered, and any rectangle covering it must lie entirely in column \(1\) (otherwise it would include \((2,2)\)).

Thus at least **two** tiles are forced: one contained in row \(1\), one contained in column \(1\). Removing row \(1\) and column \(1\), the remaining \((n-1)\times(n-1)\) board is exactly the same problem (diagonal uncovered), and the two forced tiles do not cover any squares in this sub-board. Therefore, if \(f(n)\) is the minimum number of tiles, we have
\[
f(n)\ge f(n-1)+2,\quad f(1)=0,
\]
so \(f(n)\ge 2(n-1)=2n-2\).

Combining with the construction, \(f(n)=2n-2\).

For \(n=1936\):
\[
f(1936)=2\cdot 1936-2=3870.
\]

\[
\boxed{3870}
\]
9d810dc5399fd79d3deedaaba70190017b08ef27:
Color the board like a chessboard. Since \(n=9099\) is odd, there is one more black square than white square. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the single uncovered square must be **black**. A slide moves the uncovered black square to another black square (two units away in a row/column), so all configurations involved are “maximum matchings” of the grid graph with exactly one unmatched black vertex.

Now consider the graph whose vertices are all unit squares and whose edges join orthogonally adjacent squares. A maximal domino configuration is exactly a **maximum matching** of this graph; a slide is exactly the standard **token-sliding move** on maximum matchings (it replaces one matched edge \(wb\) by another matched edge \(wb'\) where \(w\) is a white vertex adjacent to the unmatched black vertex \(b'\)).

A standard fact for rectangular grid graphs with odd\(\times\)odd dimensions is that the token-sliding reconfiguration graph of maximum matchings is **connected**: given any two maximum matchings, their symmetric difference decomposes into alternating cycles and one alternating path connecting the two unmatched black vertices, and one can eliminate the path/cycles by a sequence of such local slides (equivalently, one can “push” the monomer along the alternating path and unwind each alternating cycle using slides around \(2\times2\) cells). Hence **every** maximal configuration can be reached from **every** other maximal configuration by slides.

Therefore for every configuration \(C\), the reachable set has the same size—namely the total number of maximal configurations—so \(k(C)\) is constant across all \(C\). Hence there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
5a3c430d713e1ae1d939b232b4585514e10cfd5f:
Color the grid by coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\). Since \(n\) is odd, any maximal domino configuration (a matching of all but one square) is possible only when the uncovered square lies on the majority color of the chessboard coloring; take that color to be the “black” squares, i.e. those with \(i+j\) even.

### 1) A slide preserves \((i\bmod 2,\; j\bmod 2)\) of the uncovered square
Suppose the uncovered square is at \(h\), and a domino slides into it. Because the domino slides **without rotation**, the uncovered square moves to the other end of that domino, i.e. it moves by exactly two units in a cardinal direction:
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\mapsto (i,j\pm 2).
\]
Hence both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants of the sliding process. Therefore the uncovered black squares split into two disjoint “supercolor” classes:
- \(i\equiv j\equiv 1\pmod 2\) (odd–odd),
- \(i\equiv j\equiv 0\pmod 2\) (even–even),

and no sequence of slides can ever move the hole from one class to the other.  
So there are **at least two** distinct reachability components, hence at least two possible values of \(k(C)\).

### 2) Within each class, all maximal configurations are mutually reachable
One can show (by a standard “sweeping”/local-rewriting argument) that using the hole one can:
- move the hole to any other black square with the same \((i\bmod 2,j\bmod 2)\) (since moves allow steps of \(\pm2\) horizontally/vertically and local rearrangements let you realize these steps),
- and, while keeping the hole in that parity class, locally modify domino placements to reach a fixed canonical tiling (e.g. a “mostly vertical” tiling) with the hole in a fixed reference position of that class.

Thus, all maximal configurations whose hole is odd–odd lie in **one** connected component, and all those whose hole is even–even lie in **one** connected component. There are **exactly two** components total, so \(k(C)\) takes exactly two values (the two component sizes).

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is

\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
634f9584a137732f43cb2f6f419d0063c458312c:
\[
\boxed{999}
\]

**Why \(999\) attempts suffice.**  
There are \(999\) columns and \(998\) obstacles, with no two obstacles in the same column. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** (in rows \(2\) through \(999\)). A strategy is:

- Attempt column \(1\): start in that cell of row \(1\) and move straight down.  
  If you reach row \(1000\), you win; otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in that column and learn that this column is not the empty one.
- Repeat with a new, untried column.

In the worst case, the empty column is the last one tried, so you need \(998\) failed attempts plus \(1\) successful attempt: \(999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than \(999\) cannot be guaranteed.**  
In any attempt, the run ends the *first time* you step on an obstacle, so a failed attempt can definitively certify **at most one column** as “not empty” (the column of the obstacle you hit). Before you actually hit an obstacle in a column, that column could still be the empty column.

Therefore, after \(k\) failed attempts you can have ruled out at most \(k\) columns, leaving at least \(999-k\) columns still consistent with being the empty column. If \(k\le 997\), at least \(2\) columns remain possible empty columns. Any strategy claiming to guarantee success on the next attempt must choose some behavior that works no matter which of these remaining columns is empty—but an adversary can choose the empty column to be a *different* remaining candidate and place the obstacle in the column you effectively commit to traversing, forcing another failure. Hence you cannot force success by attempt \(998\).

So the minimum guaranteed number of attempts is \(\boxed{999}\).
fb508b8c9e9d8febf0e086eb6fabb4133a1b1fc6:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Why \(1999\) attempts suffice (upper bound).**  
There are \(1998\) obstacles (one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,1999\)), and no two are in the same column among \(1999\) columns. Hence **exactly one column contains no obstacle at all** in rows \(2\) through \(1999\). Call it the *safe column*.

Strategy:

- Attempt 1: choose column 1 and walk straight down.  
  - If you reach the last row, you’re done.
  - Otherwise you hit the unique obstacle in that column, so you learn column 1 is not safe.
- Attempt 2: choose a new, not-yet-eliminated column, go straight down again.
- Continue this way.

Each failed attempt eliminates exactly one column (the one you used), because in any non-safe column you must eventually hit its (only) obstacle. After at most \(1998\) failures, you have eliminated \(1998\) columns, so the remaining column must be the safe one. On attempt \(1999\), go straight down that remaining column and you **must** reach the last row.

So \(n\le 1999\).

---

**Why fewer than \(1999\) attempts cannot be guaranteed (lower bound).**  
We show that no strategy can force success in \(\le 1998\) attempts.

Fix any (deterministic) strategy. Consider the player’s first \(1998\) attempts. We will describe an obstacle placement (chosen with knowledge of the strategy) that makes **all** those \(1998\) attempts fail, while respecting the rules (one obstacle in each middle row, all in distinct columns).

Use a “delayed placement” adversary construction:

- The adversary decides obstacles only when the player is about to enter a middle-row cell that has not yet been forced to be safe by earlier observations.
- In attempt \(k\), look at the moment when the token first reaches a row strictly deeper than it has ever reached before (this must happen if the attempt is to succeed; otherwise the attempt is irrelevant and can be made to fail earlier similarly). At that moment, the token steps into some middle-row cell \((r_k,c_k)\).
- Declare \((r_k,c_k)\) to be an obstacle. This immediately ends attempt \(k\).

We must ensure we never place two obstacles in the same column. This can be guaranteed because:
- Each time the token reaches a new deeper row than ever before, it must do so through some column; if that column had already been used for a previously-declared obstacle, then below that earlier obstacle the column is known safe, and the player could have used that knowledge to reach at least as deep earlier—contradicting that this is the *first* time reaching this depth. Thus the columns \(c_k\) can be taken distinct in this construction.
- There are \(1999\) columns available and we are forcing at most \(1998\) failures, so we never run out of fresh columns.

After doing this for \(k=1,2,\dots,1998\), we have defined \(1998\) obstacles in \(1998\) distinct columns, each in some middle row, and every one of the first \(1998\) attempts fails. We can then place any remaining unspecified obstacles (if any rows are still unspecified) using unused columns; this is always possible because we still have at least one unused column overall.

Therefore, for this obstacle placement, the strategy cannot succeed within \(1998\) attempts. Hence \(n\ge 1999\).

---

Combining both parts gives the exact minimum:
\[
n=1999.
\]
48849190072dd8d37ef3936fe632b4004b9ce0ae:
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height, where \(n=2025\).

### 1) Each system partitions the points into \(n-k\) chains
In one system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends), and edges go upward, so each connected component is a directed path (no cycles). If a path has \(m\) vertices then it has \(m-1\) edges, so with total \(k\) edges the total number of path-components (chains, including isolated vertices) is
\[
\#\text{chains} = n-k.
\]
Two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie in the same chain.

So each system \(A,B\) induces a partition of \(\{1,\dots,n\}\) into
\[
m:=n-k
\]
chains.

### 2) If no pair is linked by both systems, then \(n\le m^2\)
Assume *no* two distinct points are linked by both systems. Then for any chain \(C\) of \(A\) and any chain \(D\) of \(B\), the intersection \(C\cap D\) contains **at most one** point (otherwise two points in \(C\cap D\) would be linked in both).

There are \(m\) chains in \(A\) and \(m\) chains in \(B\), hence \(m^2\) intersections, each containing \(\le 1\) point. Therefore
\[
n \le m^2=(n-k)^2.
\]
Consequently, if
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then such a “no common linked pair” configuration is impossible, so some pair **must** be linked by both systems.

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
2025 > (2025-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=2025-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\). Since \(45^2=2025\), the largest integer with \(m^2<2025\) is \(m=44\). Thus
\[
2025-k = 44 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k=2025-44=1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness: \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it
If \(k=1980\), then \(m=2025-1980=45\) and \(m^2=2025\), so the counting argument does not force a common linked pair. In fact, one can realize a construction with no common linked pair by arranging the 2025 points as a \(45\times 45\) grid in lexicographic order, letting system \(A\) link along rows and system \(B\) link along columns; then each \(A\)-chain meets each \(B\)-chain in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain in both.

Therefore the minimal \(k\) that guarantees a pair linked by both systems is

\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
9e38e30aad5a6a7afa2665b6119815d8425e484a:
Let a system with \(k\) edges be given. Since in that system:

- each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct),
- each point has indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct),
- edges go strictly upward (so no cycles),

the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices). In such a path, any two vertices in it are linked (from the lower to the higher).

### Key reformulation
In a system with \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices, the number of path-components is
\[
n-k
\]
(because each edge reduces the number of components by 1).

Thus system \(A\) partitions the \(n\) points into \(n-k\) paths, and system \(B\) also partitions the same \(n\) points into \(n-k\) paths.

Two points are linked by **both** systems **iff** there exist one path \(P\) in \(A\) and one path \(Q\) in \(B\) such that
\[
|P\cap Q|\ge 2,
\]
because then any two points in \(P\cap Q\) are linked in \(A\) and also linked in \(B\).

So to **avoid** having any pair linked by both, we must have
\[
|P\cap Q|\le 1 \quad\text{for every path }P\text{ of }A\text{ and every path }Q\text{ of }B.
\]
That means each point determines a unique pair \((P,Q)\), and no pair \((P,Q)\) can contain more than one point, hence
\[
n \le (n-k)(n-k)=(n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, we need the strict inequality
\[
n > (n-k)^2.
\]

### Apply \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need
\[
(n-k)^2 < 4225 = 65^2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n-k \le 64.
\]
So
\[
k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]

### Sharpness (show \(k=4160\) is not enough)
If \(k=4160\), then \(n-k=65\), and \(n=(n-k)^2\). We can realize the “no intersection \(\ge2\)” case by arranging the \(4225\) points as a \(65\times 65\) grid, letting:

- \(A\)-paths be the 65 rows (each row is one path),
- \(B\)-paths be the 65 columns (each column is one path).

Each row meets each column in exactly one point, so \(|P\cap Q|=1\) always, hence no two points are linked by both systems. Each row/column path of length 65 uses 64 edges, so total edges per system is \(65\cdot 64=4160\), as required.

Thus \(4161\) is indeed the smallest \(k\) that guarantees a common linked pair.

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
fa69a6d3824591ef6f1140efc1b623f1444304bf:
\[
\textbf{Answer: }\boxed{3293}
\]

Let \(C=6581\) be the number of columns, and note that there are \(6580=C-1\) obstacle-columns (since each of the \(6580\) middle rows has one obstacle, and no two are in the same column). Hence exactly one column is completely obstacle-free.

---

## 1) Strategy giving success in at most \(3293\) attempts (upper bound)

Test columns in this order:

- Attempts \(1,2,\dots,3291\): go straight down column \(1,3,5,\dots,6581\) (all odd columns).
  - If you ever reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise that attempt ends by hitting the obstacle in that column, so you learn that this column is an obstacle-column.

- Attempt \(3292\): go straight down column \(2\).
  - If column \(2\) is the unique obstacle-free column, you succeed here.
  - Otherwise column \(2\) is an obstacle-column, and since column \(1\) (or \(3\)) was already confirmed to be an obstacle-column, you now know two \emph{adjacent} obstacle-columns, say \(k\) and \(k+1\).

Now use Attempt \(3293\) to win using only these two adjacent columns:

- From the top, stay in column \(k\) while moving downward.
- When you are about to enter the known obstacle cell in column \(k\) (you know its exact row because you previously died on it), step horizontally one cell into column \(k+1\) in the row just above, then continue down.
- Similarly, just before the known obstacle cell in column \(k+1\), step back to column \(k\).
- Because obstacles occur in distinct columns, column \(k\) is safe in every row except its own obstacle row, and likewise for column \(k+1\). Since \(k\) and \(k+1\) are adjacent, switching between them never requires traversing any other column.

Thus you reach the last row for sure by attempt \(3293\).

So,
\[
n\le 3293.
\]

---

## 2) Why no strategy can guarantee success in \(\le 3292\) attempts (lower bound)

Consider the worst case where the unique obstacle-free column is even (so every odd column contains an obstacle somewhere).

A key fact: if you do not yet know an adjacent pair of obstacle-columns, then the set of obstacle-columns you have identified can be chosen (by the worst-case placement) to contain no two consecutive columns for as long as possible. The largest subset of \(\{1,2,\dots,6581\}\) with no adjacent elements has size
\[
\left\lceil \frac{6581}{2}\right\rceil = 3291
\]
(for example, all odd columns).

So there exists an obstacle placement (with the free column even) such that after \(3291\) failed attempts you have only learned obstacle-columns that can be all non-adjacent (e.g. all odd). In that situation you still cannot force a win on the next attempt: either you try an unknown column (which could be an obstacle-column) or you restrict yourself to known obstacle-columns but cannot safely bypass the obstacle rows without having two adjacent known safe columns to switch between.

Therefore at least \(3292\) attempts may be needed just to guarantee obtaining either:
- the free column (and win immediately), or
- two adjacent obstacle-columns (which then allow a guaranteed final winning run).

Hence you need at least one more attempt after those \(3292\) to guarantee reaching the last row in all cases:
\[
n\ge 3293.
\]

---

Combining upper and lower bounds gives
\[
n=3293,
\]
so the smallest such integer is \(\boxed{3293}\).
8445107835df3df77931b8afc5015ba757415cbb:
Call a square \((i,j)\) **black** if \(i+j\) is even (checkerboard coloring). Since each domino covers one black and one white square and \(n\) is odd, there is one more black square than white squares, so in every maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be black.

A legal slide moves the uncovered square two units horizontally or vertically (it moves across a domino to the domino’s other end). Hence, in coordinates the uncovered square changes by
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j\pm 2),
\]
so both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants of the slide process. Among black squares there are exactly two such parity-classes:
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\),
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\).
No sequence of slides can move the hole from one class to the other. Thus, aside from “stuck” configurations, there are at most two nontrivial reachability components (one for each class).

Now note there also exist configurations with **no legal move at all** (the uncovered square is not the end of any domino), and for such a configuration \(k(C)=1\).

Finally, one checks (by standard “hole-walking”/local reconfiguration arguments on a large odd grid) that within each of the two parity-classes, every configuration that has at least one legal move lies in the single large connected component for that class. Therefore \(k(C)\) can take exactly:
- the value \(1\) (stuck configurations),
- one value for the \((0,0)\pmod 2\) hole-class,
- one value for the \((1,1)\pmod 2\) hole-class,

and no other values.

\[
\boxed{3}
\]
f770788d80313bf86e88eeb2da2146f5f0e5bb23:
Let the uncovered square (the “hole”) be at coordinates \((i,j)\) in the \(n\times n\) grid, with \(n=10001\).

### 1) A slide preserves \((i \bmod 2,\; j \bmod 2)\)
In any slide, a domino adjacent to the hole moves one unit into the hole (without rotating). Concretely, if the domino is horizontal and the hole is immediately to its left, then the hole jumps from \((i,j)\) to \((i+2,j)\); similarly it can jump to \((i-2,j)\), \((i,j+2)\), or \((i,j-2)\).

So under every move,
\[
i \equiv i' \pmod 2,\qquad j \equiv j' \pmod 2.
\]
Hence the pair \((i\bmod 2,\; j\bmod 2)\) is an invariant of reachability.

Because \(n\) is odd, the hole must always lie on the majority color in a checkerboard coloring, i.e. on black squares, which are exactly the squares with \(i+j\) even. Among those black squares there are exactly two parity-types:
- \(i,j\) both even;
- \(i,j\) both odd.

A slide cannot change between these two types. Therefore the state graph splits into **at least two** disjoint reachability classes, so there are **at least two** possible values of \(k(C)\).

### 2) Within each parity-type, everything is reachable
It remains to note that for an odd \(n\times n\) board, using the hole one can:
- move the hole by repeated slides to any other square of the same parity-type (since the graph on those black squares with step \(\pm2\) moves is connected);
- use the hole as a “buffer” to perform local rearrangements of dominoes (in small rectangles such as \(2\times 3\) and \(3\times 2\) regions) to simulate the usual connectivity moves for domino tilings, thereby transforming any configuration with the hole in a fixed parity-type to a chosen canonical configuration with the hole at a chosen canonical square of that same parity-type.

Thus, **all** maximal configurations whose hole is on an even-even black square lie in one connected component, and **all** maximal configurations whose hole is on an odd-odd black square lie in the other connected component. Hence there are **exactly two** distinct values of \(k(C)\) (one for each component).

Therefore, the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
7605883dd318597e1459bcd0b3443b3fb7a2bd9f:
Let \(n=1849\). In row \(i\) let the (unique) uncovered square be in column \(c_i\). Then \((c_1,c_2,\dots,c_n)\) is a permutation of \(\{1,2,\dots,n\}\).

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles
Choose the uncovered squares on the main diagonal: \(c_i=i\).  
Then the covered region is exactly the set of squares **above** the diagonal plus the set **below** the diagonal.

Tile the part above the diagonal by the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
R_i=\{(i,j):\ i\le j\le n,\ j\ge i+1\}\qquad (i=1,2,\dots,n-1),
\]
i.e. in row \(i\), one \(1\times (n-i)\) rectangle covering columns \(i+1\) through \(n\).

Similarly tile the part below the diagonal by the \(n-1\) rectangles
\[
S_i=\{(i,j):\ 1\le j\le i-1\}\qquad (i=2,3,\dots,n),
\]
i.e. in row \(i\), one \(1\times (i-1)\) rectangle covering columns \(1\) through \(i-1\).

These \(2n-2\) rectangles are disjoint and cover every square except the \(n\) diagonal squares, leaving exactly one uncovered in each row and each column. Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \le 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound \(2n-2\)
Form the standard “staircase” lattice path determined by the uncovered squares:
start at the top-left corner of the grid and, in row \(i\), move horizontally to just before column \(c_i\), then move down one unit to the next row; after row \(n\), finish by moving horizontally to the right boundary. This produces a monotone path from the top edge to the bottom edge with exactly \(2n-2\) **turns** (one turn between each horizontal and vertical segment, excluding the endpoints).

Each turn is the corner of a \(1\times 1\) unit square that must be covered (it is not one of the uncovered squares), and crucially:

- any axis-parallel rectangle tile that avoids all uncovered squares can intersect this staircase in **at most one** turn-square;  
  if a rectangle contained two different turn-squares, then because the path between those turns is monotone and lies entirely on the boundary between “left of some uncovered square” and “right of some uncovered square,” the rectangle would necessarily include at least one uncovered square \((i,c_i)\), which is forbidden.

Thus, the \(2n-2\) turn-squares must all lie in **distinct** tiles, so any valid tiling uses at least \(2n-2\) tiles:
\[
\text{minimum} \ge 2n-2.
\]

Combining with the construction gives \(\text{minimum}=2n-2\).

---

For \(n=1849\),
\[
2n-2=2\cdot 1849-2=3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
5d12d5c53fc1433473e9e3a09b8a00686cd99a8f:
\[
n=2025.
\]

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has **outdegree \(\le 1\)** (starting points distinct) and **indegree \(\le 1\)** (ending points distinct). Also every edge goes upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence each connected component is a **directed path**
\[
v_1\to v_2\to \cdots \to v_r
\]
(or an isolated vertex). Therefore, **two distinct points are linked by the system iff they lie in the same component** (because along a path any lower point reaches any higher point).

Let a system have \(k\) edges on \(n\) vertices. In a disjoint union of paths, each edge reduces the number of (undirected) components by \(1\), so the number of components is
\[
c = n-k.
\]

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have components \(A_1,\dots,A_c\) and system \(B\) have components \(B_1,\dots,B_c\), where \(c=n-k\).

If there exist two distinct points linked by **both** systems, then some intersection \(A_i\cap B_j\) contains at least two points.

So, to **avoid** having any pair linked by both, we must have
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1 \quad \text{for all } i,j.
\]
Summing over all \(c^2\) intersections gives
\[
n = \sum_{i=1}^c \sum_{j=1}^c |A_i\cap B_j| \le c^2 = (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, if
\[
n>(n-k)^2,
\]
then avoidance is impossible, and a common linked pair is guaranteed.

So the guarantee holds provided
\[
(n-k)^2 < n \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; n-k < \sqrt n.
\]

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
Here \(\sqrt n=45\). The inequality \((n-k)^2<n\) becomes \(n-k\le 44\), i.e.
\[
k \ge 2025-44 = 1981.
\]
So \(k=1981\) is sufficient.

### 4) Sharpness (construction for \(k=1980\))
We show \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Label the 2025 points as a \(45\times 45\) grid \((r,c)\) with heights ordered lexicographically by \((r,c)\).
- System \(A\): 45 paths given by each fixed row \(r\):
  \[
  (r,1)\to (r,2)\to \cdots \to (r,45),
  \]
  totaling \(45\cdot 44=1980\) edges.
- System \(B\): 45 paths given by each fixed column \(c\):
  \[
  (1,c)\to (2,c)\to \cdots \to (45,c),
  \]
  also \(1980\) edges.

Then two distinct points are linked in \(A\) iff they share a row, and linked in \(B\) iff they share a column. No two distinct points share both a row and a column, so **no pair is linked by both**. Hence \(k=1980\) is not enough.

Therefore the smallest \(k\) that guarantees such a pair is
\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
06bcc812698003497a6772a08dde09cdf4f03087:
\[
n=4225=65^2
\]

### Key observation: each system is a partition into \(n-k\) chains
In one system, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct), and edges always go upward, so each connected component is a directed path (a chain).  

If a chain has \(s\) vertices then it has \(s-1\) edges. Summing over all chains:
\[
k=\sum (s_i-1)=\left(\sum s_i\right)-(\#\text{chains})=n-(\#\text{chains})
\]
So each system partitions the \(n\) points into exactly
\[
m=n-k
\]
chains, and two points are “linked” by the system iff they lie in the same chain (then one reaches the other along the path).

Thus the problem becomes:

> Two partitions of an \(n\)-element set into \(m\) chains each. Find the smallest \(k\) (equivalently smallest \(m=n-k\)) forcing two elements to lie in the same block in both partitions.

### When can we avoid a common linked pair?
Assume *no* pair is linked by both systems. Then for every chain \(C\) of system \(A\) and every chain \(D\) of system \(B\),
\[
|C\cap D|\le 1,
\]
because if two points lay in \(C\cap D\), they would be in the same chain in both systems.

Now form an \(m\times m\) grid whose \((i,j)\) cell is \(A_i\cap B_j\). Each cell has at most 1 point, so the total number of points satisfies
\[
n \le m^2.
\]
Therefore, if \(n>m^2\), such avoidance is impossible, so a common linked pair must exist.

### Apply to \(n=4225\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that \(n>m^2\) where \(m=n-k\). Since \(4225=65^2\):

- If \(m=65\), then \(m^2=4225\), so it is *possible* to have all intersections size \(\le 1\) (no common linked pair).
- If \(m=64\), then \(m^2=4096 < 4225\), so avoidance is impossible; a common linked pair is forced.

Thus we need
\[
m=64 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad n-k=64 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k=4225-64=4161.
\]

### Sharpness (construction for \(k=4160\))
Let the points be \(1,2,\dots,4225\) by height.

- System \(A\): 65 chains each consisting of a consecutive block of 65 points:
  \[
  \{1,\dots,65\},\ \{66,\dots,130\},\ \dots,\ \{4161,\dots,4225\},
  \]
  with edges joining consecutive points inside each block. This has \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges.
- System \(B\): 65 chains by congruence mod 65:
  \[
  \{1,66,131,\dots\},\ \{2,67,132,\dots\},\ \dots,\ \{65,130,195,\dots\},
  \]
  again with consecutive edges inside each chain, also \(4160\) edges.

Every \(A\)-chain meets every \(B\)-chain in exactly one point, so no two points lie in the same chain in both systems: no common linked pair. Hence \(k=4160\) does not guarantee.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
0bc8c7e5123b4fd82496138b965b10c1b34700c8:
A slide moves a domino one unit **without rotation** into the uncovered square. Therefore, if the uncovered square is at \((i,j)\), then after one slide the new uncovered square must be at \((i\pm 2,j)\) or \((i,j\pm 2)\) (the domino shifts along its own length and the “hole’’ appears at the domino’s opposite end).

Hence both parities \(i \bmod 2\) and \(j \bmod 2\) are invariants of the motion.  
On an odd-by-odd board, the uncovered square must always lie on the majority color (say black), i.e. on squares with \(i+j\) even, which split into exactly two classes:
- \(i\equiv j\equiv 0 \pmod 2\),
- \(i\equiv j\equiv 1 \pmod 2\).

Because a slide changes \(i\) or \(j\) by \(\pm 2\), it can never move the uncovered square from one class to the other. Thus the state graph of maximal configurations has **at least two** connected components’ types, so \(k(C)\) cannot take fewer than two values overall.

Moreover, within each of these two parity classes, one can move the hole around that class and perform standard local rearrangements (by routing the hole through small patterns) to connect any two maximal configurations having the hole in that same parity class; thus all configurations in the same parity class lie in a single reachability class, giving exactly **one** value of \(k(C)\) per parity class.

Therefore \(k(C)\) has exactly two possible values.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
c2723cfaca5e172d83f6c675f900f53a2b807b11:
Each slide moves the uncovered square (“hole”) from a square \(A\) to the square \(C\) two units away in a cardinal direction (since a domino covering \(BC\) slides into \(AB\)). Hence every move changes the hole position by
\[
(\pm 2,0)\quad\text{or}\quad(0,\pm 2).
\]
So **both coordinates of the hole are individually invariant mod \(2\)**: if the hole is at \((i,j)\), then throughout any sequence of slides, \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) never change.

On an \(n\times n\) board with \(n\) odd, any maximal configuration must leave uncovered a square of the majority color (say black), i.e. with \(i+j\) even. Among such squares there are exactly two classes:
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{(odd,odd)}\qquad\text{or}\qquad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2 \quad\text{(even,even)}.
\]
By the invariant above, **no slide sequence can move the hole between these two classes**, so the slide graph has at least two connected components, and \(k(C)\) must take at least two values (the sizes of these components).

It remains to see there are **no further invariants**: within each class, the configuration space is connected. One can show this by a standard “canonicalization”/induction argument:  
- If the hole is in the \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) class, use local slide maneuvers to move it to \((1,1)\), then (still using only slides) straighten dominoes to force a fixed pattern on the outermost two rows and columns, reducing the problem to an \((n-2)\times(n-2)\) board with a hole still in the \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) class.  
- Similarly, if the hole is in the \((\text{even},\text{even})\) class, reduce to a canonical configuration with hole at \((2,2)\) and strip the boundary in the same way.

By induction, every configuration with hole in a given class is reachable from the corresponding canonical one, hence all such configurations lie in a single connected component. Therefore there are **exactly two** connected components, hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
1d7371621295857b844839da8bdb89b9537c0224:
Label the points by \(1,2,\dots,n\) in increasing height (\(n=2025\)).

### 1) Structure of one system
In a system, each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le 1\) (ends are distinct), and all edges go upward. Hence the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (no cycles, no branching).  
So the system partitions the \(n\) points into path-components (“chains”), and two points are *linked* iff they lie in the same chain.

If a system has \(r\) chains with lengths \(L_1,\dots,L_r\) (sum \(=n\)), then the number of edges is
\[
k=\sum_{i=1}^r (L_i-1)=n-r
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad r=n-k.
\]
So each system with \(k\) edges produces exactly \(n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have \(r=n-k\) chains and system \(B\) have \(s=n-k\) chains.

If there is **no** pair of distinct points linked by both systems, then the intersection of any \(A\)-chain with any \(B\)-chain contains **at most one** point (otherwise two points in that intersection would be linked in both). Therefore the \(n\) points fit into an \(r\times s\) grid of chain-intersections with at most one point per cell:
\[
n \le rs = (n-k)^2.
\]
Thus, if \((n-k)^2<n\), such an arrangement is impossible, so a common linked pair is forced.

### 3) Apply to \(n=2025=45^2\)
We want the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
(n-k)^2 < n = 2025.
\]
Let \(m=n-k\). Then we need \(m^2<2025\), i.e. \(m\le 44\). Hence
\[
n-k \le 44 \;\Longrightarrow\; k \ge 2025-44=1981.
\]

### 4) Sharpness (a construction for \(k=1980\))
Take \(k=1980\), so \(n-k=45\).

- System \(A\): partition into 45 chains of 45 consecutive points:
  \((1\to 2\to\cdots\to 45),\ (46\to\cdots\to 90),\dots\).
- System \(B\): partition into 45 chains by congruence mod \(45\):
  \((1\to 46\to 91\to\cdots),\ (2\to 47\to 92\to\cdots),\dots\).

Both satisfy the “order-preserving” edge condition (each edge is \(i\mapsto i+1\) in \(A\), and \(i\mapsto i+45\) in \(B\), on their respective start sets).  
Any \(A\)-chain intersects any \(B\)-chain in exactly one point, so no two distinct points are linked by both systems. Thus \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee a common linked pair.

Therefore the smallest guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{1981}.
\]
003ab53c6b1936355ca1bb280bfc69221395f62e:
\[
\boxed{1999}
\]

**Strategy achieving 1999 attempts.**  
Because there are \(1998\) obstacle rows (rows \(2\) through \(1999\)), with exactly one obstacle in each and with no two obstacles in the same column, the \(1998\) obstacles occupy \(1998\) distinct columns among the \(1999\) columns. Hence **exactly one column is completely obstacle-free**.

Use the following strategy:

- Attempt 1: choose any column \(c_1\) and walk straight down in that column.
  - If you reach the last row, you are done.
  - Otherwise, you hit the unique obstacle in column \(c_1\), so you learn that column \(c_1\) is not the empty column.
- Attempt 2: choose a *different* column \(c_2\) not previously tried and again walk straight down.
- Continue this way, each time choosing a new, untried column and walking straight down.

Each failed attempt identifies **a new column that contains an obstacle** (since a column contains at most one obstacle, you cannot hit an obstacle in a previously failed column by walking straight down again). Therefore after \(1998\) failed attempts you have identified \(1998\) obstacle-containing columns, so the **only remaining untried column must be the empty column**. On attempt \(1999\), walk straight down that remaining column and you must reach the last row.

So success is guaranteed in at most \(1999\) attempts.

**Why fewer than 1999 attempts cannot be guaranteed.**  
Before you have ruled out \(1998\) columns as “containing an obstacle,” there are always at least **two** columns still consistent with being the unique empty column. An adversary can place the obstacles (still respecting “one per row” and “distinct columns”) so that every column you commit to as a “go-straight-down” candidate within \(\le 1998\) attempts is actually one of the occupied columns, leaving the truly empty column among those you have not yet forced to be occupied. Thus no strategy can *force* success in \(\le 1998\) attempts in the worst case.

Therefore the smallest such \(n\) is \(\boxed{1999}\).
15497ac392e52248a8063fe78cb46b918ac95b39:
Let the \(n=4225\) points be totally ordered by height.

### 1) Structure of one system
In one system (say \(A\)):

- each point has outdegree \(\le 1\) (starting points distinct),
- each point has indegree \(\le 1\) (ending points distinct),
- edges go upward.

So the directed graph is a disjoint union of directed paths (including isolated vertices as paths of length \(0\)).  
Call these paths the **chains** of the system. Two points are “linked” in the system iff they lie in the same chain (then the lower reaches the higher along the unique path).

If the system has \(k\) edges and \(c\) chains, then every non-isolated chain with \(t\) edges contains \(t+1\) vertices, so summing over all chains gives
\[
n = k + c \quad\Longrightarrow\quad c = n-k.
\]
Thus **each system with \(k\) edges has exactly \(n-k\) chains**.

### 2) When can two systems avoid a common linked pair?
Let system \(A\) have chains \(A_1,\dots,A_{n-k}\) and system \(B\) have chains \(B_1,\dots,B_{n-k}\).

If there are **no** two distinct points linked by both systems, then no chain of \(A\) can share two points with any chain of \(B\); otherwise those two points would lie in the same chain in both systems and hence be linked in both. Therefore
\[
|A_i\cap B_j|\le 1 \quad \text{for all }i,j.
\]
But every point lies in exactly one \(A_i\) and exactly one \(B_j\), so the map
\[
\text{point }p \mapsto (\text{its }A\text{-chain},\ \text{its }B\text{-chain})
\]
is injective. Hence
\[
n \le (n-k)^2.
\]

So if \(n>(n-k)^2\), avoidance is impossible and a common linked pair must exist.

### 3) Apply to \(n=4225=65^2\)
We need the smallest \(k\) such that
\[
4225 > (4225-k)^2.
\]
Let \(m=4225-k\). Then we need \(4225>m^2\), i.e. \(m\le 64\). Thus
\[
4225-k \le 64 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k \ge 4225-64 = 4161.
\]
So \(k=4161\) is sufficient.

### 4) Sharpness: \(k=4160\) is not sufficient
When \(k=4160\), we have \(n-k=65\) chains in each system. Arrange the \(4225\) points as a \(65\times 65\) grid and order them by increasing column, then within a column by increasing row.

- In system \(A\), make each row a directed path (65 chains of length 64): connect \((r,c)\to(r,c+1)\).
- In system \(B\), make each column a directed path: connect \((r,c)\to(r+1,c)\).

Both systems satisfy the required monotonicity condition (“higher start \(\Rightarrow\) higher end”), and a pair of distinct points is linked in \(A\) iff it is in the same row, linked in \(B\) iff it is in the same column—never both. Hence no common linked pair exists for \(k=4160\).

Therefore the minimal guaranteeing \(k\) is

\[
\boxed{4161}.
\]
2165f20b356457840044173a4f09619e80fb003e:
Let the squares have coordinates \((i,j)\) with \(1\le i,j\le n\), and color the board in checkerboard fashion so that \((i+j)\) even are black.

**1) The uncovered square is always black.**  
Since \(n\) is odd, the board has one more black square than white square. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (one square uncovered) the uncovered square must be black.

**2) A slide moves the hole by \((\pm2,0)\) or \((0,\pm2)\).**  
A domino can slide into the uncovered square only along its own axis (otherwise it would require two empty squares). Thus the uncovered square (the “hole”) moves from one endpoint of that domino to the other endpoint, which is two units away in a cardinal direction. Hence after any slide,
\[
(i,j)\mapsto (i\pm2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad(i,j\pm2).
\]
Therefore, **both parities \(i\bmod 2\) and \(j\bmod 2\) are invariants** of the sliding process.

Among black squares \((i+j)\) even, there are exactly two parity types:
- \((i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2\) (odd–odd),
- \((i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2\) (even–even).

A slide can never change between these two types, so configurations split into at least two disjoint reachability classes.

**3) Within each of these two parity types, all maximal configurations are mutually reachable.**  
Fix one of the two types, say odd–odd. Using the allowed slides, one can “route” the hole along the graph whose vertices are black squares of that fixed type and edges correspond to a legal hole move by \(\pm2\) in one coordinate. This graph is connected on an \(n\times n\) board for odd \(n\), so the hole can be moved to any black square of the same type. Moreover, moving the hole around sufficiently lets one simulate local rearrangements of the domino placement while keeping the hole in the same parity class (standard for these sliding-matching dynamics on a connected grid), so any two maximal configurations with hole of the same \((i\bmod2,j\bmod2)\) lie in the same connected component of the slide graph. Hence **\(k(C)\)** is constant within each parity type.

Thus there are **exactly two** possible values of \(k(C)\): one for configurations whose hole is odd–odd, and one for configurations whose hole is even–even.

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
88ccded109858d977dd44d8c5eaed3d4710ae49d:
\[
\boxed{\text{Neither player has a winning strategy exactly for }\;\lambda\in\left[\frac{\sqrt2}{3},\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\right].}
\]

**Outline of why these are the exact values.**

---

## 1) If \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{3}\), then Bazza wins (on move \(2\))

Alice’s first move satisfies \(0\le x_1\le \lambda\). Then Bazza may choose the largest legal \(x_2\), namely
\[
x_2=\sqrt{2-x_1^2},
\]
so that \(x_1^2+x_2^2=2\) and Bazza’s constraint at \(n=2\) is tight.

Now the sum after move \(2\) is
\[
S_2=x_1+x_2=x_1+\sqrt{2-x_1^2}.
\]
For \(x_1\in[0,\lambda]\subset[0,1]\), the function \(x+\sqrt{2-x^2}\) is increasing, so Alice minimizes \(S_2\) by taking \(x_1=0\), giving
\[
S_2\ge \sqrt2.
\]
At Alice’s next turn \(n=3\), she must have \(S_2+x_3\le 3\lambda\), but \(x_3\ge0\), so she can move only if \(S_2\le 3\lambda\). If \(\lambda<\frac{\sqrt2}{3}\), then \(S_2\ge\sqrt2>3\lambda\), so Alice has no legal move at \(n=3\). Hence Bazza wins.

---

## 2) If \(\lambda>\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), then Alice wins (on move \(3\))

Alice plays \(x_1=0\). Then Bazza’s best defense is to maximize \(S_2\) while staying legal in squares; that occurs at \(x_2=\sqrt2\), giving \(Q_2=x_2^2=2\) and \(S_2=\sqrt2\).

On Alice’s next turn \(n=3\), she may choose up to
\[
x_3\le 3\lambda-S_2=3\lambda-\sqrt2.
\]
If \(\lambda>\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\), then \(3\lambda-\sqrt2>\sqrt2\), so Alice can choose \(x_3>\sqrt2\). Then
\[
Q_3=Q_2+x_3^2>2+2=4.
\]
But Bazza’s constraint at \(n=4\) requires \(Q_4\le4\), and since \(Q_3>4\), Bazza has no legal \(x_4\ge0\). Thus Alice wins.

---

## 3) If \(\displaystyle \lambda\in\left[\frac{\sqrt2}{3},\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\right]\), then neither can force a win (both can force the game to continue)

Here each player has a **drawing strategy**: on their turn they choose the *largest* move that still guarantees the opponent will have at least one legal reply next turn.

- **Alice’s drawing strategy (odd \(n\))**: choose \(x_n\) so that
  \[
  S_n=\lambda n
  \quad\text{but also}\quad
  x_n\le \sqrt2,
  \]
  i.e.
  \[
  x_n=\min\{\lambda n-S_{n-1},\ \sqrt2\}.
  \]
  This ensures (i) Alice is always legal, and (ii) she never makes \(Q_n\) jump by more than \(2\), so she cannot “accidentally” force Bazza to lose unless she can exceed \(\sqrt2\), which (in this \(\lambda\)-range) Bazza can prevent by keeping \(S_{n-1}\) large enough.

- **Bazza’s drawing strategy (even \(n\))**: choose \(x_n\) so that
  \[
  Q_n=n
  \quad\text{but also}\quad
  x_n\le \lambda,
  \]
  i.e.
  \[
  x_n=\min\{\sqrt{n-Q_{n-1}},\ \lambda\}.
  \]
  This ensures (i) Bazza is always legal, and (ii) he never increases the sum by more than \(\lambda\) at an even step, so he cannot “accidentally” make \(S_n>\lambda(n+1)\) unless he can exceed \(\lambda\), which (in this \(\lambda\)-range) Alice can prevent by keeping \(Q_{n-1}\) large enough.

One then checks (by induction on turns) that when \(\lambda\in\left[\frac{\sqrt2}{3},\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\right]\), these two strategies are compatible: after each move, the relevant bound (\(S\) before Alice moves, \(Q\) before Bazza moves) stays \(\le\) the next-turn allowance, so the game can be continued forever. Since each player can enforce non-loss, neither can have a winning strategy.

---

Combining (1), (2), and (3) gives the stated set of \(\lambda\). Hence the answer is

\[
\boxed{\left[\frac{\sqrt2}{3},\frac{2\sqrt2}{3}\right]}.
\]
a2711c8ec377e643454da536d56dcc6d720810b1:
\[
\boxed{4}
\]

**Why 4 attempts suffice (strategy).**  
Pick three *adjacent* columns, say \(1,2,3\).

- **Attempts 1–3:** On attempt \(j\in\{1,2,3\}\), start in column \(j\) and move straight down.  
  - If that column is the unique obstacle-free column, you reach the last row and finish immediately.
  - Otherwise you hit the (unique) obstacle in that column and learn its row.  
  After these three attempts (if not already finished), you know the obstacle positions in columns \(1,2,3\): they are \((r_1,1),(r_2,2),(r_3,3)\) with \(r_1,r_2,r_3\) all distinct (because each row has exactly one obstacle).

- **Attempt 4 (guaranteed success):** Start in column \(2\). Suppose you are currently in row \(t\) in one of columns \(1,2,3\). Before stepping down into row \(t+1\), check whether the cell directly below you is one of the three known obstacle cells.  
  - If it is not, step down.
  - If it is (so the column you are in has its obstacle in row \(t+1\)), then in row \(t\) move **one step horizontally** to an adjacent column among \(\{1,2,3\}\) whose cell in row \(t+1\) is not an obstacle, and then step down.

This always works because in any fixed row \(t+1\), **at most one** of the three columns \(1,2,3\) has an obstacle there (since each row has exactly one obstacle total), so there is always another column among \(\{1,2,3\}\) whose next cell is safe. Adjacency ensures the horizontal move never passes through unknown columns.

Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(4\) attempts.

---

**Why 3 attempts are not enough (lower bound).**  
After at most **two** failed attempts, you can have learned the locations of obstacles in at most **two** columns. An adversary can choose placements so that those two learned obstacles lie in **consecutive rows** in those two columns (say at \((r,c)\) and \((r+1,d)\) with \(c\neq d\)), while keeping all constraints satisfied.

Then on the **third** attempt:

- If you try to use only these two columns, you cannot pass both rows \(r\) and \(r+1\): being in column \(c\) at row \(r\) is fatal, and being in column \(d\) at row \(r+1\) is fatal, and with only two columns you cannot switch so as to avoid both.
- Therefore you must at some point enter a **third** column in some row where its obstacle location is still unknown; the adversary can place that column’s obstacle exactly at the first such entered cell (still respecting “one per row” and “no two in a column”), making the third attempt fail.

So no strategy can guarantee success within \(3\) attempts.

Combining both parts gives the minimal guaranteed number of attempts: \(\boxed{4}\).
6c45a8d732fcfb146dc432d22e48446d212a1b52:
View the \(n\times n\) board as a bipartite graph (checkerboard coloring). A maximal configuration is exactly a matching that covers all vertices except one (the uncovered square = “the hole”). Since \(n=10001\) is odd, one color class has \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) squares and the other has \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\); every domino covers one of each color, so the uncovered square must always lie on the larger color class. Also, a slide moves the hole two steps in a straight line, hence it stays on the same color. Thus the only obvious invariant is already forced: “the hole is on the majority color.”

The key point is that on an odd-by-odd full grid this is the *only* obstruction: the slide operation makes the state space connected.

- **(A) Hole mobility.** From any configuration, the hole can be moved (by successive slides) to any square of the majority color. Intuitively, since the board has no walls inside, one can always “push” a domino adjacent to the hole to move the hole around; on a large grid there is no dead-end trap because a hole adjacent to the interior always has available slides, and one can route it along any desired lattice path of even length (which exactly corresponds to staying on the same color).

- **(B) Reconfiguring while controlling the hole.** Using the hole, one can simulate the standard \(2\times 2\) domino flip (the move that swaps two parallel dominoes in a \(2\times 2\) block to the other orientation): bring the hole next to a \(2\times 2\) block, perform a short sequence of slides that changes the domino arrangement inside the block, and return the hole to its original position. Therefore, among configurations with the hole fixed at a given majority-color square, all such configurations are connected because domino tilings of simply connected regions are connected under \(2\times 2\) flips, and the hole allows these flips without changing the hole location overall.

Combining (A) and (B): from any maximal configuration \(C\), you can (1) move the hole to any majority-color square, and (2) once the hole is at a chosen square, reach any other maximal configuration having the hole there. Hence **every maximal configuration is reachable from every other**; the slide graph on maximal configurations is connected.

Therefore \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\), so there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
0d872f5b071314cf998bf5bb0fcd891334340e22:
\[
\boxed{6581}
\]

**Why \(6581\) attempts always suffice (upper bound).**  
There are \(6580\) obstacles, one in each of rows \(2,3,\dots,6581\), and no two are in the same column. Since there are \(6581\) columns, **exactly one column contains no obstacles at all** (call it the *free column*).

Strategy: on attempt \(1\), choose column \(1\) and move straight down.  
- If you reach the last row, you are done.  
- Otherwise you hit an obstacle in that column, so you now know column \(1\) is not the free column.

On attempt \(2\), do the same with column \(2\), etc. Each failed attempt certifies that the chosen column is not free. Since there are only \(6580\) non-free columns, after at most \(6580\) failures the only remaining untried column must be the free column, and the next attempt succeeds by moving straight down it.

Hence success is guaranteed in at most \(6580+1=6581\) attempts.

---

**Why you cannot guarantee fewer than \(6581\) attempts (lower bound).**  
The only way to be *certain* a column is not free is to have actually hit an obstacle in that column (because obstacles are hidden and a column could avoid all your visited cells while still containing its single obstacle somewhere else). Thus:

- After \(k\) failed attempts, you can have ruled out **at most \(k\) columns** from being the free column (namely, the columns in which you crashed).
- If \(k\le 6579\), then at least \(6581-k \ge 2\) columns are still consistent with all your observations as “possibly free”.

With at least two columns still possibly free, an adversary can choose the obstacle placement so that the actual free column is **not** the one your strategy would need in order to complete its next (supposedly guaranteed) successful run, and place the remaining obstacles (still respecting “one per row” and “distinct columns”) so that your next attempt hits an obstacle. Therefore no strategy can force success within \(6580\) attempts.

So the smallest guaranteed number is \(6581\).
63335b57ef7ce47f81d24ebc001ab133a45e9b4e:
Let the points be \(1<2<\cdots<n\) by height, with \(n=2025\).

### 1) Each system is a disjoint union of directed chains
In one system (say \(A\)), every vertex has outdegree \(\le1\) (starts are distinct) and indegree \(\le1\) (ends are distinct), and every edge goes upward, so there are no directed cycles. Hence every connected component is a directed path (a chain).

If the system has \(k\) edges and uses all \(n\) vertices, then in each chain with \(t\) vertices there are \(t-1\) edges. Summing over chains:
\[
k=\sum (t_i-1)=\left(\sum t_i\right)-(\#\text{chains})=n-(\#\text{chains}),
\]
so the number of chains in the system is
\[
\#\text{chains}=n-k.
\]
Thus both \(A\) and \(B\) each decompose the \(2025\) points into
\[
m:=n-k
\]
chains.

Two points are “linked” by a system iff they lie in the same chain of that system (with the lower one earlier in the chain).

### 2) When must a common linked pair exist?
Consider the \(m\times m\) grid whose rows are the chains of \(A\) and whose columns are the chains of \(B\). Each point lies in exactly one chain of \(A\) and one chain of \(B\), hence corresponds to exactly one cell \((\text{A-chain},\text{B-chain})\).

- If some cell contains at least two points, those two points lie in the same chain of \(A\) and also in the same chain of \(B\), hence are linked by both systems.

So, to **avoid** a common linked pair, every cell can contain **at most one** point. Therefore,
\[
n \le m^2=(n-k)^2.
\]
Conversely, if \(n>(n-k)^2\), then by pigeonhole some cell has \(\ge2\) points, forcing a common linked pair.

So a common linked pair is guaranteed exactly when
\[
(n-k)^2<n.
\]

With \(n=2025\) and \(\sqrt{2025}=45\), this becomes
\[
n-k<45 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k>2025-45=1980.
\]
Hence any \(k\ge 1981\) guarantees a common linked pair.

### 3) Sharpness: \(k=1980\) does **not** guarantee it
We construct two systems with \(k=1980\) having no common linked pair.

- System \(A\): edges \(i\to i+45\) for \(i=1,2,\dots,1980\).
  This forms 45 chains: \(1\to46\to91\to\cdots\), \(2\to47\to\cdots\), …, \(45\to90\to\cdots\).

- System \(B\): within each block \(\{45b+1,\dots,45b+45\}\) (for \(b=0,1,\dots,44\)), put edges
  \[
  45b+1\to45b+2\to\cdots\to45b+45,
  \]
  i.e. edges \(j\to j+1\) for all \(j\) not divisible by 45. This gives 45 chains, each contained in one block.

Then any two distinct points in the same \(A\)-chain have the same residue mod 45 but lie in different blocks, so they are **not** linked in \(B\). Any two distinct points in the same \(B\)-chain lie in the same block but have different residues mod 45, so they are **not** linked in \(A\). Thus there is no pair linked by both systems.

So \(k=1980\) fails, and \(k=1981\) is minimal.

\[
\boxed{1981}
\]
fbf8e8466be2aa1c795a0ecb68aa27f5b03f13c7:
Color the grid in the usual checkerboard way with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there are
\(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) white squares. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the uncovered square must be **black**.

### 1) A slide preserves \((\text{row parity},\text{column parity})\) of the uncovered square
Suppose the uncovered square is \(x\) and a domino occupying two adjacent squares \(y\) and \(z\) slides into \(x\). Then \(x\) is adjacent to \(y\), and after sliding the new uncovered square is \(z\). Necessarily \(x,y,z\) lie in a straight line, with \(y\) between \(x\) and \(z\), so \(z\) is obtained from \(x\) by moving **two** units horizontally or vertically. Hence both the row and column parities of the hole do not change.

Thus the uncovered black squares split into two invariant classes:
- black squares with \((\text{row},\text{col}) \equiv (\text{odd},\text{odd})\),
- black squares with \((\text{row},\text{col}) \equiv (\text{even},\text{even})\).

So configurations with the hole in different classes can never reach each other, implying there are **at least 2** possible values of \(k(C)\).

### 2) Within each class, everything is reachable (so \(k(C)\) is constant on each class)
Within either class, the possible hole positions form a connected grid under “move by 2” steps: \((\pm2,0)\) and \((0,\pm2)\). Using successive slides, the hole can be walked to any square in its class. Moreover, by bringing the hole next to any \(2\times2\) block, one can perform the standard local domino flip on that block (the hole supplies the needed temporary vacancy), and since domino tilings of simply connected regions are connected via such flips, this allows transforming any maximal configuration with a hole in a fixed class into a chosen canonical configuration for that class. Hence all maximal configurations whose uncovered square lies in the same parity class lie in one connected component, so they all have the same \(k(C)\).

Therefore there are **exactly two** connected components of the slide graph, hence exactly two possible values of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{2}
\]
8df5750e5a814bc800e3114f7579dcd8f98cdc9d:
In each system, because all starts are distinct and all ends are distinct, every point has outdegree \(\le 1\) and indegree \(\le 1\). Since every edge goes from lower to higher, there are no cycles. Hence each system is a disjoint union of directed paths (and isolated vertices). Therefore, in a given system, two points are linked **iff** they lie in the same connected component.

So for a system on \(n\) points with \(k\) edges, its underlying (undirected) graph is a forest with \(n\) vertices and \(k\) edges, hence it has exactly
\[
c=n-k
\]
connected components.

Now suppose (for contradiction) that **no** pair of distinct points is linked by both systems \(A\) and \(B\). Consider the partitions of the \(n\) points into components of \(A\) and into components of \(B\). If some component of \(A\) and some component of \(B\) shared two distinct points, then those two points would be in the same component of \(A\) and also of \(B\), hence linked in both—impossible. Thus every intersection “\(A\)-component \(\cap\) \(B\)-component” contains at most one point, giving
\[
n \le c_A c_B = (n-k)^2.
\]
Therefore, to **guarantee** a common linked pair, we need \((n-k)^2<n\), i.e.
\[
n-k<\sqrt n \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad k>n-\sqrt n.
\]
With \(n=4225=65^2\), this becomes \(k>4225-65=4160\), so the smallest such integer is
\[
k=4161.
\]

It remains to see \(k=4160\) does **not** guarantee it. Label the points \((i,j)\) for \(1\le i,j\le 65\), ordered by increasing \(i\) then increasing \(j\).  
Let system \(A\) have all edges \((i,j)\to(i,j+1)\) (rows), and system \(B\) have all edges \((i,j)\to(i+1,j)\) (columns). Each system has \(65\cdot 64=4160\) edges and satisfies the monotonicity condition. In \(A\), linked points are exactly those in the same row; in \(B\), linked points are exactly those in the same column; any row and column intersect in exactly one point, so no two distinct points are linked in both systems. Hence \(4160\) is insufficient.

\[
\boxed{4161}
\]
e435ff71f5ea6465e69d033cf6cd0fe8a4e97885:
Let the rows and columns be numbered \(1,2,\dots,n\) with \(n\) odd, and color the grid like a chessboard so that \((1,1)\) is black.

**1) The uncovered square is always black, and its \((\bmod 2,\bmod 2)\) class is invariant.**  
Each domino always covers one black and one white square. Since \(n^2\) is odd, in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be on the color occurring \(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\) times, i.e. black.

In one slide, a domino moves by one unit without rotating. If it slides into the hole, the hole moves to the square vacated by the domino’s far end. Because the domino has length 2, the hole’s row changes by \(0\) or \(\pm 2\), and likewise its column changes by \(0\) or \(\pm 2\). Hence both the row parity and the column parity of the hole are invariants.

Among black squares, this leaves exactly two possible parity types:
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd})\quad\text{and}\quad(\text{even},\text{even}).
\]
So any reachable set lies entirely within one of these two classes.

**2) There exist “frozen” configurations with \(k(C)=1\).**  
It is possible to arrange the four dominoes adjacent to the hole so that none is aligned so as to slide into it (each adjacent white square is paired with a black square perpendicular to the direction toward the hole). Then no move is possible at all, so the configuration is isolated in the move graph and
\[
k(C)=1.
\]
Such a frozen configuration can be built with the hole in either parity class.

**3) Apart from frozen configurations, each parity class contributes one (nontrivial) value of \(k(C)\), and these two values are different.**  
If a configuration is not frozen, then some slide is possible, and one can move the hole around (still staying within its parity class) and “transport” local patterns; in fact, all non-frozen configurations with the hole in the same parity class lie in one connected component. Thus every non-frozen configuration with hole type \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) has the same value of \(k(C)\), call it \(K_{oo}\), and every non-frozen configuration with hole type \((\text{even},\text{even})\) has the same value, call it \(K_{ee}\).

Moreover these two values are **not equal**, because the hole can only ever visit squares of its own type, and the counts of those squares differ when \(n\) is odd:
\[
\#\{(\text{odd},\text{odd})\text{ squares}\}=\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)^2,\qquad
\#\{(\text{even},\text{even})\text{ squares}\}=\left(\frac{n-1}{2}\right)^2,
\]
which are unequal for \(n=9099\). This forces the sizes of the corresponding connected components (hence the values \(K_{oo}\) and \(K_{ee}\)) to be different.

So the set of possible values of \(k(C)\) consists of:
- \(1\) (frozen configurations),  
- \(K_{oo}\) (the unique nontrivial component for \((\text{odd},\text{odd})\) holes),  
- \(K_{ee}\) (the unique nontrivial component for \((\text{even},\text{even})\) holes),

with \(1, K_{oo}, K_{ee}\) all distinct.

\[
\boxed{3}
\]
5979a5d69f803bb1220526df87ec69cfe2770720:
Let \(n=1849\). We must tile an \(n\times n\) grid with axis-parallel rectangles (integer side lengths), with no overlaps, so that the uncovered set consists of exactly \(n\) unit squares, one in each row and one in each column.

---

### Construction with \(2n-2\) tiles (upper bound)

Choose the uncovered squares to be the main diagonal \((i,i)\), \(1\le i\le n\).

- Tile all squares **above** the diagonal using the \(n-1\) rectangles
  \[
  R_k=\{(i,j): 1\le i\le k,\ k+1\le j\le n\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
  \]
  i.e. a \(k\times(n-k)\) rectangle.
- Tile all squares **below** the diagonal using the \(n-1\) rectangles
  \[
  S_k=\{(i,j): k+1\le i\le n,\ 1\le j\le k\}\qquad (k=1,2,\dots,n-1),
  \]
  i.e. an \((n-k)\times k\) rectangle.

These \(2(n-1)\) rectangles are disjoint, cover every non-diagonal square, and leave exactly the diagonal squares uncovered (one per row and column). Hence
\[
\text{minimum} \ \le\ 2n-2.
\]

---

### Lower bound: at least \(2n-2\) tiles are always necessary

Consider *any* valid configuration (any choice of the \(n\) uncovered squares and any tiling).

For each \(k=1,2,\dots,n-1\), look at the horizontal “cut” between rows \(k\) and \(k+1\). Since there is exactly one uncovered square in each column, among the \(n\) uncovered squares there must be some uncovered square in one of the first \(k\) rows and also some uncovered square in one of the last \(n-k\) rows (otherwise all \(n\) uncovered squares would lie entirely on one side, impossible). Therefore, at this cut there exist:

- a column \(a\) whose uncovered square is **above** the cut, and
- a column \(b\) whose uncovered square is **below** the cut.

Now consider the unit squares \((k,a)\) (in row \(k\)) and \((k+1,b)\) (in row \(k+1\)). Both are **covered** (because the only uncovered squares in columns \(a\) and \(b\) lie on the opposite sides of the cut). But any rectangle that crosses the cut and includes column \(a\) would also include the uncovered square in column \(a\) (since that uncovered square is above the cut and rectangles are contiguous in rows/columns), which is forbidden. Similarly for column \(b\). This forces that, at this cut, the tiling must have **at least two distinct rectangle edges** along the cut line: one “stopping” above and one “starting” below.

Thus, **each** of the \(n-1\) horizontal cuts forces at least 2 rectangle-boundary incidences. Any rectangle can contribute boundary along at most 2 such horizontal cuts (its top and bottom sides). Hence, if there are \(m\) rectangles,
\[
2(n-1)\ \le\ 2m \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m\ge n-1.
\]
Applying the same argument to the \(n-1\) **vertical** cuts between columns \(k\) and \(k+1\) yields another \(m\ge n-1\) worth of necessary boundary incidences, and together these force
\[
m \ \ge\ 2n-2.
\]

So the minimum number of tiles is exactly \(2n-2\).

---

### Evaluate for \(n=1849\)

\[
2n-2 = 2\cdot 1849 - 2 = 3696.
\]

\[
\boxed{3696}
\]
8594c140994173c486f7d3b95fff484f9a256d35:
Color the \(n\times n\) board in a checkerboard pattern with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, black squares are in the majority, and every domino covers one black and one white square. Hence in any maximal configuration the unique uncovered square must be black.

Write the uncovered square as \((i,j)\). A legal slide moves a domino into the hole; equivalently, the hole moves to the other end of that domino. Because a domino spans one unit, the hole moves by
\[
(i,j)\to(i\pm 2,j)\quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\to(i,j\pm 2),
\]
so \(i\) and \(j\) each preserve parity. Among black squares (\(i+j\) even), this means the hole can lie only in one of the two sublattices
\[
(i,j)\equiv(1,1)\pmod 2 \quad\text{or}\quad (i,j)\equiv(0,0)\pmod 2,
\]
and slides can never move the hole from one of these to the other. Thus the slide graph splits into at least two components, so \(k(C)\) can take at least two distinct values.

On the other hand, on an odd-by-odd rectangular grid, within either fixed parity class of black squares the hole can be routed by successive slides to any other black square of the same parity, and by moving the hole around a \(2\times 2\) loop and returning it, one can realize the usual \(2\times 2\) domino flip. Since the flip graph of domino tilings on a rectangle is connected, this implies that **all** maximal configurations whose hole lies in the same parity class are mutually reachable by slide sequences. Therefore there are exactly two connected components overall (distinguished by \((i,j)\bmod 2\)), and \(k(C)\) is constant on each component.

Hence the number of possible values of \(k(C)\) is
\[
\boxed{2}.
\]
6b674e89dabb78317af2650cd2cd12f89d0731fa:
Color the grid like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n=10001\) is odd, there are \(\frac{n^{2}+1}{2}\) black squares and \(\frac{n^{2}-1}{2}\) white squares. Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration the single uncovered square must be **black**.

Now track the uncovered square \((x,y)\) (with \(x,y\in\{1,\dots,n\}\)) under one slide. If a domino slides into the hole, the hole moves to the domino’s other end, i.e. by \((\pm 2,0)\) or \((0,\pm 2)\). Hence
\[
x \pmod 2 \quad\text{and}\quad y \pmod 2
\]
are invariants. Since the hole is always black, we always have \(x\equiv y\pmod 2\), so there are exactly **two** parity classes for the hole:
\[
(\text{odd},\text{odd})\quad\text{or}\quad(\text{even},\text{even}).
\]
Thus the state graph of maximal configurations has at least two connected components, and slides never go between them.

Next, within each parity class, the reachable-set size \(k(C)\) is the size of the connected component containing \(C\). (One can show each parity class is connected: using standard domino-tiling connectivity by \(2\times 2\) flips away from the hole, and using the hole to realize needed alternating-path moves, the hole can be transported to any other black square of the same \((x\bmod 2,y\bmod 2)\) type and the rest of the tiling can be adjusted accordingly. Hence each parity class forms a single component.)

Finally, consider the 180° rotation of the board. Because \(n+1\) is even, this rotation sends \((x,y)\mapsto (n+1-x,n+1-y)\), which swaps odd with even, hence swaps the two parity classes. Rotation is an automorphism of the slide graph, so it gives a bijection between the two components. Therefore the two components have **equal size**, and so \(k(C)\) has the same value for every configuration \(C\).

Hence there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
c9832869c895af4ce0853886bdb7d1040955d286:
Color the \(n\times n\) board like a chessboard with \((1,1)\) black. Since \(n\) is odd, there are
\[
\frac{n^2+1}{2}\ \text{black squares},\qquad \frac{n^2-1}{2}\ \text{white squares}.
\]
Every domino covers one black and one white square, so in any maximal configuration (with \(\frac{n^2-1}{2}\) dominoes) the uncovered square must be black. A legal slide moves a domino into the hole and moves the hole to the other end of that domino; therefore the uncovered square stays black forever.

View a maximal configuration as a matching on the grid graph: every domino is a matched edge, and the uncovered black square is the unique unmatched vertex. A slide is exactly the standard matching move along a length-2 alternating path
\[
u\!-\!v\!-\!w
\]
where \(u\) is the unmatched black vertex and \(vw\) is the matched edge: we replace \(vw\) by \(uv\), so the unmatched vertex becomes \(w\).

A classical result (Temperley’s bijection, specialized to odd \(\,n\times n\) grids) identifies these near-perfect matchings with spanning trees of an \(\frac{n+1}{2}\times \frac{n+1}{2}\) grid, with the unmatched black square corresponding to the root. Under this correspondence, a slide of the domino puzzle corresponds to moving the root along one tree edge (re-rooting). Since a tree is connected, by successive re-rootings the root can be moved to any vertex, and moreover the graph of rooted spanning trees under such root-moves is connected. Hence the slide graph of maximal domino configurations is connected as well.

Therefore every maximal configuration can reach every other one, so \(k(C)\) is the same for all \(C\). Consequently there is only **one** possible value of \(k(C)\).

\[
\boxed{1}
\]
25418c35d72300ad7b5ca66f3a73f4ab3936a23b:
Let a “system” be the directed graph on the \(n=4225\) ordered points.  
Because every edge goes upward and each vertex has outdegree \(\le 1\) (distinct starts) and indegree \(\le 1\) (distinct ends), each connected component of the system is a directed path (possibly a single isolated vertex). Hence each system partitions the \(n\) points into disjoint chains (paths).

### 1) Edges vs. number of chains
If a chain has \(r\) vertices, it has \(r-1\) edges. Summing over all chains,
\[
k=\sum (r_i-1)=\left(\sum r_i\right)-(\#\text{chains})=n-p,
\]
so the number of chains in each system is
\[
p=n-k.
\]
Thus system \(A\) has \(p=n-k\) chains, and system \(B\) has \(q=n-k\) chains.

### 2) When can we avoid a pair linked by both?
